EDCS-00380551.translation: Belonging to the century of Quintus Cornelius Verecundus.
EDCS-00380551.summary: This inscription likely marks a boundary or property belonging to the century of Quintus Cornelius Verecundus, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380552.text_edition: \n Imperatore Traiano Hadriano Caesare Augusto II Gnaeo Fusco Salinatore consulibus pridie Idus Martias cum ventum esset in rem praesentem silvam Verlucionium arepennia decem quinque plus minus quod est in civitate Cantiacorum pago DIBVSSV RABIAS adfinibus heredibus et heredibus Caesenni Vitalis et via vicinale quod se emisse diceret Lucius Iulius Bellicus de Tito Valerio Silvino |(denariis) quadraginta sicut emptione continetur Lucius Bellicus testatus est se \n
EDCS-00380552.translation: Under the consulship of Emperor Trajan Hadrianus Caesar Augustus, twice, and Gnaeus Fuscus Salinator, on the day before the Ides of March, when we came to the present location, fifteen or so acres of the wood called Verlucionium, which is in the territory of the Cantiaci, in the region of Dibussurrabias, to the neighbours, heirs, and heirs of Caesennius Vitalis, and the public road, as Lucius Julius Bellicus stated he bought it from Titus Valerius Silvinus for forty denarii, as is contained in the deed of sale, Lucius Bellicus testified that he...
EDCS-00380552.summary: This inscription is a land sale document from the reign of Hadrian (consular dating to AD 118), detailing the purchase of fifteen acres of woodland in the territory of the Cantiaci (modern Kent, England).

EDCS-00380558.translation: May you fare well, mistress Iuliana.
EDCS-00380558.summary: This is a personal dedication or good-wish, possibly for a woman named Iuliana, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380559.summary: This is a name inscription, likely for Aurelius Ursicinus, probably Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380560.summary: This is a possessive inscription, likely indicating ownership by Aurelius Ursicinus, probably Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380561.summary: This is a possessive inscription, likely indicating ownership by Aurelius Ursicinus, probably Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380565.summary: This is a wish for a 'foreigner' (peregrinus), possibly inscribed on a tomb or monument, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380567.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription possibly related to foreigners ('peregrini'), dating likely to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380568.translation: Whoever they are, may they live thus.
EDCS-00380568.summary: This is a short, perhaps proverbial or dedicatory phrase, possibly wishing life to a group, likely Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380570.summary: This is a statement of identity, likely referring to a 'Silvicola' (forest-dweller or a spirit of the woods), probably Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-00380571.summary: This is a short, possibly funerary, inscription wishing a long life to someone named Silvicola.

EDCS-00380583.text_edition: \n SERADVASORISDVAS si servus si anciila si libertus liberta si mulier si baro popiam ferream EAENEC furtum fecerit domino Neptuno corulo parentatur\n
EDCS-00380583.translation: Whether male slave, female slave, freedman, freedwoman, woman, or man, if they steal the iron door from their master Neptune Corulo, let them be punished.
EDCS-00380583.summary: This inscription records a punitive curse or law for theft, invoking Neptune Corulo and specifying penalties for various social classes.

EDCS-00380587.summary: A very brief inscription stating that Cravirius was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-00380602.summary: A short personal greeting from Icotasgus to Amidda, likely from a private context.

EDCS-00380614.translation: Paterclos made this for Martini, the decurion.
EDCS-00380614.summary: This inscription records that Paterclos made something for Martini, who held the rank of decurion.

EDCS-00380616.text_edition: \n Nonis Martiis missi ad hospitium cum Marco medico faciendum structores numero XXX ad lapidem flammandum numero XVIIII ad lutum viminibus castrorum facien[dum ------\n
EDCS-00380616.translation: On the Nones of March, 30 builders were sent to the lodging to make it ready, 19 to prepare the lime for firing, and [unknown number] to make mud with wickerwork from the camp...
EDCS-00380616.summary: This inscription details logistical arrangements and labor assignments for construction or maintenance work, likely military, on a specific date.

EDCS-00380617.text_edition: \n Felicioni |(centurioni) mutuo XII Kalendas Iunias condimentorum |(sextarii) semissem |(denarii) |(quadrantem) halicae |(sextarii) semissem |(denarii) |(quadrantem) ova numero IIX |(denarii) |(quadrantem) XII Kalendas Iulias \n
EDCS-00380617.translation: To Felicionius the centurion, on the 11th day before the Kalends of June: half a sextarius of condiments for a quarter denarius; half a sextarius of halica for a quarter denarius; 8 eggs for a quarter denarius. On the 11th day before the Kalends of July.
EDCS-00380617.summary: This inscription records a list of provisions purchased or accounted for on specific dates, with quantities, prices, and recipients.

EDCS-00380618.text_edition: \n Eo magis me castigavit dum dicerem mercem nihil valere vel effunderem pro homine probo tuam maiestatem imploro ne patiaris me innocentem virgis castigatum esse et domine prout praefecto non potui queri qua valetudini detinebatur questus sum beneficiario Frustra et centurionibus ceteris numeri eius proinde tuam misericordiam imploro ne patiaris me hominem transmarinarum et innocentem de cuius fide inquiras virgis cruentatum esse ac si aliquid sceleris comississem\n
EDCS-00380618.translation: For this reason, he punished me more severely while I said the merchandise was worthless, or spilled it. I implore your majesty, for the sake of an honest man, do not allow me to be punished with rods while innocent. And, lord, as I could not complain to the prefect because he was detained by illness, I complained to the official and other centurions of his cohort in vain. Therefore, I implore your mercy, do not allow me, an innocent man from overseas, whose loyalty you may inquire into, to be covered in bloody welts as if I had committed some crime.
EDCS-00380618.summary: This is a plea for justice or clemency from an individual appealing to a higher authority against a subordinate official who allegedly punished them unjustly.

EDCS-00380623.text_edition: \n Maior Maritimo suo salutem scire te volui epistulas mihi missas esse ab patri sic meo in quibus scribit mihi ut ei notum faciam quid gessero defussa quod spica gessisti negotium cum Caesarianis fac ut certum mihi rescribas ut ego patri meo sic resscribere sic possim si quid inter numeraveris ego tibi sine mora Britem expellam pro ssumma quod efficiatur ego cum haec tibi scriberem lectum calfaciebam opto sis felicissimus salutat te frater vale si puerum missurus es mittes chirografum cum eo quo securior sim Vindolande Cocceiio Maritimo a Maiore\n
EDCS-00380623.translation: Maior sends greetings to his Maritimus. I want you to know that letters have been sent to me from my father, in which he writes to me to inform him what I have done with the goods you carried. Make sure you send me a prompt reply so that I can write back to my father. If you pay anything, I will expel Brites for you without delay, for the highest good. As I write this to you, I was warming myself by the fire. I hope you are most fortunate. Your brother greets you. Farewell. If you are going to send a boy, send him with a signed document so that I may be more secure. From Vindolanda, to Cocceius Maritimus, from Maior.
EDCS-00380623.summary: This is a personal letter, found inscribed, detailing family matters, business transactions, and requesting a written confirmation of payment from Maritimus to Maior, originating from Vindolanda.

EDCS-03000499.summary: This is a dedication or tomb inscription for Tiberius Claudius Catuarus.

EDCS-03000501.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti Britannicum ex argentariis Veb Lucius R Adiutor\n
EDCS-03000501.translation: From the money-changers of Emperor Vespasian Augustus, conqueror of Britain. Lucius Adiutor (dedicated?).
EDCS-03000501.summary: This is a dedication, possibly by Lucius Adiutor, related to the money-changers and Emperor Vespasian's victory in Britain, likely from the 1st century AD.

EDCS-03000502.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti Britannicum ex argentariis Veb Lucius R Adiutor\n
EDCS-03000502.translation: From the money-changers of Emperor Vespasian Augustus, conqueror of Britain. Lucius Adiutor (dedicated?).
EDCS-03000502.summary: This is a dedication, possibly by Lucius Adiutor, related to the money-changers and Emperor Vespasian's victory in Britain, likely from the 1st century AD.

EDCS-03000506.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the name of Secundinus, son of Maximus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03000511.summary: This inscription records the initials and possibly the name of Publius P. R., likely a personal dedication or ownership mark from the Roman period.

EDCS-03000519.translation: Emperor Carausius, August, bringer of Peace.
EDCS-03000519.summary: This inscription proclaims Emperor Carausius and his title 'Augustus' and 'Pax Augusta', dating it to his reign (286-293 AD).

EDCS-03000525.text_edition: \n Matrix tibi dico sede in tuo loco VO--S dedit tibi adiuro te per Ia et per Saba et per Adnai ne latus teneas sed sede in tuo loco nec noceas Cleuomedem filiam A\n
EDCS-03000525.translation: I tell you, Matrix, to remain in your place; she gave you to me. I adjure you by Ia, Saba, and Adnai, not to hold the side [of the child?], but to remain in your place and not to harm Cleuomedes, daughter of A[...].
EDCS-03000525.summary: This is an adjuration or magical inscription, possibly a curse or charm, directed at 'Matrix' to protect Cleuomedes, dating from the Roman period.

EDCS-03000526.translation: From the century of Ulpius Reginus.
EDCS-03000526.summary: This inscription indicates a division or unit named after Ulpius Reginus, likely related to a military or administrative context from the Roman period.

EDCS-03000543.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius Aurelius M., likely a dedication or ownership mark from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300607.text_edition: \n Deo Mercurio Mintla Rufus donavi eos vel mulier vel PARIVSLIIFASPATEM materiam sagi donavi\n
EDCS-03300607.translation: To the god Mercury, Rufus donated them, whether woman or mother [or similar interpretation]. I donated the material for the cloak.
EDCS-03300607.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Mercury by Rufus, who donated items and material for a cloak, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300609.text_edition: \n Aunillus Vicariana Covitius Mini donat Varicillum Minura Atavacum \n
EDCS-03300609.translation: Aunillus Vicariana, son of Covitius Mini, donates Varicillum and Minura Atavacum.
EDCS-03300609.summary: This is a dedication by Aunillus Vicariana, son of Covitius Mini, donating personal items or land, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-03300610.translation: Lucilia Mellossi, Minuassus, son of Senebellena, from Aexsiemum.
EDCS-03300610.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucilia Mellossi, identified by her patronymic and place of origin, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03300630.text_edition: \n Sumtuaria --- Iunius --- |(denarios) IIII Maternus sumtuaria Bellettus\n
EDCS-03300630.translation: Sumtuaria ---; Maternus paid 4 denarii for the sumtuaria Bellettus.
EDCS-03300630.summary: This inscription records a payment of 4 denarii for a 'sumtuaria Bellettus', possibly related to luxury goods or services, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700500.text_edition: \n SAS VAS Fronto II Tertius II Semilicinus II Sentius I Agilis I Fortunatus I VIIII\n
EDCS-03700500.translation: Fronto (led) 2 (men), Tertius 2, Semilicinus 2, Sentius 1, Agilis 1, Fortunatus 9.
EDCS-03700500.summary: This inscription appears to be a tally or record of individuals, possibly soldiers or laborers, with their respective counts, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700506.summary: This is a military inscription specifying a unit, likely a century commanded by Nigrus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700512.text_edition: \n Nomine Camulorigis et Titocunae molam quam perdederunt sic in fanum sic dei devovi cuicumque numen sic involasit sic molam illam ut sanguinem suum mittat usque diem quo moriatur quicumque involasit! furta moriatur et PAVLATORIAM quicumque illam involasit sic et ipse moriatur quicumque illam involasit sic et VERTOGN de hospitio vel vissacio sic quicumque illam involasit sic a devo sic moriatur\n
EDCS-03700512.translation: In the name of Camulorigis and Titocuna, the mill which they lost; thus they vowed it to the sanctuary, thus to the god. May whoever stole that mill, to whomsoever the divinity has entered, thus shed his own blood until the day he dies, whoever stole it! May he die, and may whoever stole the PAVLATORIAM die, and may he himself die, whoever stole it! Thus may he die, whoever stole it, and thus may VERTOGN of the household or of hospitality die, whoever stole it! Thus I vow, thus may he die.
EDCS-03700512.summary: This is a votive inscription invoking a deity, likely associated with a sanctuary, to curse anyone who stole a mill and potentially other items, threatening death and illness upon the thief, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700514.translation: The pottery workshop of Placidus of Marsianensis.
EDCS-03700514.summary: This is a maker's mark identifying the pottery workshop of Placidus from Marsianensis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700516.text_edition: \n Corduba X pondo XCCL N CIA pondo CCXXII receptum accepit Auctinus OMA\n
EDCS-03700516.translation: Corduba: 10 pounds, 1250; N CIA: received 222 pounds.
EDCS-03700516.summary: This inscription records weights or quantities, possibly of goods or metals, measured in pounds for Corduba and N CIA, with Auctinus receiving the latter, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-03700521.translation: Seven and a half [units] and of Cassius De-
EDCS-03700521.summary: This inscription is a fragment that seems to record a quantity of 'seven and a half' and possibly refers to a person named Cassius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04900613.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying a person named Martia, also known as Martina, likely a funerary epitaph from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04900617.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Mercurio Honoratus conqueror numini tuo me perdidisse rotas duas et vaccas quattuor et resculas plurimas de hospitiolo meo rogaverim genium numinis tuui sic ut ei qui mihi fraudem fecerit sanitatem ei non permittas nec iacere nec sedere nec bibere nec manducare si baro si mulier si puer si puella si servus si liber nissi sic meam rem ad me pertulerit et meam concordiam habuerit iteratis praecibus sic rogo numen tuum ut petitio mea statim pareat me vindicatum esse a maiestate tua\n
EDCS-04900617.translation: To the holy god Mercury, Honoratus complains that he lost two wheels, four cows, and very many small things from his small household. I implore the divine spirit of your god that, if the one who defrauded me, your divinity may not permit him health, nor to lie down, nor to sit, nor to drink, nor to eat, whether he be a baron, a woman, a boy, a girl, a slave, or a free person, unless he brings my property back to me and has my harmony. With repeated prayers, I thus ask your divinity that my petition may be immediately heeded, and that I may be vindicated by your majesty.
EDCS-04900617.summary: This is a lengthy votive and imprecatory inscription to Mercury, where Honoratus describes stolen goods and curses the thief, requesting divine retribution and the return of his property, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-04900618.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely recording the patronymic of an individual.

EDCS-04900627.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelia Sentica vixit annos XXXV Aurelius Verulus coniuci sic karissimae\n
EDCS-04900627.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aelia Sentica lived 35 years. Aurelius Verulus [erected this] for his most beloved wife.
EDCS-04900627.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the death of Aelia Sentica, dedicated by her husband Aurelius Verulus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-04900630.text_edition: \n Imperatore Domitiano Imperatore Domitiano VIIII consule VII Idus Novembres Quintus Cassius Secundus miles legionis XX centuria Calvi Prisci scribsi me debere Gaio Geminio Mansueto militi legionis eiusdem centuria Vetti Proculi denarios centum quos \n
EDCS-04900630.translation: During the consulship of Emperor Domitian for the 9th time, and the 7th consulship [of someone else?], on the Ides of November. Quintus Cassius Secundus, a soldier of the 20th legion, century of Calvus Priscus, wrote that he owes one hundred denarii to Gaius Geminius Mansuetus, a soldier of the same legion, century of Vettius Proculus, which...
EDCS-04900630.summary: This is a military record of a debt, dated to November 13, 81 CE, between two soldiers of Legio XX.

EDCS-04900641.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely a name associated with a monument or property.

EDCS-05000531.translation: Marcus Ulpius Maxim---, primus pilus (chief centurion)
EDCS-05000531.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription commemorating Marcus Ulpius Maxim---, who held the high rank of primus pilus, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-05000532.text_edition: \n Olivarum albarum CCL Caio L A Averni\n
EDCS-05000532.translation: 250 measures of white olives for Gaius L. A. Avernius
EDCS-05000532.summary: This inscription records a quantity of goods (olives) intended for a recipient named Gaius L. A. Avernius, possibly related to trade or an estate.

EDCS-05000534.text_edition: \n Luci Iuli Salutaris penicillum ad lippitudinem Marini caesarianum\n
EDCS-05000534.translation: Lucius Iulius Salutaris's brush for eye-inflammation, the Caesarian type
EDCS-05000534.summary: This inscription describes a medical product, specifically an eye salve or treatment, sold or prepared by Lucius Iulius Salutaris.

EDCS-05000537.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription recording the name of Publius Anicius Sedatus, likely indicating ownership or dedication.

EDCS-05000539.summary: This inscription records the name and rank of Lucius I. L., who was a centurion, likely for identification on a monument or grave.

EDCS-05000546.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription with a repeated name, possibly a dedication or a possessive statement.

EDCS-05000550.translation: Of the elite horsemen of Magnus.
EDCS-05000550.summary: This inscription records a designation related to the elite cavalry (equites singulares) of a person named Magnus, likely from a military context.

EDCS-05000551.summary: This inscription is a fragment naming Quintus Valerius L, possibly indicating ownership or a dedication, with an incomplete context.

EDCS-05000554.summary: This inscription is a dedication to a person named Albano, identified as a tanner (seplasiarius).

EDCS-05000557.translation: Of the century of Claudius Agis.
EDCS-05000557.summary: This inscription likely refers to a military unit, specifically a century commanded by Claudius Agis.

EDCS-05000559.text_edition: \n XV Kalendas Iunias numerus purus cohortis I Tungrorum cui praeest Iulius Verecundus praefectus DCCLII in is |(centuriones) VI ex eis absentes singulares legati XLVI officio Ferocis Coris CCCXXXVII in is |(centuriones) II Londinio |(centurio) vas ad appadun VI in is |(centurio) I AC allia VIIII in is |(centurio) I Cipendiatum XI in a I XXXXV summa absentes CCCCLVI in is |(centuriones) V reliqui praesentes CCLXXXXVI in is |(centurio) I ex eis aegri XV volnerati sic VI lippientes X summa eorum XXXI reliqui valentes CCLXV in is |(centurio) I\n
EDCS-05000559.translation: On the 19th of May, the full strength of the 1st Cohort of Tungri, under the command of Prefect Julius Verecundus, 752 men, of whom 6 centurions were absent, 46 special troops, 337 in the office of Ferox Coris, of whom 2 centurions were absent, one centurion at Londinium, 6 at Appadum, of whom 1 centurion was absent, 7 in Alia, of whom 1 centurion was absent, 11 in Cipendiatum, 45 in total. Absent: 456, of whom 5 centurions. Present: 296, of whom 1 centurion. Of these, 15 sick, 6 wounded, 10 with sore eyes, a total of 31. The remaining 265 fit for duty, of whom 1 centurion.
EDCS-05000559.summary: This inscription details the strength and status of the 1st Cohort of Tungri on a specific date, listing numbers of men, centurions, and their conditions (absent, present, sick, wounded), likely for administrative or military record-keeping.

EDCS-05200468.text_edition: \n Chrauttius Veldeio suo fratri contubernali antiquo plurimam salutem et rogo te Veldei frater miror quod mihi tot tempus nihil rescripsti a parentibus nostris si quid audieris aut QVOT M in quo numero sit et illum a me salutabiss sic verbis meis et Virilem veterinarium rogabis illum ut forficem quam mihi promisit pretio mittas per aliquem de nostris et rogo te frater Virilis salutes a me Thuttenam sororem Velbuteium rescribas nobis cum se habeat opto sis felicissimus vale Londini Veldedeio equisioni consularis a Chrauttio fratre\n
EDCS-05200468.translation: Chrauttius sends many greetings to his old tent-mate Veldeius. I wonder, brother Veldeius, why you have not replied to me for so long. If you have heard anything from our parents, or about QVOT M and what rank he holds, and greet him on my behalf with these words. And ask Virilis the veterinarian to send me the shears you promised me for a price through one of our men. And I ask you, brother, to greet Thutta my sister, and write back to us about how you are. I wish you the best. Farewell from Londinium. To Veldedeius, of the consular cavalry, from Chrauttius, his brother.
EDCS-05200468.summary: This is a personal letter inscribed on stone, likely a funerary monument or a memorial, from Chrauttius to his friend and former comrade Veldeius, discussing family news, personal requests, and greetings, dating to the Roman period in Britain.

EDCS-05200469.text_edition: \n Octavius Candido fratri suo salutem a Marino Nervio pondo centum explicabo e quo tu de hac re scripseras ne mentionem mihi fecit aliquotiens tibi scripseram spicas me emisse prope modios quinque milia propter quod |(denarii) mihi necessari sunt nisi mittis me aliquit sic |(denariorum) minime quingentos futurum est ut quod arrae dedi perdam |(denarios) circa trecentos et erubescam ita rogo quam primum aliquit sic |(denariorum) mi mitte Coria quae scribis esse Cataractonio scribe dentur mi et karrum de quo scribis et quit sic sit cum eo karro mi scribe iam illec sic cepissem nissi sic iumenta non curavi vexsare sic dum viae malae sunt vide cum Tertio de denariis VIII semis quos a Fatale accepit non illos mi accepto tulit scito mae explesse coria CLXX et bracis excussi habeo modios CXI fac |(denarios) mi mittas ut possim spicam habere in excussorio iam autem si quit sic habui perexcussi contubernalis Fronti amici hic fuerat desiderabat coria ei adsignarem et ita |(denarios) daturus erat dixi ei coria intra Kalendas Martias daturum Idibus Ianuariis constituerat se venturum nec intervenit nec curavit accipere cum haberet coria si pecuniam daret dabam ei Frontinium Iulium audio magno licere pro cori ratione quem hic comparavit |(denarios) quinos saluta Spectatum Ianuarium Firmum epistulas a Gleucone accepi vale Vindolandae\n
EDCS-05200469.translation: Octavius sends greetings to his brother Candidus. From Marino, one hundred pounds [of grain?]. Regarding the matter you wrote to me about, he did not mention it to me, although I wrote to you several times that I bought about five thousand modii of spelt, for which I need [denarii]. If you do not send me some [denarii], at least five hundred, it is likely that I will lose what I have given as earnest money, about three hundred [denarii], and be ashamed. So I ask you to send me some [denarii] as soon as possible. Write to Coria that they should be given to me, and the cart about which you write, and whatever is with that cart, write to me. Now, I would have taken it, but I did not bother to bring the pack animals because the roads are bad. See with Tertius about the eight and a half denarii which he received from Fatale; he has not acknowledged them to me. Know that I have finished processing 170 hides and have threshed 111 modii of barley. Make it so that you send me [denarii] so that I can have spelt in the threshing floor. Now, if I had had anything, I would have processed it. My tent-mate Frontus, my friend, was here and wanted me to assign hides to him, and for that he was going to give [denarii]. I told him I would give him the hides before the Kalends of March. He had arranged to come on the Ides of January, but he neither came nor bothered to receive them when he had the hides. If he paid the money, I would give him. I hear Frontinius Julius is selling dearly for [a?] hide ration, which he bought here for five [denarii]. Greet Spectatus, Ianuaris, Firmus. I received letters from Gleucon. Farewell from Vindolanda.
EDCS-05200469.summary: This inscription is a long letter from Octavius to his brother Candidus, discussing financial matters, a business deal involving hides and grain, and requests for money, likely originating from the Roman fort of Vindolanda in Britain.

EDCS-05500596.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Publia Valeria Maximina nnorum VI Oppia Valeria et Sextus Pompeius Capratinus filiae pientissimae faciendum curaverunt sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-05500596.translation: Sacred to the Divine Shades. Publia Valeria Maximina, aged six years. Oppia Valeria and Sextus Pompeius Capratinus had this made for their most devoted daughter. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-05500596.summary: This inscription is a tombstone for a young girl named Publia Valeria Maximina, erected by her parents, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-06100390.text_edition: \n Nomen furis qui frenem involaverit si liber si servus si baro si mulier deo donator duas partes AFIMA sua tertia ad sanitatem\n
EDCS-06100390.translation: The name of the thief who stole the bridle: whether he is a free man, a slave, a barbarian, or a woman, let him/her dedicate two parts of his/her property to the god, and the third part for his/her health.
EDCS-06100390.summary: This inscription is a votive dedication associated with a theft, possibly set up at a shrine, stipulating a penance involving dedication of property to a deity for the recovery of a stolen bridle.

EDCS-06100391.text_edition: \n Deo Mercurio Docilinus QVAENM Varianus et Peregrina et Sabinianus qui pecori meo dolum malum intulerunt et INT RR prolocuntur sic rogo te ut eos maximo leto adigas nec eis sanitatem nec somnum permittas nisi a te quod mihi administraverint redemerint\n
EDCS-06100391.translation: To the god Mercury. Docilinus, the Varian, and Peregrina and Sabinianus, who brought malice to my flock, and INT RR [illegible text]. I ask you, that you drive them to the greatest suffering and permit neither health nor sleep to them, unless they have redeemed from you what they administered to me.
EDCS-06100391.summary: This inscription is a curse or imprecation directed at a god, likely Mercury, by Docilinus against individuals who harmed his flock, seeking divine punishment and restitution.

EDCS-07000709.text_edition: \n Biccus dat Mercurio quidquid perdidit si vir si mascet ne meiat sic ne cacet ne loquatur ne dormiat ne vigilet nec salutem nec sanitatem nessi sic in templo Mercurii pertulerit ne conscientiam de perferat sic nessi sic me intercedente\n
EDCS-07000709.translation: Biccus dedicates to Mercury whatever he has lost, whether he be a man or male, let him not urinate, nor be silent, nor speak, nor sleep, nor stay awake, nor have health, nor be well, unless he has brought it into the temple of Mercury; let him not carry this conscience about him, unless, with me acting as intermediary.
EDCS-07000709.summary: This is a votive inscription by Biccus, dedicating lost property to Mercury and imposing curses on anyone who breaks the vow.

EDCS-07000710.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Lingonum equitata cui praeest Fulvius Felix praefectus \n
EDCS-07000710.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, by the First Cohort of Lingones, cavalry unit, commanded by Fulvius Felix, prefect.
EDCS-07000710.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter by the first cohort of Lingones cavalry, led by their prefect, Fulvius Felix.

EDCS-07000715.translation: You will give [it] to Julius Macrinus.
EDCS-07000715.summary: This is a brief inscription, likely a reminder or instruction, stating that something is to be given to Julius Macrinus.

EDCS-07000719.translation: Of the century of Quintus Attius Cilo.
EDCS-07000719.summary: This inscription likely marks the ownership or dedication of a tomb or monument belonging to the century of Quintus Attius Cilo.

EDCS-07000720.text_edition: \n Deo Vinotono Silvano Augusto Titus Orbius Primianus praefectus cohortis I Thracum \n
EDCS-07000720.translation: To the Divine God Vinotonus Silvanus Augustus, Titus Orbius Primianus, prefect of the First Cohort of Thracians.
EDCS-07000720.summary: This is a dedication to the god Vinotonus Silvanus Augustus by Titus Orbius Primianus, prefect of the First Cohort of Thracians.

EDCS-07400541.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription that appears to be a dedication or a plea addressed to someone named Sanctus.

EDCS-07400545.summary: This is a simple inscription naming L. Ursillus, possibly as the owner of the object or the dedicator.

EDCS-07400549.text_edition: \n Tibi rogo Metunus ut me vendicas de iste sic numene sic me vendicas ante quod veniant dies novem rogo te Metunus ut tu mi vendicas ante quod veniant dies novem\n
EDCS-07400549.translation: I beg you, Metunus, that you vindicate me from this situation; I beg you, Metunus, that you vindicate me before nine days pass.
EDCS-07400549.summary: This is a votive inscription or prayer addressed to the deity Metunus, asking for vindication within a specific timeframe.

EDCS-07400550.text_edition: \n Exsup<e=A>rantius Silvi<o=E>l<a=E>, Sattavill<a=E>, Exsup<e=A>ratus Silvicol<ae=E> Avitus Melusso datus pervici <te=TIBI> Santinus Magetus APIDIMIS Antonius Sanctus Vassianus Varasius datus \n
EDCS-07400550.translation: Exsuperantius, son of Silvius, Exsuperatus, son of Silvicola, Avitus, son of Melussus, dedicated by the vicus Santinus Magetus, to the Apidimi, Antonius Sanctus, Vassianus Varasius, dedicated.
EDCS-07400550.summary: This is a complex dedication by several individuals, likely from a vicus (village or district), possibly to local deities or for the well-being of the community.

EDCS-07400552.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus decurionis coloniae EboracensisVVCVS Marcus Aurelius Irenaeus pientissime fecit\n
EDCS-07400552.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Marcus Aurelius Irenaeus, most devotedly, made [this] for a decurion of the colony of Eboracum.
EDCS-07400552.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording that Marcus Aurelius Irenaeus, a decurion of the colony of Eboracum, had this monument made most devotedly.

EDCS-07400555.translation: To the holy god Vitiris, CRN dedicated.
EDCS-07400555.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the god Vitiris.

EDCS-07400556.text_edition: \n Legionis VI victricis piae fidelis centuria Severi pedes CII\n
EDCS-07400556.translation: Of the VI Victrix Legion, pious and faithful, Severus's century, 102 feet.
EDCS-07400556.summary: This inscription records a measurement, likely land, associated with a century of the VI Victrix Legion.

EDCS-07400559.text_edition: \n Claudia Severa Lepidinae suae salutem III Idus Septembres soror ad diem sollemnem natalem meum rogo libenter facias uti venias ad nos iucundiorem mihi diem interventu tuo factura si s Cerialem tuum saluta Aelius meus te et filios salutant sperabo te soror vale soror anima mea ita valeam karissima et have Sulpiciae Lepidinae Cerialis a Claudia Severa\n
EDCS-07400559.translation: Claudia Severa to her Lepidina, greetings. On the Ides of September, I beg you to come to me gladly for my birthday celebration; you will make the day more pleasant for me by your presence. If Cerialis greets your husband, my Aelius greets you. I will wait for you, sister. Farewell, my dearest sister, my soul. And so farewell, and greetings. From Claudia Severa to Sulpicia Lepidina Cerialis.
EDCS-07400559.summary: This is a personal letter inscribed on stone, likely a tombstone, from Claudia Severa to her sister Sulpicia Lepidina, inviting her to her birthday celebration.

EDCS-07600291.text_edition: \n Donatio diebus quo perit Butu resque quae nec ante sanetate sic nec salute nesi sic quam in domo die sanetate sic in domo dei\n
EDCS-07600291.translation: A donation made on the days when Butus perished, and things which were neither before healed nor saved like this in the house, like this in the house of the god.
EDCS-07600291.summary: This inscription appears to record a donation related to misfortune and healing, possibly in a religious context.

EDCS-07600292.text_edition: \n Legionis XX Valeriae victricis cohortis centuria Corneli Crescentis\n
EDCS-07600292.translation: Of the XX Valeria Victrix Legion, Cornelus Crescentis's cohort, century.
EDCS-07600292.summary: This inscription identifies a military unit, specifically a century within a cohort of the XX Valeria Victrix Legion.

EDCS-07600293.text_edition: \n Docimedis perdidit manicilia dua qui illas involvit ut mentes suas perdat et oculos suos in fano ubi destinat\n
EDCS-07600293.translation: Docimedis lost two maniculae; whoever stole them may they lose their minds and their eyes in the sanctuary where they dedicated them.
EDCS-07600293.summary: This inscription records a curse against the thief of two maniculae (gloves or similar items) belonging to Docimedis, invoking divine retribution.

EDCS-07600294.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Roman emperor Postumus.

EDCS-07800228.text_edition: \n Ulpius Silvanus factus Arausione emeritus legionis II Augustae votum solvit\n
EDCS-07800228.translation: Ulpius Silvanus, having been discharged from the II Augusta Legion, fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800228.summary: This inscription records a soldier, Ulpius Silvanus, fulfilling a vow after his honorable discharge from the II Augusta Legion.

EDCS-07800231.text_edition: \n Titus Egnatius Tyrannus defictus est et Publius Cicereius Felix defictus est Titus Egnatius Tyrannus defictus est et Publius Cicereius Felix\n
EDCS-07800231.translation: Titus Egnatius Tyrannus died, and Publius Cicereius Felix died. Titus Egnatius Tyrannus died, and Publius Cicereius Felix.
EDCS-07800231.summary: This inscription records the death of two individuals, Titus Egnatius Tyrannus and Publius Cicereius Felix.

EDCS-07800232.text_edition: \n Tretiam Mariam defi<g=C> et ill<i=E>us vitam et mentem et memoriam et iocinera pulmones intermxixita fata cogitata memoriam s<ic=CI> non possitt loqui (quae) s<e=I>creta sint neque SINIT am<a=E>re possit neque claudo \n
EDCS-07800232.translation: Tretia Maria... and his life and mind and memory and liver, lungs... fate, thoughts, memory... cannot speak secret things, nor can he love, nor lame...
EDCS-07800232.summary: This fragmented inscription seems to be part of a curse or lament, detailing the afflictions upon Tretia Maria and possibly her associates.

EDCS-07800234.text_edition: \n Aulus Alfidius Pomptina Olussa ex testamento heres posuit nnum LXX natus Athenis hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800234.translation: Aulus Alfidius Olussa, heir of Pomptina, by his will, placed this monument; aged 70 years, he is buried here at Athens.
EDCS-07800234.summary: This funerary epitaph, likely from Athens and dated to the Roman Imperial period, records the burial of Aulus Alfidius Olussa, who provided for his monument in his will.

EDCS-07800235.text_edition: \n Marco Aurelio Eucarpo filio pientissimo vixit nnos XV menses VI Aurelia Eucarpia mater possuit sic\n
EDCS-07800235.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Eucarpus, my most dutiful son, who lived 15 years and 6 months. Aurelia Eucarpia, mother, placed this.
EDCS-07800235.summary: This epitaph, likely from the Roman Imperial period, records the tombstone erected by Aurelia Eucarpia for her son, Marcus Aurelius Eucarpus.

EDCS-07800236.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Agricola miles legionis VI Victricis vixit annos XLII dies X Albia Faustina coniugi inconparabili faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800236.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavius Agricola, a soldier of the VI Victrix Legion, who lived 42 years and 10 days. Albia Faustina had this made for her incomparable wife.
EDCS-07800236.summary: This funerary inscription, from the Roman Imperial period, records that Albia Faustina commissioned this monument for her husband, Flavius Agricola, a soldier of the VI Victrix Legion.

EDCS-07800238.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Valens miles legionis XX Valeriae victricis annorum XL hic situs est curam agente Flavio Attio herede\n
EDCS-07800238.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Julius Valens, a soldier of the XX Valeria Victrix Legion, aged 40 years, is buried here. The care was managed by his heir, Flavius Attius.
EDCS-07800238.summary: This Roman Imperial period funerary epitaph records the burial of Julius Valens, a soldier of the XX Valeria Victrix Legion, with Flavius Attius acting as his heir.

EDCS-07800239.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titi Licini Ascani vivus sibi fecit\n
EDCS-07800239.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Titus Licinius Ascani made this for himself while alive.
EDCS-07800239.summary: This Roman Imperial period funerary inscription records Titus Licinius Ascani's self-funded tomb, intended for himself.

EDCS-07800241.text_edition: \n Memoriae Valeri Amandini Valeri Superventor et Marcellus patri fecerunt\n
EDCS-07800241.translation: For the memory of Valerius Amandinus. Valerius Superventor and Marcellus made this for their father.
EDCS-07800241.summary: This Roman Imperial period funerary epitaph records that Valerius Superventor and Marcellus erected a monument in memory of their father, Valerius Amandinus.

EDCS-07800242.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vivio Marciano |(centurione) legionis II Augustae Ianuaria Martina coniunx pientissima posuit memoriam\n
EDCS-07800242.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Ianuaria Martina, his most dutiful wife, placed this memorial for Vivius Marcianus, a centurion of the II Augusta Legion.
EDCS-07800242.summary: This Roman Imperial period funerary inscription records Ianuaria Martina's memorial for her husband, Vivius Marcianus, a centurion in the II Augusta Legion.

EDCS-07800244.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus r Luci filius Claudia Celsus speculator legionis II Augustae Antonius Dardanus Cursor Valerius Pudens s Probus speculatores legionis\n
EDCS-07800244.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Lucius, son of Claudia Celsus, a speculator (scout/sharpshooter) of the II Augusta Legion; Antonius Dardanus Cursor and Valerius Pudens s Probus, speculators of the legion.
EDCS-07800244.summary: This Roman Imperial period funerary inscription names several individuals, including Lucius, son of Claudia Celsus, and speculatores (scouts) from the II Augusta Legion, possibly recording their deaths or service.

EDCS-07800245.text_edition: \n Gaius Etruscus Atiae carissimae suae meritis eius\n
EDCS-07800245.translation: Gaius Etruscus to his dearest Atia, for her merits.
EDCS-07800245.summary: This Roman Imperial period inscription is a dedication by Gaius Etruscus to his beloved Atia, honoring her merits.

EDCS-07800246.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Claudiae Martinae annorum XIX Anencletus provincialis coniugi pientissimae hic sita est\n
EDCS-07800246.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Claudia Martina, aged 19. Anencletus, a provincial, placed this here for his most dutiful wife.
EDCS-07800246.summary: This Roman Imperial period funerary epitaph records the burial of Claudia Martina, aged 19, with the monument erected by her husband, Anencletus.

EDCS-07800247.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Grata Dagobiti filia annorum XL Solinus coniugi karissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800247.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Solinus had this made for his dearest wife, Grata, daughter of Dagobitus, aged 40.
EDCS-07800247.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Grata, set up by her husband Solinus, dating likely to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07800268.translation: To Ulpia, my daughter, from her mother.
EDCS-07800268.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Ulpia, dedicated by her mother, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800269.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to provide a meaningful translation or summary.

EDCS-07800300.text_edition: \n Neptuno aram Lucius Aufidius Pantera praefectus classis Britannicae\n
EDCS-07800300.translation: Lucius Aufidius Pantera, prefect of the British fleet, an altar to Neptune.
EDCS-07800300.summary: This dedicatory inscription records an altar set up for Neptune by Lucius Aufidius Pantera, prefect of the British fleet, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800302.text_edition: \n Iuliae Augustae matri senatus et castrorum Marcus Sabinius Victor ob \n
EDCS-07800302.translation: To Julia Augusta, mother of the Senate and of the camps, Marcus Sabinius Victor [set this up] on account of...
EDCS-07800302.summary: This dedicatory inscription, possibly for an altar or monument, is set up by Marcus Sabinius Victor to Julia Augusta, mother of the Senate and of the camps, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07800308.summary: This inscription is too fragmentary to provide a meaningful translation or summary.

EDCS-07800323.text_edition: \n Memoriae Flaviae Victorinae Titus Tammonius Victor coniunx posuit\n
EDCS-07800323.translation: Titus Tammonius Victor placed this to the memory of Flavia Victorina, his wife.
EDCS-07800323.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Flavia Victorina, set up by her husband Titus Tammonius Victor, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800324.text_edition: \n Matribus Italis Germanis Gallis Britannis Antonius Lucretianus beneficiarius consularis restituit\n
EDCS-07800324.translation: Antonius Lucretianus, a beneficiarius of a consular governor, restored [this] to the Italian, German, and British Mothers.
EDCS-07800324.summary: This dedicatory inscription records the restoration of a shrine to the Matres Italis, Germanis, Gallis, Britannis by Antonius Lucretianus, a beneficiarius consularis, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800326.text_edition: \n Genio sacrum Lucullus Ammini filius de suo posuit\n
EDCS-07800326.translation: Sacred to the Genius. Lucullus, son of Amminus, set this up at his own expense.
EDCS-07800326.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription for a shrine to a Genius, set up at his own expense by Lucullus, son of Amminus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800327.text_edition: \n Neptuno et Minervae templum pro salute domus divinae ex auctoritate Tiberi Claudi Cogidubni regis magni Britanniae collegium fabrorum et qui in eo sunt de suo dederunt donante aream ente Pudentini filio\n
EDCS-07800327.translation: The guild of builders and those who are in it, gave a temple to Neptune and Minerva for the safety of the divine house at the authority of Tiberius Claudius Cogidubnus, great king of Britain, as a gift, with the area being donated by Pudentinus, son of [son of?].
EDCS-07800327.summary: This is a dedicatory inscription recording a temple built for Neptune and Minerva by the guild of builders for the safety of the imperial family, under the authority of King Cogidubnus, likely in the mid-1st century CE.

EDCS-07800330.text_edition: \n cca Aelia Cauva filia annorum XXXVI\n
EDCS-07800330.summary: This inscription records the age of Aelia Cauva, likely from a tombstone, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-07800331.summary: This inscription records the age of Catia Censorina, likely from a tombstone, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-07800336.text_edition: \n Deae Ancastae Geminus Mani votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800336.translation: Geminus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the goddess Ancasta.
EDCS-07800336.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Geminus fulfilling a vow to the goddess Ancasta.

EDCS-07800337.translation: To Marcus Aurelius Carinus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-07800337.summary: This is an inscription dedicating something to Marcus Aurelius Carinus, a noble Caesar, likely from the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-07800338.summary: This inscription records that Civilis made something, likely a monument or artwork.

EDCS-07800342.inscription_type: carmina, inscriptiones christianae, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-07800342.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Septimius vir perfectissimus praeses Britanniae primae restituit civis Remus Signum et erectam prisca religione columnam Septimius renovat primae provinciae rector\n
EDCS-07800342.translation: Lucius Septimius, a most distinguished man and governor of the first province of Britannia, restored this column to Jupiter Optimus Maximus; Septimius, governor of the first province, renews the statue and the column, erected with ancient reverence.
EDCS-07800342.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a statue and column to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Lucius Septimius, governor of Britannia Prima, likely in the Roman period.

EDCS-07800343.translation: To the god Silvanus, Sabidius Maximus.
EDCS-07800343.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus by Sabidius Maximus.

EDCS-07800344.text_edition: \n Sulevis Sulinus Bruceti votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800344.translation: Sulinus Bruceti willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Sulevis.
EDCS-07800344.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Sulinus Bruceti fulfilling a vow to the Sulevis, likely Celtic deities.

EDCS-07800345.summary: This inscription names Sulevis Primus, likely a person or deity, possibly with a military context due to 'IIAS'.

EDCS-07800347.text_edition: \n Dannicus eques alae Indianae turma Albani stipendiorum XVI cives Rauricus curaverunt Fulvius Natalis it Flavius Bitucus ex testamento hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800347.translation: Dannicus, a cavalryman of the ala Indiana, of the troop of Albani, with 16 years of service, and the citizens of Rauricus, had this done; Fulvius Natalis and Flavius Bitucus are buried here according to their wills.
EDCS-07800347.summary: This inscription records military service and burial arrangements, noting Dannicus of the ala Indiana and the burial of Fulvius Natalis and Flavius Bitucus, likely from the Roman military period in a province.

EDCS-07800348.text_edition: \n Sextus Valerius Genialis eques alae Trhaecum civis Frisiavus turma Genialis annorum XXXX stipendiorum XX hic situs est heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800348.translation: Sextus Valerius Genialis, a cavalryman of the Thracian ala, citizen of Frisia, of the troop of Genialis, aged 40, with 20 years of service, is buried here; his heir arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-07800348.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Sextus Valerius Genialis, a Roman cavalry soldier, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800349.text_edition: \n Philus Cassavi filius civis Sequanus nnorum XXXXV hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800349.translation: Philus, son of Cassavus, a citizen of the Sequani, aged 45, is buried here.
EDCS-07800349.summary: This tombstone marks the burial of Philus, a Sequanian citizen, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800350.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Publiae Vicanae Publius Vitalis coniux\n
EDCS-07800350.translation: To the Divine Shades. Publius Vitalis, husband, for Publia Vicana.
EDCS-07800350.summary: This is a funerary dedication by a husband to his deceased wife, Publia Vicana, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800351.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Castae Castrensis vixit annos \n
EDCS-07800351.translation: To the Divine Shades. Castra lived for [unknown number of] years.
EDCS-07800351.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Castra, indicating her age is missing, probably from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800352.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iuliae Castae coniugi vixit nnos XXXIII\n
EDCS-07800352.translation: To the Divine Shades. For Julia Casta, his wife, lived 33 years.
EDCS-07800352.summary: This funerary dedication records the life span of Julia Casta, wife of the dedicator, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800358.text_edition: \n Deo Genio cohortis cunctae Morivendus VV\n
EDCS-07800358.translation: To the Genius of the entire cohort, Morivendus made this.
EDCS-07800358.summary: This dedicatory inscription honors the Genius of a cohort, made by Morivendus, possibly dating to the 2nd-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800360.text_edition: \n Rufus Sita eques cohortis VI Tracum nnorum XL stipendiorum XXII heredes exs testamento faciendum curaverunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800360.translation: Rufus Sita, a cavalryman of the 6th cohort of Thracians, aged 40, with 22 years of service, is buried here; his heirs arranged for this to be made according to his will.
EDCS-07800360.summary: This tombstone records the burial of Rufus Sita, a Roman cavalry soldier, who left instructions for his tomb in his will, likely from the 2nd-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800361.text_edition: \n miles legionis XX centuria Livi Saturnini stipendiorum XIII annorum XXXX\n
EDCS-07800361.translation: A soldier of the 20th legion, centuria of Livius Saturninus, with 13 years of service, aged 40.
EDCS-07800361.summary: This inscription identifies a soldier by his legion, centuria, service years, and age, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800362.translation: Ingenuina, his wife, lived for 20 years.
EDCS-07800362.summary: This funerary inscription records the life span of Ingenuina, wife of the dedicator, likely from the 1st-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800371.text_edition: \n Deo Romulo Gulioepius donavit Iuventinus fecit\n
EDCS-07800371.translation: To the god Romulus. Gulioepius dedicated this; Iuventinus made it.
EDCS-07800371.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Romulus, mentioning both the dedicator and the maker, likely from the 2nd-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800372.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Ingenuilla vixit annos XX menses V dies XXVIIII\n
EDCS-07800372.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Iulia Ingenuilla lived 20 years, 5 months, and 29 days.
EDCS-07800372.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Iulia Ingenuilla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800373.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Sulicena annos XIIII vixit\n
EDCS-07800373.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Sulicena lived 14 years.
EDCS-07800373.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Sulicena, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800375.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Metti natione Geta vixit nnos XXXV heres posuit\n
EDCS-07800375.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Metti, by nationality a Geta, lived 35 years; his heir erected this.
EDCS-07800375.summary: This is a funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Metti, a Geta by origin, whose heir set up the monument.

EDCS-07800376.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication to the personification of Hope, possibly on a tomb or shrine.

EDCS-07800377.text_edition: \n Deae Dianae sacratissimae votum solvit Vettius Benignus libertus\n
EDCS-07800377.translation: Vettius Benignus, a freedman, fulfilled a vow to the most sacred goddess Diana.
EDCS-07800377.summary: This is a votive inscription by Vettius Benignus, a freedman, dedicating something to the goddess Diana.

EDCS-07800378.text_edition: \n Genio loci IANP legionis VI victricis Forianus votum solvit laetus libens merito\n
EDCS-07800378.translation: Forianus of the VI Victrix legion fulfilled his vow to the genius of the place, gladly, willingly, and deservedly.
EDCS-07800378.summary: This is a votive inscription by Forianus of the VI Victrix legion to the genius loci.

EDCS-07800379.text_edition: \n Peregrinus Secundi filius civis Trever Loucetio Marti et Nemetona votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800379.translation: Peregrinus, son of Secundus, a citizen of Trier, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Loucetius Mars and Nemetona.
EDCS-07800379.summary: This is a votive inscription by Peregrinus, a citizen of Trier, to the deities Loucetius Mars and Nemetona.

EDCS-07800382.text_edition: \n Claudius Ligur ae nimia vetustate colegio longa seria annorum sua pecunia refici et repingi curavit Gaius Protacius deae Sulis Minervae EPEPAM\n
EDCS-07800382.translation: Gaius Protacius, on account of extreme age and the long years of neglect of the temple, took care to have it restored and repainted at his own expense to the goddess Sulis Minerva.
EDCS-07800382.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a temple to the goddess Sulis Minerva, funded by Gaius Protacius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800385.text_edition: \n Deae Suli pro salute et incolumitate Marci Aufidi Maximi |(centurionis) legionis VI victricis Aufidius Eutuches lebertus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800385.translation: Aufidius Eutuches, freedman, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the goddess Sulis for the health and safety of Marcus Aufidius Maximus, a centurion of the VI Victrix legion.
EDCS-07800385.summary: This is a votive inscription by Aufidius Eutuches, a freedman, to the goddess Sulis for the well-being of the centurion Marcus Aufidius Maximus.

EDCS-07800386.text_edition: \n Deae Suli pro salute et incolumitate Aufidi Maximi |(centurionis) legionis VI victricis Marcus Aufidius Lemnus libertus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800386.translation: Marcus Aufidius Lemnus, a freedman, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the goddess Sulis for the health and safety of Aufidius Maximus, a centurion of the VI Victrix legion.
EDCS-07800386.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Aufidius Lemnus, a freedman, to the goddess Sulis for the well-being of the centurion Aufidius Maximus.

EDCS-07800388.text_edition: \n Deae Suli Minervae et Numinibus Augustorum Gaius Curiatius Saturninus |(centurio) legionis II Augustae pro se suisque votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800388.translation: To the goddess Sulis Minerva and the Divine Powers of the Emperors, Gaius Curiatius Saturninus, a centurion of the Second Legion Augusta, paid his vow for himself and his family, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-07800388.summary: This is a votive inscription to the goddess Sulis Minerva and the Divine Powers of the Emperors, dedicated by a centurion of the Second Legion Augusta.

EDCS-07800389.text_edition: \n Deae Suli ob salutem sacrum Gai Iavoleni Saturnalis imaginiferi legionis II Augustae Lucius Manius Dionisias sic libertus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800389.translation: To the goddess Sulis, for health, Lucius Manius Dionisias, freedman of Gaius Iavolenus Saturnalis, signifer of the Second Legion Augusta, paid his vow, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-07800389.summary: This is a votive inscription to the goddess Sulis for health, dedicated by the freedman of a signifer of the Second Legion Augusta.

EDCS-07800390.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Sulis by Quintus Pompeius Anicetus.

EDCS-07800392.text_edition: \n Deae Suli Minervae Sulinus Maturi filius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800392.translation: Sulinus, son of Maturus, paid his vow to the goddess Sulis Minerva, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-07800392.summary: This is a votive inscription to the goddess Sulis Minerva, dedicated by Sulinus, son of Maturus.

EDCS-07800393.text_edition: \n Sulevis Sulinus scultor Bruceti filius sacrum fecit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800393.translation: Sulinus, son of Brucetus, a sculptor, made this sacred offering to Sulevis, willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-07800393.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Sulevis made by Sulinus, a sculptor and son of Brucetus.

EDCS-07800394.text_edition: \n Locum religiosum per insolentiam erutum virtuti et numini Augusti repurgatum reddidit Gaius Severius Emeritus regionarius\n
EDCS-07800394.translation: Gaius Severius Emeritus, a regional supervisor, restored to virtue and divine authority the imperial power a sacred place, which had been dug up through insolence.
EDCS-07800394.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a sacred place by Gaius Severius Emeritus, a regional supervisor, likely in honor of the Emperor's virtue and divine authority.

EDCS-07800395.text_edition: \n Novanti filius pro se et suis ex visu possuit sic\n
EDCS-07800395.translation: Novanti's son set this up for himself and his family, according to a vision.
EDCS-07800395.summary: This is a dedication set up by Novanti's son for himself and his family, inspired by a vision.

EDCS-07800396.text_edition: \n Qui mihi VILBIAM involavit sic liquat sic comodo sic aqua ella sic muta qui eam voravit si Velvinna Exsupereus Verianus Severinus A<u>gustalis Comitianus Catusminianus Germanilla Iovina\n
EDCS-07800396.translation: Whoever stole VILBIA from me, let him be like this: let VILBIA be like this, let the thief, if he stole it, be rendered dumb, and let the goddess Velvinna Exsupereus Verianus Severinus Augustalis Comitianus Catusminianus Germanilla Iovina punish him.
EDCS-07800396.summary: This is a curse inscription invoking the goddess Velvinna against a thief who stole an object named VILBIA.

EDCS-07800397.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius Calpurnius Receptus sacerdos deae Sulis vixit annos LXXV Calpurnia Trifosa liberta coniunx faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800397.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Caius Calpurnius Receptus, a priest of the goddess Sulis, lived 75 years. Calpurnia Trifosa, his freedwoman wife, saw to the making of this monument.
EDCS-07800397.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Caius Calpurnius Receptus, a priest of the goddess Sulis, erected by his freedwoman wife.

EDCS-07800398.text_edition: \n Iulius Vitalis fabriciensis legionis XX Valeriae victricis stipendiorum IX annorum XXIX natione Belga ex collegio fabricensium elatus hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800398.translation: Julius Vitalis from the armoury workshop, of the 20th Legion Valeria Victrix, after 9 years of service and at the age of 29, a Belgian by nationality, was carried out from the guild of armourers; he is buried here.
EDCS-07800398.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Julius Vitalis, a Belgian armourer of the 20th Legion Valeria Victrix, detailing his service and age.

EDCS-07800399.text_edition: \n Gaius Murrius Gai filius Arniensis Foro Iuli Modestus miles legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis centuria Iuli Secundi nnorum XXV stipendiorum hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800399.translation: Gaius Murrius Modestus, son of Gaius, of the Arnian tribe, from Forum Iulii, a soldier of Legio II Adjutrix Pia Fidelis, in the century of Iulius Secundus, aged 25 years and with 25 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-07800399.summary: This is a soldier's tombstone for Gaius Murrius Modestus, a legionary of Legio II Adjutrix, dating possibly to the 2nd century AD.

EDCS-07800400.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marcus Valerius Marci filius Latinus civis Equester miles legionis XX annorum XXXV stipendiorum XX hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800400.translation: To the divine spirits. Marcus Valerius Latinus, son of Marcus, a citizen of Equester, a soldier of Legio XX, aged 35 years and with 20 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-07800400.summary: This is a soldier's tombstone for Marcus Valerius Latinus, a legionary of Legio XX, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-07800401.text_edition: \n Lucius Vitellius Mantai filius Tancinus cives Hispanus Cauriensis eques alae Vettonum civium Romanorum nnorum XXXXVI stipendiorum XXVI hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800401.translation: Lucius Vitellius Tancinus, son of Mantaius, a citizen of Hispania from Cauria, a cavalryman of the ala Vettonum civium Romanorum, aged 46 years and with 26 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-07800401.summary: This is a tombstone for Lucius Vitellius Tancinus, a Roman cavalryman serving in the ala Vettonum, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-07800402.text_edition: \n Sergia Antigonus Nicopoli emeritus ex legione XX annorum XLV hic situs est Gaius Tiberinus heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800402.translation: To the divine spirits. Sergia Antigonus from Nicopolis, a veteran of Legio XX, aged 45 years, is buried here. Gaius Tiberinus, the heir, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-07800402.summary: This is a tombstone for Sergia Antigonus, a female veteran of Legio XX, with the monument erected by her heir, Gaius Tiberinus, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-07800403.text_edition: \n decurio coloniae Glevensis vixit annos LXXX VI\n
EDCS-07800403.translation: Decurion of the colony of Glevum, lived 86 years.
EDCS-07800403.summary: This is an epitaph for a decurion of the Roman colony of Glevum, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800404.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Merc Magni liberta alumna vixit annum I menses VI dies XII\n
EDCS-07800404.translation: To the divine spirits. Merc, freedwoman and adopted daughter of Magnus, lived 1 year, 6 months, and 12 days.
EDCS-07800404.summary: This is a child's tombstone for Merc, a freedwoman and alumna, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800405.text_edition: \n Rusoniae Aventinae civi Mediomatricae nnum LVIII hic sita est Lucius Ulpius Sestius heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800405.translation: Rusonia Aventina, a citizen of Mediomatrica, aged 58 years, is buried here. Lucius Ulpius Sestius, the heir, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-07800405.summary: This is a tombstone for Rusonia Aventina, a woman from the civitas Mediomatricorum, with the monument erected by her heir, Lucius Ulpius Sestius, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800406.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Successae Petroniae vixit nnos III menses IIII dies IX Vettius Romulus et Victoria Sabina filiae karissimae fecerunt\n
EDCS-07800406.translation: To the divine spirits. Successa Petronia lived 3 years, 4 months, and 9 days. Vettius Romulus and Victoria Sabina made this for their dearest daughter.
EDCS-07800406.summary: This is a tombstone for a child named Successa Petronia, with the monument erected by her parents, Vettius Romulus and Victoria Sabina, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07800407.text_edition: \n Vibia Iucunda annorum XXX hic sepulta est\n
EDCS-07800407.translation: Vibia Iucunda, aged 30 years, is buried here.
EDCS-07800407.summary: This is a tombstone for Vibia Iucunda, a woman who died at the age of 30, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-07800411.summary: This is a very brief epitaph recording the name of a woman, Ilivssa, identified as a wife, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800412.summary: This inscription likely records the name of an individual, Cornelianus, potentially part of a larger dedication or marker, with the date being indeterminate.

EDCS-07800421.text_edition: \n Pro salute imperatoris Caesaris Marci Aureli Antonini Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti Naevius Augusti libertus adiutor procuratorum principia ruina oppressa a solo restituit\n
EDCS-07800421.translation: For the safety of the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus, Naevius, freedman of Augustus, assistant to the procurators, restored the principia from the ground up, which had been crushed by ruins.
EDCS-07800421.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a principia (headquarters building) by Naevius, an assistant to procurators, for the health of Emperor Marcus Aurelius Antoninus, likely dating to his reign (161-180 AD).

EDCS-07800422.translation: dedicated and in the consulship of Quintianus.
EDCS-07800422.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records a dedication made in the consulship of Quintianus, with the dedicator and specific object of dedication missing, and the date inferable from the consul's name.

EDCS-07800423.text_edition: \n Numinibus divorum Augustorum Caius Indutius Felix Silvano votum solvit libens merito CON VIC GA\n
EDCS-07800423.translation: To the divine spirits of the Augusti, Caius Indutius Felix willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Silvanus.
EDCS-07800423.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Caius Indutius Felix paying a vow to Silvanus and honoring the divine spirits of the Emperors, likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-07800424.summary: This inscription fragment likely refers to the title 'Pater Patriae' (Father of the Fatherland) bestowed upon an emperor, possibly accompanied by a sacred designation, with the date indeterminate.

EDCS-07800429.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Rigisamo Iventius Sabinus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07800429.translation: To the god Mars Rigisamus, Iventius Sabinus willingly, gladly, and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-07800429.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Iventius Sabinus paying a vow to the god Mars Rigisamus, likely dating to the Roman imperial period, possibly in a Gallic province.

EDCS-07800433.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Medocio Campesium et Victorie Alexandri Pii Felicis Augusti nostri donum Lossio Veda de suo posuit nepos Vepogeni Caledo\n
EDCS-07800433.translation: To the god Mars Medocius, to Victory, and to Alexander Pius Felix Augustus our lord, Lossio Veda, grandson of Vepogenus Caledo, dedicated this gift from his own resources.
EDCS-07800433.summary: This inscription records Lossio Veda dedicating a gift from his own funds to the gods Mars Medocius and Victory, and to Emperor Alexander Severus, likely dating to his reign (222-235 AD).

EDCS-07800434.text_edition: \n Matribus Sulevis Similis Atti filius civis Cantiacus votum libens solvit\n
EDCS-07800434.translation: To the Mothers, Similis, son of Atti, a citizen of the Cantiaci, willingly paid his vow.
EDCS-07800434.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Similis, son of Atti, a Cantiacus tribesman, paying a vow to the Matres (Mothers), likely dating to the Roman period in Britain.

EDCS-07800435.text_edition: \n Numinibus Augustorum et Mercurio deo Andescociuouco Imilico Aesurilini libertus aram opere maronio de suo dedit\n
EDCS-07800435.translation: To the divine spirits of the Augusti and to the god Andescotiucus Mercurius, Imilico, freedman of Aesurilinus, dedicated an altar of marble work from his own resources.
EDCS-07800435.summary: This inscription records Imilico, a freedman, dedicating a marble altar from his own funds to the divine spirits of the Emperors and to the god Andescotiucus Mercurius, likely dating to the Roman imperial period in Gaul.

EDCS-07800436.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano Callirio donum Cintusmus aerarius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800436.translation: To the god Silvanus, Cintusmus the coppersmith willingly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-07800436.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Cintusmus, a coppersmith, paying a vow to the god Silvanus, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-07800437.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano Hermes votum solvit libens vovit\n
EDCS-07800437.translation: Hermes fulfilled his vow to the god Silvanus, having willingly made the offering.
EDCS-07800437.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Silvanus by Hermes, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800442.text_edition: \n Marcus Favonius Marci filius Pollia Facilis |(centurio) legionis XX Verecundus et Novicius liberti posuerunt hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800442.translation: Marcus Favonius Facilis, son of Marcus, of the Pollia tribe, a centurion of the 20th legion, was laid to rest here; his freedmen Verecundus and Novicius erected this.
EDCS-07800442.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Favonius Facilis, a centurion, erected by his freedmen, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800443.text_edition: \n Longinus Sdapezematygi filius duplicarius ala prima Thracum pago Sardica nnorum XL aerorum XV heres exs sic testamento faciendum curavit hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800443.translation: Longinus, son of Sdapezematygi, a duplicarius in the First Thracian Wing, from the district of Sardica, aged 40 years, 15 years of service, his heir had this made according to his will; he was laid to rest here.
EDCS-07800443.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Longinus, a duplicarius in a Thracian cavalry unit, who died at 40, with his heir fulfilling his will, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800444.text_edition: \n O Macri us eques Romanus vixit annos XX Valeria Frontina coniuux et Florius Cogitatus et Florius Fidelis fecerunt\n
EDCS-07800444.translation: O Macrius, Roman knight, lived 20 years. Valeria Frontina, his wife, and Florius Cogitatus and Florius Fidelis, made this.
EDCS-07800444.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Macrius, a Roman knight, erected by his wife and two others, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800448.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Aurel [died] aged...
EDCS-07800448.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription dedicated to the spirits of the departed (Dis Manibus) for an individual named Aurelius, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800455.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Corotiaco Simplicia pro se votum posuit libens merito Glaucus fecit\n
EDCS-07800455.translation: Simplicia placed this vow for herself to the god Corotiacus Mars, willingly and deservedly; Glaucus made it.
EDCS-07800455.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Celtic god Mars Corotiacus by Simplicia, with Glaucus as the craftsman, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800457.text_edition: \n promisi denarios sex pro voto soluto pecuniam dedi\n
EDCS-07800457.translation: I promised six denarii for the fulfilled vow, I gave the money.
EDCS-07800457.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a financial promise related to a vow, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800460.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Alatori Dum Censorinus Gemelli filius votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800460.translation: Dum Censorinus, son of Gemellus, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the god Mars Alator.
EDCS-07800460.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the god Mars Alator by Dum Censorinus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800461.text_edition: \n Marti Toutati Tiberius Claudius Primus Attii libertus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800461.translation: Tiberius Claudius Primus, freedman of Attius, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to Mars Toutatis.
EDCS-07800461.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to the Celtic god Mars Toutatis by Tiberius Claudius Primus, a freedman, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800463.text_edition: \n Vetus quomodo sanies sign<i=E>ficatur Tacita deficta \n
EDCS-07800463.translation: How old is the old affliction of gangrene...
EDCS-07800463.summary: This inscription appears to be a medical or philosophical text discussing the nature of gangrene, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800475.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Anicius Saturninus strator consularis monumentum sibi fecit\n
EDCS-07800475.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Anicius Saturninus, attendant of a consular, made this monument for himself.
EDCS-07800475.summary: A funerary monument set up by Anicius Saturninus, an attendant of a consular official, for himself, likely dating to the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800476.summary: A fragment mentioning the name Flavius and an age (25), possibly part of a funerary or military record, date uncertain.

EDCS-07800477.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et Numinibus Augustorum Marcus Varius Severus beneficiarius consularis aram cum cancellis de suo posuit\n
EDCS-07800477.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the Divine Powers of the Emperors, Marcus Varius Severus, a beneficiarius of a consular, dedicated this altar with its railing at his own expense.
EDCS-07800477.summary: An altar dedicated by Marcus Varius Severus, a beneficiarius of a consular official, to Jupiter and the Divine Powers of the Emperors, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800496.text_edition: \n Parcis Deabus et Numinibus Augustorum Gaius Antistius Frontinus curator tertium aram de suo dedicavit\n
EDCS-07800496.translation: To the Parcae, the Fates, and to the Divine Powers of the Emperors, Gaius Antistius Frontinus, curator for the third time, dedicated this altar at his own expense.
EDCS-07800496.summary: An altar dedicated by Gaius Antistius Frontinus, who served as curator for the third time, to the Parcae and the Divine Powers of the Emperors, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800499.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Volusia Faustina civis Lindensis vixit nnos XXVI mensem I dies XXVI Aurelius Senecio decurio ob merita coniugi posuit Dis Manibus Claudia Catiotui vixtt annos LX\n
EDCS-07800499.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Volusia Faustina, a citizen of Lindum, lived 26 years, 1 month, and 26 days. Aurelius Senecio, a decurion, set this up for his wife out of consideration for her merits. To the Spirits of the Departed, Claudia Catiotu lived 60 years.
EDCS-07800499.summary: A complex funerary inscription for Volusia Faustina and Claudia Catiotu, possibly mother and daughter or related individuals, with ages and a dedication by Aurelius Senecio, likely from the Roman province of Britannia.

EDCS-07800500.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Helius natione Grecus vixit annos XXXX Flavia Ingenua coniugi posuit\n
EDCS-07800500.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Flavius Helius, a Greek by birth, lived 40 years. Flavia Ingenua set this up for her husband.
EDCS-07800500.summary: A funerary inscription for Flavius Helius, of Greek origin, with his age and a dedication by his wife Flavia Ingenua, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-07800501.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gai Iuli Galeria Caleni Lugduno veterani ex legione VI Victrice pia Fideli heres a se memoriae fecit\n
EDCS-07800501.translation: Dedicated to the Spirits of the Departed. Gaius Iulius Galenus, a veteran of the Sixth Legion Victrix, faithful and devoted, from Lugdunum, his heir made this memorial for him.
EDCS-07800501.summary: A funerary monument for Gaius Iulius Galenus, a veteran of the Sixth Legion Victrix from Lugdunum, erected by his heir, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-07800502.text_edition: \n legionis II adiutricis piae fidelis Ponti Proculi Lucius Licinius Luci filius Galeria Saliga Lugduno annorum XX stipendiorum II\n
EDCS-07800502.translation: From the Second Legion Adjutrix, faithful and devoted. Lucius Licinius, son of Lucius, of the Galerian tribe, from Lugdunum, aged 20 years, with 2 years of service.
EDCS-07800502.summary: A record of a soldier, Lucius Licinius, serving in the Second Legion Adjutrix, with his age and length of service, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800503.text_edition: \n Quinti Corneli Quinti filii Claudia equitis legionis VIIII centuria Cassi Martia<l=E>is annorum XL stipendiorum XIX hic situs est \n
EDCS-07800503.translation: Quintus Cornelius, son of Quintus, of the Claudia tribe, an equestrian soldier in the Ninth Legion, century of Cassius Martialis, aged 40 years, with 19 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-07800503.summary: A tombstone for Quintus Cornelius, an equestrian soldier in the Ninth Legion, detailing his age and service, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800504.text_edition: \n Gaio Saufeio Gai filio Fabia Heraclea militi legionis VIIII nnum XXXX stipendiorum XXII hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800504.translation: To Gaius Saufeius, son of Gaius, of the Fabia tribe, a soldier in the Ninth Legion, aged 40 years, with 22 years of service, is buried here.
EDCS-07800504.summary: A tombstone for Gaius Saufeius, a soldier in the Ninth Legion, detailing his age and service, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800505.text_edition: \n Luci Semproni Flavini militis legionis VIIII centuria Babudi Severi aerum VII annorum XXX Hispani Galeria Clunia\n
EDCS-07800505.translation: Lucius Sempronius Flavius, soldier of the Ninth Legion, of the century of Babudius Severus, aged 37, from Clunia in Hispania.
EDCS-07800505.summary: This epitaph records Lucius Sempronius Flavius, a soldier of the Ninth Legion, who died at the age of 37.

EDCS-07800506.text_edition: \n Caius Valerius Cai filius Maecia miles legionis IX signifer |(centuriae) Hospitis nnorum XXXV stipendiorum XIIII testamento poni iussit hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800506.translation: Gaius Valerius, son of Gaius, of the Maecian tribe, soldier of the Ninth Legion, standard-bearer of the century of Hospitus, aged 35, with 14 years of service, ordered this to be set up by his will; he lies here.
EDCS-07800506.summary: This tombstone commemorates Gaius Valerius, a standard-bearer in the Ninth Legion, who served for 14 years and died at 35.

EDCS-07800507.text_edition: \n Titus Valerius Titi filius Claudia Pudens Savaria miles legionis II Adiutricis piae fidelis centuria Dossenni Proculi annorum XXX aera VI heres de suo posuit hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800507.translation: Titus Valerius, son of Titus, of the Claudian tribe, Pudens, soldier of the Second Adiutrix Legion, the pious and faithful, of the century of Dossennius Proculus, aged 30, with 6 years of service; his heir placed this at his own expense; he lies here.
EDCS-07800507.summary: This epitaph records Titus Valerius Pudens, a soldier in the Second Adiutrix Legion, who died at 30 after 6 years of service.

EDCS-07800508.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Quintus Valerius Victorinus miles ISCON N\n
EDCS-07800508.translation: To the departed spirits: Quintus Valerius Victorinus, soldier of ISCON.
EDCS-07800508.summary: This fragmentary inscription is a dedication to the spirits of the departed, honoring Quintus Valerius Victorinus, a soldier of ISCON.

EDCS-07800509.text_edition: \n rcu s Marci filius Camilla Pisauro miles legionis VIIII\n
EDCS-07800509.translation: Marcus, son of Marcus, of the Camilla tribe, from Pisaurum, soldier of the Ninth Legion.
EDCS-07800509.summary: This epitaph records Marcus, a soldier of the Ninth Legion, originating from Pisaurum.

EDCS-07800510.text_edition: \n Marci Laetili Maximi cohorte I militis\n
EDCS-07800510.translation: Of Marcus Laetilius Maximus, soldier of the First Cohort.
EDCS-07800510.summary: This inscription fragment mentions Marcus Laetilius Maximus, a soldier of the First Cohort.

EDCS-07800511.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus nomini Sacri Brusci fili civis Senoni et Carssounae coniugis eius et Quinti fili Eirtig me let wircean and fios godian Criste to lofe and sancte Marie \xe2\x98\xa7\n
EDCS-07800511.translation: To the spirits of the departed, in the name of Sacrus, son of Bruscus, citizen of Senonia, and of Carssouna, his wife, and of Quintus, their son. Eirtig me let wircean and fios godian Criste to lofe and sancte Marie.
