EDCS-07800913.translation: Cingetissa placed this gift for the goddess Brigantia.
EDCS-07800913.summary: This is a votive inscription by Cingetissa for the goddess Brigantia.

EDCS-07800914.summary: This inscription is a fragmentary personal name, possibly a funerary marker.

EDCS-07800915.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Candiediniae Fortunatae pia vixit annos X\n
EDCS-07800915.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Underworld. Candiedinia Fortunata lived piously for 10 years.
EDCS-07800915.summary: A funerary dedication to Candiedinia Fortunata, who died at the age of ten, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800918.text_edition: \n Verbeiae sacrum Clodius Fronto praefectus cohortis II Lingonum\n
EDCS-07800918.translation: Sacred to Verbeia. Clodius Fronto, prefect of the Second Cohort of Lingones.
EDCS-07800918.summary: A dedication to the goddess Verbeia by Clodius Fronto, a prefect of a cohort of Lingones, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800919.text_edition: \n Pro salute imperatorum Caesarum Augustorum Antonini et Veri Iovi dilectorum Caecilius Lucanus praefectus cohortis\n
EDCS-07800919.translation: For the safety of our Emperors, Caesars, Augusti, Antoninus and Verus, beloved of Jupiter. Caecilius Lucanus, prefect of a cohort.
EDCS-07800919.summary: A dedication for the safety of Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Verus, made by Caecilius Lucanus, a cohort prefect, likely from the Antonine period.

EDCS-07800922.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Vedic riconis filia annorum XXX civis Cornovia hic sita est\n
EDCS-07800922.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Vedic rīconis, daughter of Vedic, aged 30, a citizen of Cornovia, is laid here.
EDCS-07800922.summary: A funerary dedication for Vedic rīconis, daughter of Vedic, a citizen of Cornovia, who died at the age of 30, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800923.text_edition: \n Deo Arciaconi et Numini Augusti Mat Vitalis ordinatus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800923.translation: To the god Arciaconus and the Divine Majesty of Augustus. Mat Vitalis, having been ordained, willingly paid his vow, deservedly.
EDCS-07800923.summary: A votive dedication to the god Arciaconus and the Emperor's divine spirit by Mat Vitalis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800926.text_edition: \n Britanniae Sanctae posuit Nikomedes Augustorum Nostrorum libertus\n
EDCS-07800926.translation: Nikomedes, freedman of our Augusti, dedicated this to Britannia Sancta.
EDCS-07800926.summary: A dedication to Britannia Sancta by Nikomedes, a freedman of the emperors, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800927.text_edition: \n Deae Fortunae Sosia Iuncina Quinti Antoni Isaurici legati Augusti\n
EDCS-07800927.translation: To the goddess Fortuna. Sosia Iuncina, wife of Quintus Antonius Isauricus, imperial legate.
EDCS-07800927.summary: A dedication to the goddess Fortuna by Sosia Iuncina, wife of the imperial legate Quintus Antonius Isauricus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800932.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dis Deabusque Hospitalibus Penatibusque ob conservatam salutem suam suorumque Publius Aelius Marcianus praefectus cohortis aram sacravit ANC dedit\n
EDCS-07800932.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, to the Gods and Goddesses of Hospitality, and to the Household Gods, for his own safety and that of his family, Publius Aelius Marcianus, prefect of a cohort, consecrated this altar and gave it.
EDCS-07800932.summary: A votive altar dedicated to Jupiter, household gods, and gods of hospitality by Publius Aelius Marcianus, a cohort prefect, for his family's safety, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800933.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Gaius Agrius Auspex votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07800933.translation: To the god Mars. Gaius Agrius Auspex willingly paid his vow, deservedly.
EDCS-07800933.summary: A votive dedication to the god Mars by Gaius Agrius Auspex, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800935.text_edition: \n Gaius Iulius Crescens Matribus Domesticis votum solvit merito libens\n
EDCS-07800935.translation: Gaius Iulius Crescens paid his vow to the Domestic Mothers, deservedly and willingly.
EDCS-07800935.summary: A votive dedication to the Matres Domesticae (Domestic Mothers) by Gaius Iulius Crescens, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800936.text_edition: \n Matribus Afris Italis Gallis Marcus Minucius Audens miles legionis VI victricis gubernator legionis VI votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07800936.translation: To the African, Italian, and Gallic Mothers, Marcus Minucius Audens, a soldier of the VI Victrix legion and steersman of the VI Victrix legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800936.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated by a soldier of the VI Victrix legion to various Matres (Mother Goddesses).

EDCS-07800937.text_edition: \n Matribus suis Marcus Rustius votum solvit laetus Massa libens merito\n
EDCS-07800937.translation: To his Mothers, Marcus Rustius gladly, Massi, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07800937.summary: This is a votive inscription by Marcus Rustius to his Mothers, likely a reference to local or ancestral deities.

EDCS-07800941.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Serapi templum a solo fecit Claudius Hieronymianus legatus legionis VI victricis\n
EDCS-07800941.translation: Claudius Hieronymianus, legate of the VI Victrix legion, built this temple to the holy god Serapis from the ground up.
EDCS-07800941.summary: This inscription records the construction of a temple to Serapis by Claudius Hieronymianus, a legate of the VI Victrix legion.

EDCS-07800942.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Silvano sacrum Lucius Celerinius Vitalis cornicularius legionis VIIII Hispanae votum solvit libens laetus merito et donum hoc donum adpertiniat cautum attiggam\n
EDCS-07800942.translation: To the holy god Silvanus, Lucius Celerinius Vitalis, a cornicularius of the IX Hispana legion, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow and this gift, let the gift bring what is intended, I touch it.
EDCS-07800942.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Silvanus by Lucius Celerinius Vitalis, an officer in the IX Hispana legion, recording a vow and a gift.

EDCS-07800943.translation: To the Old God, Primulus fulfilled his vow gladly and deservedly.
EDCS-07800943.summary: This is a votive inscription by Primulus to a 'Deus Vetus' (Old God).

EDCS-07800945.translation: To the gods of the governor's praetorium, Scribonius Demetrius.
EDCS-07800945.summary: This is a dedication by Scribonius Demetrius to the gods associated with the governor's praetorium.

EDCS-07800961.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelio Supero centurioni legionis VI qui vixit annis XXXVIII mensibus IIII diebus XIII Aurelia Censorina coniunx memoriam possuit sic\n
EDCS-07800961.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Censorina erected this memorial to her husband Aurelius Superus, a centurion of the VI legion, who lived 38 years, 4 months, and 13 days.
EDCS-07800961.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Aurelius Superus, a centurion of the VI legion, erected by his wife Aurelia Censorina.

EDCS-07800962.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Bebius Augusta Crescens Vindelicum miles legionis VI victricis piae fidelis annorum XLIII stipendiorum XXIII heres amico faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800962.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Bebius Augusta Crescens Vindelicum, a loyal and dutiful soldier of the VI Victrix legion, who lived 43 years and served 23 years, his heir arranged for this to be made for his friend.
EDCS-07800962.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Bebius Augusta Crescens Vindelicum, a soldier of the VI Victrix legion, erected by his heir.

EDCS-07800963.text_edition: \n Memoriae o Bassaei Iuli et Felicis fili sui dulcissimi\n
EDCS-07800963.translation: To the memory of Bassaeus Iulus and Felix, his dearest son.
EDCS-07800963.summary: This inscription is a memorial to Bassaeus Iulus and his son Felix.

EDCS-07800964.text_edition: \n Lucius Duccius Luci Voltinia Rufinus Vienna signifer legionis VIIII annorum XXIIX hic situs est\n
EDCS-07800964.translation: Lucius Duccius Rufinus, son of Lucius, of the Voltinian tribe, from Vienna, a standard-bearer of the IX legion, 28 years old, is buried here.
EDCS-07800964.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Duccius Rufinus, a 28-year-old standard-bearer of the IX legion.

EDCS-07800965.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavi Bellatoris decurionis coloniae Eboracensis vixit annis XXVIIII mensibus III II \n
EDCS-07800965.translation: To the spirits of the departed, of Flavius Bellatoris, decurion of the colony of Eboracum, who lived 29 years, 3 months, and 2 days.
EDCS-07800965.summary: This is a tombstone recording the death of Flavius Bellatoris, a decurion in the colony of Eboracum.

EDCS-07800966.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titi Flavi Flavini |(centurionis) legionis VI Caius Classicius Aprilis heres priusquam obiret fieri iussit\n
EDCS-07800966.translation: To the spirits of the departed, of Titus Flavius Flavini, centurion of the VI Legion. Caius Classicius Aprilis, his heir, ordered this to be made before his own death.
EDCS-07800966.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Titus Flavius Flavini, a centurion of the VI Legion, with his heir, Caius Classicius Aprilis, arranging for its creation.

EDCS-07800967.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a funerary dedication to Julia Secunda Vocc.

EDCS-07800968.text_edition: \n Memoriae Valeri Theodoriani Nomentani vixit annos XXXV menses VI Emilia Theodora mater heres curavit\n
EDCS-07800968.translation: To the memory of Valerius Theodorianus Nomentanus, who lived 35 years and 6 months. Emilia Theodora, his mother and heir, took care of this.
EDCS-07800968.summary: This tombstone records the death of Valerius Theodorianus Nomentanus, with his mother and heir, Emilia Theodora, responsible for its erection.

EDCS-07800969.text_edition: \n Marcus Verecundius Diogenes sevir coloniae Eboracensis idemque Morinorum et cives Biturix Cubus haec sibi vivus fecit\n
EDCS-07800969.translation: Marcus Verecundius Diogenes, a seviral official of the colony of Eboracum, and also of the Morini, and a citizen of Biturix Cubus, made these for himself while alive.
EDCS-07800969.summary: This self-made monument records Marcus Verecundius Diogenes, a civic official from Eboracum and other regions, who erected it during his lifetime.

EDCS-07800970.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus lius filius Cresces a veteranus legionis VI victricis Ca Pri et V\n
EDCS-07800970.translation: To the spirits of the departed, of Ilius son Cresces, a veteran of the VI Victrix Legion. Ca Pri and V...
EDCS-07800970.summary: This is a damaged funerary inscription dedicated to Ilius Cresces, a veteran of the VI Victrix Legion.

EDCS-07800972.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Hylus, described as a 'dearest pupil'.

EDCS-07800973.translation: To Aelia Aeliana, who lived [no details].
EDCS-07800973.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely a funerary dedication to Aelia Aeliana.

EDCS-07800974.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aelie Severe honeste femine coniugi Caecili Rufi quondam vixit annos XXVII menses VIIII dies IIII Caecilius Musicus libertus eius posuit\n
EDCS-07800974.translation: To the spirits of the departed, of Aelia Severa, an honorable wife of Caecilius Rufus. She lived 27 years, 9 months, and 4 days. Caecilius Musicus, her freedman, placed this.
EDCS-07800974.summary: This tombstone commemorates Aelia Severa, wife of Caecilius Rufus, with the inscription erected by her freedman, Caecilius Musicus.

EDCS-07800975.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Corellia Optata annorum XIII Secreti Manes qui regna Acherusia Ditis incolitis quos parva petunt post lumina vite exiguus cinis et simulacrum corporis umbra insontis gnate genitor spe captus iniqua supremum hunc nate miserandus defleo finem Quintus Corellius Fortis pater faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800975.translation: To the spirits of the departed, of Corellia Optata, aged 13. O spirits of the underworld who inhabit the Acherusian realm of Pluto, whom the little ones seek, after the brief light of life, this meager ash and the shadowy image of an innocent body. Your father, filled with unjust hope, mourns this wretched end for you, my son. Quintus Corellius Fortis, the father, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-07800975.summary: This funerary epitaph, written by a grieving father Quintus Corellius Fortis, commemorates his daughter Corellia Optata, who died at the age of 13.

EDCS-07800976.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flaviae Augustinae vixit annos XXXVIIII menses VII dies XI filius Saenius Augustinus vixit annum I dies III a vixit annum I menses VIIII dies V Gaius Aeresius Saenus veteranus legionis VI victricis coniugi carissimae et sibi faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800976.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavia Augustina lived 39 years, 7 months, 11 days. Saenius Augustinus lived 1 year, 3 days. A veteran of the VI Victrix legion, Gaius Aeresius Saenus, had this made for his dearest wife and himself.
EDCS-07800976.summary: A funerary monument erected by Gaius Aeresius Saenus for his wife Flavia Augustina and himself, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800977.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulie Brice annorum XXXI Sepronie Martine annorum VI Sepronius Martinus faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800977.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Brice, 31 years old. Sepronia Martina, 6 years old. Sepronius Martinus had this made.
EDCS-07800977.summary: A funerary inscription honoring Iulia Brice and Sepronia Martina, likely commissioned by Sepronius Martinus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800978.text_edition: \n Iuliae Fortunate domo Sardinia Verecundio Diogeni fida coniuncta marito\n
EDCS-07800978.translation: To Iulia Fortunata, from Sardinia. To Verecundius Diogenes, her faithful partner in marriage.
EDCS-07800978.summary: A dedication to Iulia Fortunata by her husband Verecundius Diogenes, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800979.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulie Velve pientissime vixit annos L Aurelius Mercurialis heres faciundum curavit vivus sibi et suis fecit\n
EDCS-07800979.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Iulia Velve, most pious, lived 50 years. Aurelius Mercurialis, her heir, had this made. He made this monument while alive for himself and his family.
EDCS-07800979.summary: A funerary inscription for Iulia Velve, commissioned by her heir Aurelius Mercurialis who also provided for himself and his family, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800980.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mantiniae Maericae et Candidae eius matri Baritae Marcus Aurinius Simnus titulum posuit\n
EDCS-07800980.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Mantinia Maerica and her mother Candida. Marcus Aurinius Simnus placed this monument.
EDCS-07800980.summary: A funerary dedication by Marcus Aurinius Simnus for his mother Candida and his wife Mantinia Maerica, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800981.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Simpliciae Florentinae animae innocentissimae quae vixit menses decem Felicius Simplex pater fecit |(centurio) legionis VI victricis\n
EDCS-07800981.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Simplicia Florentina, most innocent soul, who lived ten months. Felicius Simplex, father, and a centurion of the VI Victrix legion, made this.
EDCS-07800981.summary: A funerary inscription for Simplicia Florentina, commissioned by her father Felicius Simplex, a centurion in the VI Victrix legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800982.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ulpiae Felicissimae quae vixit annis XXIII menses XI dies posuerunt Ulpius Felix et Andronica parentes\n
EDCS-07800982.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Ulpia Felicissima, who lived 23 years, 11 months, [and some] days. Ulpius Felix and Andronica, her parents, placed this.
EDCS-07800982.summary: A funerary monument erected by Ulpius Felix and Andronica for their daughter Ulpia Felicissima, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800983.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Decimina, daughter of Decimus.
EDCS-07800983.summary: A funerary dedication to Decimina, daughter of Decimus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800984.text_edition: \n coniugi piissimae fecit coniux sit tibi terra levis\n
EDCS-07800984.translation: For his most pious wife, the husband made this. May the earth lie lightly on you.
EDCS-07800984.summary: A funerary dedication by a husband for his pious wife, possibly part of a larger inscription, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800985.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. For Minna.
EDCS-07800985.summary: A funerary dedication to Minna, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07800986.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Eglectae annorum XXX hic sitae secus Crescentem filium annorum III Antonius Sthepanus coniugi faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07800986.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Eglecta, aged 30, buried here beside Crescentes, her son, aged 3. Antonius Stephanus had this made for his wife.
EDCS-07800986.summary: This epitaph records the burial of Eglecta and her young son Crescentes, with the monument erected by her husband, Antonius Stephanus.

EDCS-07800987.text_edition: \n filio vixit annos XIII Vitellia Procula mater pro parte eres Ave Maria\n
EDCS-07800987.translation: He lived 13 years. Vitellia Procula, his mother, [provided] part of the inheritance. Hail Mary.
EDCS-07800987.summary: This epitaph records the short life of a son, with his mother Vitellia Procula contributing to the burial arrangements, and includes a Christian invocation.

EDCS-07800989.text_edition: \n go mi primi pili sub cura ri et Marci Coss\n
EDCS-07800989.translation: To me, the primipilus, under the supervision of Rius and Marcus, consuls.
EDCS-07800989.summary: This inscription likely records a military honor or appointment for a primipilus, mentioning the consuls Rius and Marcus.

EDCS-07800998.inscription_type: tituli honorarii, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-07800998.text_edition: \n Ob honorem domus divinae imperatoris Caesaris Titi Aeli Hadriani Antonini Augusti Pii patris patriae consulis II et Numinibus Augustorum Marcus Ulpius Ianuarius aedilis vici Petuariensis proscaenium de suo dedit\n
EDCS-07800998.translation: For the honor of the divine house of the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, consul for the second time, and the Numinibuses of the Augusts, Marcus Ulpius Ianuarius, aedile of the Petuariensis district, dedicated the stage setting at his own expense.
EDCS-07800998.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a stage setting by Marcus Ulpius Ianuarius, an aedile, in honor of Emperor Antoninus Pius and the imperial family, likely dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-07801000.text_edition: \n Aur vixit et An no annos I \n
EDCS-07801000.translation: Aur [name] lived and An [name] [one year old].
EDCS-07801000.summary: This fragmented inscription likely records the ages of two individuals, possibly children, named Aur- and An-.

EDCS-07801001.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Felicule coniugi karissime Gaius M P faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07801001.translation: To the spirits of the departed. To Felicula, dearest wife. Gaius M P had this made.
EDCS-07801001.summary: This epitaph records the death of Felicula, dedicated to her by her husband Gaius M P.

EDCS-07801002.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Rigae Scirus dicavit sacrum votum solvens libens merito\n
EDCS-07801002.translation: To the god Mars Rigaeus. Scirus dedicated this sacred vow, gladly and deservedly fulfilling it.
EDCS-07801002.summary: This dedication records a vow made to the god Mars Rigaeus by Scirus.

EDCS-07801005.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Macrinus ex equite singulari vixit\n
EDCS-07801005.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Aurelius Macrinus, formerly an equestrian soldier, lived [duration].
EDCS-07801005.summary: This epitaph records the death of Aurelius Macrinus, who served as an equestrian soldier.

EDCS-07801011.text_edition: \n Titia Pinta vixit nnos XXXVIII et Valerio Adiutori vixit nnos XX et Varialo vixit nnos XV Valerius Vindicianus coniugi et filiis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07801011.translation: Titia Pinta lived 38 years, and Valerius Adiutor lived 20 years, and Variolus lived 15 years. Valerius Vindicianus had this made for his wife and sons.
EDCS-07801011.summary: This epitaph records the deaths of Titia Pinta, Valerius Adiutor, and Varialus, with the monument erected by Valerius Vindicianus for his family.

EDCS-07801012.text_edition: \n Iustinianus praepositus Vindicianus magister turrem castrum fecit a solo\n
EDCS-07801012.translation: Iustinianus, the commander, Vindicianus, the master, built the tower-fortress from the ground up.
EDCS-07801012.summary: This inscription records the construction of a tower-fortress by Iustinianus and Vindicianus.

EDCS-07801016.text_edition: \n Deo qui vias et semitas commentus est Titus Aurelius Dasso fecit votum libens laetus merito Quintus Varius Vitalis beneficiarius consularis aram sacram restituit Aproniano et Bradua consulibus\n
EDCS-07801016.translation: To the god who devised roads and paths, Titus Aurelius Dasso made a vow willingly, happily, and deservedly. Quintus Varius Vitalis, a consular beneficiarius, restored this sacred altar during the consulship of Apronianus and Bradua.
EDCS-07801016.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a sacred altar to a god of roads, dedicated by Quintus Varius Vitalis, a consular beneficiarius, during the consulship of Apronianus and Bradua.

EDCS-07801017.text_edition: \n Deae Suriae aram Gaius N O beneficiarius\n
EDCS-07801017.translation: To the goddess Syria, Gaius N O, beneficiarius.
EDCS-07801017.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddess Syria by Gaius N O, a beneficiarius.

EDCS-07801018.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Vheteri pro salute Aureli Muciani votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801018.translation: To the holy god Vheteri, he fulfilled his vow willingly, deservedly, and happily for the health of Aurelius Mucianus.
EDCS-07801018.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to the god Vheteri for the health of Aurelius Mucianus.

EDCS-07801021.text_edition: \n Deae Fortunae Virius Lupus legatus Augusti propraetore balineum vi ignis exustum cohorti I Thracum restituit curante Valerio Frontone praefecto equitum alae Vettonum\n
EDCS-07801021.translation: To the goddess Fortune, Virius Lupus, legate of the emperor with propraetorian authority, restored the bathhouse, which had been destroyed by fire, to the First Cohort of Thracians, under the care of Valerius Fronto, prefect of the cavalry of the Vettonian Ala.
EDCS-07801021.summary: This inscription records the restoration of a bathhouse to the First Cohort of Thracians, which had been destroyed by fire, undertaken by Virius Lupus, a legate, and supervised by Valerius Fronto, a cavalry prefect.

EDCS-07801022.text_edition: \n Marti Condati Arponatus votum solvit libens merito Marti \n
EDCS-07801022.translation: To Condatis, Arponatus fulfilled his vow willingly, deservedly, and happily. To Mars.
EDCS-07801022.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfillment to the god Condatis by Arponatus.

EDCS-07801023.text_edition: \n Deo Vinotono Silvano Iulius Secundus |(centurio) cohortis I Thracum votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07801023.translation: To the god Vinotonus Silvanus, Julius Secundus, centurion of the First Cohort of Thracians, fulfilled his vow willingly, happily, and deservedly.
EDCS-07801023.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfillment to the god Vinotonus Silvanus by Julius Secundus, a centurion of the First Cohort of Thracians.

EDCS-07801024.text_edition: \n Deo Vinotono Lucius Caesius Frontinus praefectus cohortis I Thracum domo Parma votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07801024.translation: To the god Vinotonus, Lucius Caesius Frontinus, prefect of the First Cohort of Thracians, from Parma, fulfilled his vow willingly, happily, and deservedly.
EDCS-07801024.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfillment to the god Vinotonus by Lucius Caesius Frontinus, prefect of the First Cohort of Thracians, from Parma.

EDCS-07801031.text_edition: \n Impperatoribus Caessaribus Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Arabico Adiabenico Parthico maximo et Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Auggustis [et Publio Septimio Getae nobilissimo Caesari] iussu Luci Alfeni Senecionis legati Auggustorum pro praetore cohors I Thracum equitata\n
EDCS-07801031.translation: To the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax Arabicus Adiabenicus Parthicus Maximus and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus, and to the most noble Caesar Publius Septimius Geta, by order of Lucius Alfenius Senecio, legate of the emperors with pro-praetorian authority, the First Cohort of Thracians, equipped with cavalry.
EDCS-07801031.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Septimius Severus, Caracalla, and Geta, commissioned by their legate Lucius Alfenius Senecio, on behalf of the First Cohort of Thracians equitata.

EDCS-07801033.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Enemnogenus aram posuit votum solvit pro se et suis merito\n
EDCS-07801033.translation: To the god Martis Enemnogenus, he placed an altar and fulfilled his vow for himself and his family, deservedly.
EDCS-07801033.summary: This inscription records the placement of an altar and the fulfillment of a vow to the god Martis Enemnogenus by an unnamed individual for themselves and their family.

EDCS-07801035.text_edition: \n Deae Nymphae NEINE Brica et Ianuaria filia libentes ex voto solverunt\n
EDCS-07801035.translation: To the goddess Nympha, Brica and her daughter Ianuaria willingly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-07801035.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfillment to the goddess Nympha by Brica and her daughter Ianuaria.

EDCS-07801036.text_edition: \n ONI RS SEI ellinus beneficiarius consularis provinciae superioris votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07801036.translation: Oni RS, as a beneficiary of the consular governor of the upper province, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07801036.summary: This inscription records a military man named Oni RS fulfilling a vow to an unnamed deity, likely dating to the Roman period in a provincial setting.

EDCS-07801037.text_edition: \n Imperatoribus Caesaribus Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci et Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Augustis et Publio Septimio Getae nobilissimo Caesari sub cura Luci Alfeni Senecionis legati eorum pro praetore\n
EDCS-07801037.translation: Under the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Augustus and the most noble Caesar Publius Septimius Geta, under the supervision of Lucius Alfenius Senecio, their governor.
EDCS-07801037.summary: This text names the reigning emperors and a governor, likely serving as the preamble to a decree or official inscription from the Severan period.

EDCS-07801038.text_edition: \n IING POSI HOEVMOLLINI vetustate dilabsum sub cura centuria legionis VI victricis Postumius Urbanus Britanniae Superioris citra EM \n
EDCS-07801038.translation: The [structure] which had collapsed due to age was restored under the supervision of a century of the VI Victrix Legion, by Postumius Urbanus of Britannia Superior, beyond EM.
EDCS-07801038.summary: This inscription details the restoration of a damaged structure by Postumius Urbanus, acting on behalf of a century of the VI Victrix Legion in Britannia Superior, probably dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-07801039.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Romulianus emeritus ex decurione vixit annis LX\n
EDCS-07801039.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius Romulianus, a retired decurion, lived for 60 years.
EDCS-07801039.summary: This is a tomb inscription recording the death of Aurelius Romulianus, a retired decurion, and is likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801040.text_edition: \n --- VICI --- Aureliae Romana et Sabina filiae patri pientissimo et rarissimo faciendum curaverunt MRI CARINIMORRFMPIA\n
EDCS-07801040.translation: Aurelia Romana and Sabina [erected this] for their most devoted and rare father. MRI CARINIMORRFMPIA.
EDCS-07801040.summary: This funerary inscription records the erection of a monument by Aurelia Romana and Sabina for their father, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801041.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Salvia Donata vixsit annos VIII mensem I\n
EDCS-07801041.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Salvia Donata lived for 8 years and 1 month.
EDCS-07801041.summary: This is a tomb inscription recording the short life of Salvia Donata, likely a child, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801042.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Maternus lived.
EDCS-07801042.summary: This is a fragmented tomb inscription recording the existence or death of Maternus, from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801045.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Publius Aelius Publi filius Sergia Bassus Mursa quondam |(centurio) legionis XX Valeriae victricis vixit annos S et Privatus libberti et heredes per Aelium Surinum |(centurionem) legionis VI victricis faciendum ccuraverunt si quis in hoc sepulcrum alium mortuum intulerit inferat fisco ddominorum nnostrorum HS instante Aelio Surino \n
EDCS-07801045.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed. Publius Aelius Bassus Mursa, son of Publius, of the Sergia tribe, formerly a centurion of the XX Valeria Victrix Legion, lived for [age missing] years. And Privatus, his freedman and heir, had this made through Aelius Surinus, a centurion of the VI Victrix Legion. If anyone brings or places another dead person in this tomb, they will pay 1000 sesterces to the treasury of our lords, at the request of Aelius Surinus.
EDCS-07801045.summary: This complex tomb inscription records the death of a centurion, Publius Aelius Bassus Mursa, and the arrangements made by his freedman and heir, stipulating a penalty for unauthorized burials, and it mentions two legions, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801049.translation: A traveler seeing Hermas of Commagene, 16 years old, struck by fate in his tomb, spoke these words: 'Rejoice, you child, this epitaph is from me. Even if you hasten swiftly through mortal life, for you were quickly taken from among mortals to the land of the Cimmerians, do not lie. For the child was good, and you will give [something good]'.
EDCS-07801049.summary: This Greek epitaph laments the premature death of a 16-year-old boy named Hermas from Commagene, praising his goodness and likely dating to the Roman period in the Eastern Empire.

EDCS-07801050.text_edition: \n Deo Belatucadro libens votum fecit iolus\n
EDCS-07801050.translation: Iolus gladly made his vow to the god Belatucadrus.
EDCS-07801050.summary: This inscription records a personal vow made by Iolus to the god Belatucadrus, likely found in Roman Britain.

EDCS-07801051.text_edition: \n Fortunae Servatrici Antonia Stratonis ex viso\n
EDCS-07801051.translation: To Fortuna the Preserver, from Antonia Stratonis, according to a vision.
EDCS-07801051.summary: This is a votive dedication to the goddess Fortuna by Antonia Stratonis, likely made in fulfillment of a vow prompted by a vision.

EDCS-07801053.translation: To Jupiter Serapis, Lucius Alfenus Pal(a)...
EDCS-07801053.summary: This is a dedication, likely votive, to Jupiter Serapis by Lucius Alfenus Pal(a)..., possibly a funerary or religious monument.

EDCS-07801055.text_edition: \n Fortunae Balneari N M S S Gaius Caledius Florentinus NOIV\n
EDCS-07801055.translation: To Fortuna of the Baths, N. M. S. S., Gaius Caledius Florentinus NOIV...
EDCS-07801055.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Fortuna Balnearis by Gaius Caledius Florentinus, possibly related to a bathhouse and the abbreviations suggest a formulaic or ritualistic context.

EDCS-07801056.text_edition: \n Felicitati equitum L Aurelius Marcus decurio alae votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07801056.translation: To Felicitas, Lucius Aurelius Marcus, decurion of the cavalry squadron, willingly, happily, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07801056.summary: This is a votive inscription by Lucius Aurelius Marcus, a decurion of a cavalry ala, dedicating to Felicitas (good fortune) and fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-07801059.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead, for Gaius Attonius.
EDCS-07801059.summary: This is a funerary dedication to the Dis Manes (Spirits of the Dead) in memory of Gaius Attonius.

EDCS-07801060.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription likely identifying a person as the daughter of Crescentius, who held the rank of imaginifer (standard-bearer).

EDCS-07801065.text_edition: \n Deo Blatucairo Audagus votum solvit pro sua salute\n
EDCS-07801065.translation: To the god Blatucairus, Audagus fulfilled his vow for his own health.
EDCS-07801065.summary: This is a votive inscription by Audagus to the god Blatucairus, offering a vow for his own well-being.

EDCS-07801066.text_edition: \n Deo Belatucadro Iulianus aram voto solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801066.translation: To the god Belatucadrus, Iulianus fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-07801066.summary: This is a votive inscription by Iulianus to the god Belatucadrus, fulfilling a vow with willingness and merit.

EDCS-07801071.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Ianuarius numeri equitum Stratonicianorum votum merito pro se et souis\n
EDCS-07801071.translation: To the god Mars, Ianuarius of the cavalry unit of the Stratoniciani fulfilled his vow deservedly for himself and his family.
EDCS-07801071.summary: This is a votive inscription by Ianuarius, a member of the Stratonician cavalry, to the god Mars, dedicating a vow for himself and his relatives.

EDCS-07801073.text_edition: \n ri Gallorum tribunus militum legionis VIII Augustae\n
EDCS-07801073.translation: (tribune) of the Gallorum cavalry squadron, tribune of soldiers of the Legion VIII Augusta.
EDCS-07801073.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely refers to a military officer who served as a tribune in the Gallorum cavalry and subsequently as a military tribune in the Legio VIII Augusta.

EDCS-07801075.text_edition: \n Annamoris pater et Ressona mater faciendum curaverunt\n
EDCS-07801075.translation: Annamoris' father and Ressona his mother saw to its construction.
EDCS-07801075.summary: This is a funerary dedication by parents for their child, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-07801076.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Crescentinus vixit annis XVIII Vidaris pater posuit\n
EDCS-07801076.translation: To the Divine Shades. Crescentinus lived 18 years. Vidaris his father placed this monument.
EDCS-07801076.summary: This is a funerary epitaph commemorating a young man named Crescentinus, erected by his father.

EDCS-07801077.text_edition: \n Plumae Lunaris titulo posuit coniugi carissimae\n
EDCS-07801077.translation: Plumae placed this inscription for Lunar, his dearest wife.
EDCS-07801077.summary: This is a funerary dedication made by a husband for his wife.

EDCS-07801078.text_edition: \n Dis C Manibus Tittus M vixit annis plus minus XXXII M frater titulum posuit\n
EDCS-07801078.translation: To the Divine Shades. Tittus M lived about 32 years. His brother M placed this inscription.
EDCS-07801078.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Tittus M, erected by his brother.

EDCS-07801083.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Genio loci Suubrius Apollinaris princeps cohortis I V\n
EDCS-07801083.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to the Genius of the place, Suubrius Apollinaris, princeps of the First Cohort of Volunteers.
EDCS-07801083.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter and the local genius by a military officer, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-07801084.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription that appears to record a name, possibly a funerary dedication or a military reference.

EDCS-07801087.text_edition: \n Dibus Herculi et Silvano fecit Primus custos armorum pro se et vexillatione votum solvens libens merito\n
EDCS-07801087.translation: To Hercules and Silvanus, Primus, custos armorum, fulfilling his vow willingly and deservedly for himself and the detachment.
EDCS-07801087.summary: This is a votive offering to Hercules and Silvanus by a military official responsible for arms, likely from the Roman army.

EDCS-07801088.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors II Tracum equitata cui praeest Manilius Nepos praefectus\n
EDCS-07801088.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Second Cohort of Thracian Horsemen, commanded by Gaius Manilius Nepos, prefect.
EDCS-07801088.summary: This records a dedication by the Second Cohort of Thracian Horsemen under their prefect, likely from the Imperial period.

EDCS-07801089.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano cohors II Lingonum cui praeest Gaius Pompeius Marci filius Saturninus\n
EDCS-07801089.translation: To the god Silvanus, the Second Cohort of Lingones, commanded by Gaius Pompeius Marci son Saturninus.
EDCS-07801089.summary: This records a dedication to Silvanus by the Second Cohort of Lingones under their commander, likely from the Roman army.

EDCS-07801091.text_edition: \n curante Valerio Luperco praefecto cohortis II Lingonum\n
EDCS-07801091.translation: under the supervision of Valerius Lupercus, prefect of the Second Cohort of Lingones.
EDCS-07801091.summary: This is a fragment mentioning a military official and his command, likely part of a larger inscription.

EDCS-07801095.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Smertrio Macri filio militi cohortis II Thracum equitatae stipendiorum X uicsit sic annos XXXV qies sic V\n
EDCS-07801095.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. To Smertrius, son of Macer, a soldier of the II Cohort of Thracians, equipped with cavalry, with 10 years of service, who lived 35 years and rested 5.
EDCS-07801095.summary: A funerary inscription for a soldier of the II Cohort of Thracians, dating likely to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801096.summary: A very fragmentary inscription, possibly recording a funerary dedication or an honorific text for Lucius Severinus.

EDCS-07801097.text_edition: \n D COII et filis eorum Aurelius et Secundus MISC \n
EDCS-07801097.translation: To the Divine [Emperor] and his sons. Aurelius and Secundus MISC.
EDCS-07801097.summary: A dedication to the divine emperor and his sons, made by Aurelius and Secundus, likely dating to the Severan period or later.

EDCS-07801100.text_edition: \n Belatucadro Iulius Civilis optio votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801100.translation: To Belatucadrus, Julius Civilis, optio, willingly fulfilled his vow deservedly.
EDCS-07801100.summary: A votive inscription to the god Belatucadrus by Julius Civilis, an optio, likely from Roman Britain.

EDCS-07801101.text_edition: \n Dis deabusque Paulus Postumius Acilianus praefectus cohortis I Delmatarum\n
EDCS-07801101.translation: To the gods and goddesses, Paulus Postumius Acilianus, prefect of the I Cohort of Dalmatians.
EDCS-07801101.summary: A dedication to the gods by Paulus Postumius Acilianus, prefect of the I Cohort of Dalmatians, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801103.text_edition: \n Genio loci Fortunae Reduci Romae Aeternae et Fato Bono Gaius Cornelius Peregrinus tribunus cohortis ex provincia Mauretania Caesariensi domo Saldis decurio votum solvit laetus libens merito Volanti vivas\n
EDCS-07801103.translation: To the Genius of the place, to Fortuna Redux, to Eternal Rome, and to Good Fate, Gaius Cornelius Peregrinus, tribune of a cohort from the province of Mauretania Caesariensis, from the town of Saldis, a decurion, happily and willingly fulfilled his vow deservedly to Volans, may you live.
EDCS-07801103.summary: A complex votive inscription by Gaius Cornelius Peregrinus, a decurion and former tribune, dedicated to various deities and abstract concepts, likely from the late 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801104.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo iana Quinti filia Hermione\n
EDCS-07801104.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Iana, daughter of Quintus, Hermione.
EDCS-07801104.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Iana Hermione, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801105.text_edition: \n Iovi Augusto Marcus Censorius Marci filius Voltinia Cornelianus legionis X Fretensis praepositus cohortis I Hispanorum ex provincia Narbonensi domo Nemauso votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801105.translation: To Jupiter Augustus, Marcus Censorius, son of Marcus, of the Voltinia tribe, Cornelianus, of the X Legion Fretensis, commander of the I Cohort of Spaniards, from the province of Narbonensis, from the town of Nemausus, willingly fulfilled his vow deservedly.
EDCS-07801105.summary: A votive inscription by Marcus Censorius Cornelianus, commander of the I Cohort of Spaniards, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801107.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Hispanorum equitata cui praeest Lucius Antistius Luci filius Quirina Lupus Verianus praefectus domu Sicca ex Africa\n
EDCS-07801107.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the I Cohort of Spaniards, equipped with cavalry, commanded by Lucius Antistius Lupus Verianus, son of Lucius, of the Quirina tribe, prefect, from the town of Sicca in Africa.
EDCS-07801107.summary: An inscription recording the dedication by the I Cohort of Spaniards under its prefect, Lucius Antistius Lupus Verianus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801108.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Hispanorum cui praeest Gaius Caballius Priscus tribunus\n
EDCS-07801108.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the I Cohort of Spaniards, commanded by Gaius Caballius Priscus, tribune.
EDCS-07801108.summary: An inscription recording the dedication by the I Cohort of Spaniards under its tribune, Gaius Caballius Priscus, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801109.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Gaius Caballius Priscus tribunus\n
EDCS-07801109.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Caballius Priscus, tribune.
EDCS-07801109.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, likely on a military monument, by a tribune named Gaius Caballius Priscus.

EDCS-07801110.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Gaius Caballius Priscus tribunus\n
EDCS-07801110.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Caballius Priscus, tribune.
EDCS-07801110.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, likely on a military monument, by a tribune named Gaius Caballius Priscus.

EDCS-07801111.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Gaius Caballius Priscus tribunus \n
EDCS-07801111.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Gaius Caballius Priscus, tribune.
EDCS-07801111.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, likely on a military monument, by a tribune named Gaius Caballius Priscus.

EDCS-07801112.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Hispanorum equitata cui praeest Publius Cornelius Publi filius Galeria Ur praefectus F P ROMAN votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801112.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the first cohort of Spaniards, cavalry, commanded by Publius Cornelius, son of Publius, of the Galerian tribe, prefect, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly for the sake of Rome.
EDCS-07801112.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter by the prefect of the first cohort of Spanish cavalry, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-07801113.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Hispanorum cui praeest Helstrius Novellus praefectus\n
EDCS-07801113.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the first cohort of Spaniards, commanded by Helstrius Novellus, prefect.
EDCS-07801113.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter by the prefect of the first cohort of Spaniards.

EDCS-07801114.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Hispanorum cui praeest Marcus Maenius Agrippa tribunus posuit\n
EDCS-07801114.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the first cohort of Spaniards, commanded by Marcus Maenius Agrippa, tribune, erected this.
EDCS-07801114.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument to Jupiter by Marcus Maenius Agrippa, tribune of the first cohort of Spaniards.

EDCS-07801115.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et numini Augusti Marcus Maenius Agrippa tribunus posuit\n
EDCS-07801115.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the divine will of Augustus, Marcus Maenius Agrippa, tribune, erected this.
EDCS-07801115.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument to Jupiter and the divine will of Augustus by Marcus Maenius Agrippa, tribune.

EDCS-07801116.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo et numini Augusti Maenius Agrippa tribunus posuit\n
EDCS-07801116.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest and to the divine will of Augustus, Maenius Agrippa, tribune, erected this.
EDCS-07801116.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument to Jupiter and the divine will of Augustus by Maenius Agrippa, tribune.

EDCS-07801118.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Cammius Maximus praefectus cohortis I Hispanorum equitatae et tribunus XVIII cohortis Voluntariorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801118.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucius Cammius Maximus, prefect of the first cohort of Spanish cavalry and tribune of the eighteenth cohort of volunteers, fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-07801118.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter by Lucius Cammius Maximus, who held multiple military commands.

EDCS-07801119.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Cammius Maximus praefectus cohortis I Hispanorum equitatae votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07801119.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucius Cammius Maximus, prefect of the first cohort of Spanish cavalry, fulfilled his vow willingly, gladly, and deservedly.
EDCS-07801119.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Jupiter by Lucius Cammius Maximus, prefect of the first cohort of Spanish cavalry.

EDCS-07801120.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Lucius Cammius Maximus praefectus cohortis I Hispanorum equitatae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801120.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Lucius Cammius Maximus, prefect of the First Cohort of Batavians (cavalry), gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07801120.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Lucius Cammius Maximus, prefect of the First Cohort of Batavians, to Jupiter Best and Greatest, likely dating to the period when auxiliary cohorts were first granted cavalry status.

EDCS-07801121.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Baetasiorum civium Romanorum cui praeest Titus Attius Tutor praefectus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07801121.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the First Cohort of Batavians, Roman citizens, commanded by its prefect Titus Attius Tutor, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled its vow.
EDCS-07801121.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by the First Cohort of Batavians, Roman citizens, under their prefect Titus Attius Tutor, to Jupiter Best and Greatest.

EDCS-07801122.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Dalmatarum cui praeest Lucius Caecilius Vegetus praefectus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801122.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the First Cohort of Dalmatians, commanded by its prefect Lucius Caecilius Vegetus, gladly and deservedly fulfilled its vow.
EDCS-07801122.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by the First Cohort of Dalmatians, under their prefect Lucius Caecilius Vegetus, to Jupiter Best and Greatest.

EDCS-07801123.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Capitolino pro salute Antonini Augusti Pii Postumius Acilianus praefectus cohortis I Delmatarum\n
EDCS-07801123.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Capitoline deity, for the health of Antoninus Pius Augustus, Postumius Acilianus, prefect of the First Cohort of Dalmatians.
EDCS-07801123.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Postumius Acilianus, prefect of the First Cohort of Dalmatians, to Jupiter Best and Greatest and the Capitoline deity for the well-being of Emperor Antoninus Pius.

EDCS-07801124.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Acilianus praefectus P\n
EDCS-07801124.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Acilianus, prefect.
EDCS-07801124.summary: This inscription records a dedication by Acilianus, a prefect, to Jupiter Best and Greatest.

EDCS-07801128.text_edition: \n Marti Militari cohors I Baetasiorum civium Romanorum cui praeest Titus Attius Tutor praefectus votum solvit laeta libens merito\n
EDCS-07801128.translation: To Mars the Soldier, the First Cohort of Batavians, Roman citizens, commanded by its prefect Titus Attius Tutor, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled its vow.
EDCS-07801128.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by the First Cohort of Batavians, Roman citizens, under their prefect Titus Attius Tutor, to Mars the Soldier.

EDCS-07801129.text_edition: \n Marti Militari cohors I Baetasiorum civium Romanorum cui praeest Ulpius Titianus praefectus votum solvit laeta libens merito\n
EDCS-07801129.translation: To Mars the Soldier, the First Cohort of Batavians, Roman citizens, commanded by its prefect Ulpius Titianus, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled its vow.
EDCS-07801129.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by the First Cohort of Batavians, Roman citizens, under their prefect Ulpius Titianus, to Mars the Soldier.

EDCS-07801130.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Neptune by Lucius Cassius.

EDCS-07801132.text_edition: \n Deae Setloceniae Labareus Germanus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801132.translation: To the goddess Setlocenia, Labareus Germanus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07801132.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Labareus Germanus to the goddess Setlocenia.

EDCS-07801133.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae cohors I Baetasiorum civium Romanorum cui praeest Titus Attius Tutor praefectus votum solvit laeta libens merito\n
EDCS-07801133.translation: To Victory Augustus, the First Cohort of Batavians, Roman citizens, commanded by its prefect Titus Attius Tutor, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled its vow.
EDCS-07801133.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by the First Cohort of Batavians, Roman citizens, under their prefect Titus Attius Tutor, to Victory Augustus.

EDCS-07801134.text_edition: \n Victoriae Augustae cohors I Baetasiorum civium Romanorum cui praeest Ulpius Titianus praefectus votum solvit laeta libens merito\n
EDCS-07801134.translation: To Augusta Victoria, the First Cohort of Baetasii, Roman citizens, commanded by Prefect Ulpius Titianus, happily, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-07801134.summary: A military dedication to the deified Empress Victoria by the First Cohort of Baetasii, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801136.text_edition: \n Virtuti Augustae iana Quinti filia Hermionae votum solvit laeta libens merito\n
EDCS-07801136.translation: To Augusta Virtus, Hermiona, daughter of Quitus, happily, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-07801136.summary: A personal dedication to the deified Empress Virtus by Hermiona, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801137.text_edition: \n Helstrius Novellus praefectus numini Volcani posuit \n
EDCS-07801137.translation: Helstrius Novellus, prefect, erected this to the divine power of Vulcan.
EDCS-07801137.summary: A dedication to the god Vulcan by Helstrius Novellus, a prefect, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801148.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Aeli Sacas annorum \n
EDCS-07801148.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Aelius Sacas, aged...
EDCS-07801148.summary: A tomb inscription for Aelius Sacas, recording his age, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801149.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulius Marinus ordinatus vixit annis XXXX mensibus I diebus XX Maritima posuit\n
EDCS-07801149.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Julius Marinus, having been ordained, lived 30 years, 1 month, and 20 days. Maritima erected this.
EDCS-07801149.summary: A tomb inscription for Julius Marinus, recording his age at death and the person who erected it, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801150.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. Vireius Pau[li]...
EDCS-07801150.summary: A fragmentary tomb inscription for Vireius Pau[li]..., likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801151.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ingenui annorum X Iulius Simplex pater faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07801151.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Ingenuus, aged 10. His father, Julius Simplex, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-07801151.summary: A tomb inscription for Ingenuus, aged 10, commissioned by his father, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801152.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Moriregis filiis heredes eius substituti fecerunt vixit annos LXX\n
EDCS-07801152.translation: Sacred to the spirits of the departed. For Moriregis. His sons, having been substituted as heirs, made this. He lived 70 years.
EDCS-07801152.summary: A tomb inscription for Moriregis, recording his age at death and mentioning his sons as heirs, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801153.summary: A fragmentary tomb inscription for Rianorix, recording his lifespan, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801154.summary: A tomb inscription for Spurcius, recording his age at death, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801157.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iulia Martina vixit annos XII III dies XXII\n
EDCS-07801157.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Iulia Martina lived 12 years, 3 months, and 22 days.
EDCS-07801157.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Iulia Martina.

EDCS-07801158.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Luca vixit annis XX\n
EDCS-07801158.translation: To the spirits of the underworld. Luca lived 20 years.
EDCS-07801158.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the lifespan of Luca.

EDCS-07801163.summary: A fragmentary inscription that likely records a dedication or ownership by Marcus Septimius.

EDCS-07801165.summary: A very fragmentary inscription containing possible personal names, likely part of a longer text.

EDCS-07801166.summary: This inscription records the name Tiriunculus, possibly belonging to a person or an animal.

EDCS-07801171.text_edition: \n lia praefectus cohortis II nniorum fecit\n
EDCS-07801171.translation: ... praefect of the II cohort of [innior]um made this.
EDCS-07801171.summary: An inscription recording the erection of a monument by a prefect of a cohort.

EDCS-07801174.text_edition: \n a Nonio Philippo legato Augusti in cuneum Frisionum Aballavensium Philippianorum XIIII Kalendas et XIII Kalendas Novembres Gordiano II et Pompeiano consulibus et Attico et Pretextato consulibus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801174.translation: To Nonius Philippus, legate of the emperor, for the 14th and 13th days before the Kalends of November, during the consulships of Gordianus II and Pompeianus, and Atticus and Pretextatus, he willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow for the 14th (or 13th) century of the Frisian Aballavensian Philippian cohort.
EDCS-07801174.summary: A dedication recording the fulfillment of a vow by a legate of the emperor to the Frisian Aballavensian Philippian cohort, dated to the consulships of Gordianus II/Pompeianus and Atticus/Pretextatus.

EDCS-07801176.summary: A fragmentary inscription that likely records a possession or relation to Victorinus.

EDCS-07801178.text_edition: \n Deo Belatucadro sancto Aurelius Tasulus veteranus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801178.translation: To the holy god Belatucadrus, Aurelius Tasulus, a veteran, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07801178.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to the god Belatucadrus by Aurelius Tasulus, a veteran.

EDCS-07801179.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Belatucadro Aurelius Diatova aram ex voto posuit libens laetus merito M\n
EDCS-07801179.translation: To the holy god Belatucadrus, Aurelius Diatova set up an altar, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly. M
EDCS-07801179.summary: A votive inscription dedicated to the god Belatucadrus by Aurelius Diatova, who set up an altar.

EDCS-07801181.text_edition: \n Deae Bellonae Rufinus praefectus equitum alae Augustae et Latinianus filius\n
EDCS-07801181.translation: To the goddess Bellona, Rufinus, prefect of cavalry of the Augustan wing, and Latinianus, his son.
EDCS-07801181.summary: A dedication to the goddess Bellona by Rufinus, a cavalry prefect, and his son Latinianus, likely dating to the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-07801182.text_edition: \n Genio Aurelius Martialis et Aurelius S Eburacio pro se et suis votum solverunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-07801182.translation: To the Genius, Aurelius Martialis and Aurelius S Eburacio paid their vow for themselves and their families, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly.
EDCS-07801182.summary: A vow payment to the Genius by Aurelius Martialis and Aurelius S Eburacio for their families, indicating a personal religious dedication.

EDCS-07801183.text_edition: \n Herculi sortes Sigilius Emeritus dedit de sua pecunia\n
EDCS-07801183.translation: To Hercules, Sigilius Emeritus gave an oracle from his own funds.
EDCS-07801183.summary: Sigilius Emeritus dedicated an oracle to Hercules, funded by himself, suggesting a personal offering and consultation.

EDCS-07801184.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ala Augusta ob virtutem appellata cui praeest Tiberius Claudius Tiberi filius INGM Iustinus praefectus Fusciano II Silano II consulibus\n
EDCS-07801184.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Augustan wing, named for its valor, whose prefect is Tiberius Claudius Iustinus, son of Tiberius, during the consulship of Fuscianus and Silanus.
EDCS-07801184.summary: An inscription honoring Jupiter Best and Greatest by the Augustan cavalry wing, mentioning its commander Tiberius Claudius Iustinus and dating to the consulship of Fuscianus and Silanus.

EDCS-07801185.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo ala Augusta ob virtutem appellata cui praeest Publius Aelius Publi filius Sergia Magnus domo Mursa ex nnia Inferiore praefectus Aproniano et Bradua consulibus\n
EDCS-07801185.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the Augustan wing, named for its valor, whose prefect is Publius Aelius Magnus, son of Publius, of the Sergian tribe, from Mursa in Lower Pannonia, during the consulship of Apronianus and Bradua.
EDCS-07801185.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest by the Augustan cavalry wing, noting its commander Publius Aelius Magnus and his origin, and dating to the consulship of Apronianus and Bradua.

EDCS-07801186.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute imperatoris Luci Septimi Severi Augusti nostri equites alae Augustae curante Egnatio Verecundo praefecto posuerunt\n
EDCS-07801186.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus, for the safety of our emperor Lucius Septimius Severus Augustus, the horsemen of the Augustan wing erected this, under the supervision of Egnatius Verecundus, prefect.
EDCS-07801186.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest Dolichenus for the emperor's well-being, erected by the horsemen of the Augustan wing and its prefect Egnatius Verecundus.

EDCS-07801188.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo pro salute imperatoris Marci Antoni Gordiani Pii Felicis Invicti Augusti et Sabiniae Furiae Tranquillinae coniugi eius totaque domu divina eorum ala Augusta Gordiana ob virtutem appellata posuit cui praeest Aemilius Crispinus prefectus equitum natus in provincia Africa de Tusdro sub cura Nonii Philippi legati Augusti pro pretore Attico et Praetextato consulibus\n
EDCS-07801188.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, for the safety of the emperor Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus and his empress Sabinia Furia Tranquillina and their divine family, the Augustan Gordian wing, named for its valor, erected this, whose prefect is Aemilius Crispinus, born in the province of Africa from Tusdro, under the supervision of Nonius Philippus, imperial legate pro praetore, during the consulship of Atticus and Praetextatus.
EDCS-07801188.summary: A dedication to Jupiter Best and Greatest for the safety of Emperor Gordian and his family, by the Augustan Gordian cavalry wing, mentioning its prefect Aemilius Crispinus and dating to the consulship of Atticus and Praetextatus.

EDCS-07801193.text_edition: \n T B A Titus Aurelius Ateco curator Augustae votum solvit libens animo fecit\n
EDCS-07801193.translation: Titus Aurelius Ateco, curator of Augusta, paid his vow and willingly made this.
EDCS-07801193.summary: Titus Aurelius Ateco, curator of Augusta, fulfills a vow, indicating a personal dedication related to his office.

EDCS-07801194.text_edition: \n cui praeest Septimenus Rusticus prefectus Materno et Bradua consulibus\n
EDCS-07801194.translation: Whose prefect is Septimenus Rusticus, during the consulship of Maternus and Bradua.
EDCS-07801194.summary: This fragment records the prefect Septimenus Rusticus and dates the inscription to the consulship of Maternus and Bradua, likely part of a larger military dedication.

EDCS-07801195.translation: Flavius Aventinus took care of this for the god.
EDCS-07801195.summary: Flavius Aventinus arranged for this dedication to a god, suggesting a commissioned religious act.

EDCS-07801197.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Amatius Ingenuus annorum LX tumulum \n
EDCS-07801197.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Amatius Ingenuus, aged 60, [erected this] tomb.
EDCS-07801197.summary: A tomb inscription recording the death of Amatius Ingenuus, who died at the age of 60.

EDCS-07801198.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Maelonius Secundus equis alae Augustae sesquiplicarius\n
EDCS-07801198.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Maelonius Secundus, sesquiplicarius of the ala Augusta.
EDCS-07801198.summary: A tomb inscription commemorating Maelonius Secundus, a sesquiplicarius of the ala Augusta.

EDCS-07801199.translation: Tancorix, a woman, lived sixty years.
EDCS-07801199.summary: A tomb inscription recording the lifespan of a woman named Tancorix.

EDCS-07801208.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors II Gallorum equitata Titus Domitius Hieron domo Nicomedia praefectus\n
EDCS-07801208.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest. The Second Cohort of Gauls, cavalry-equipped, [dedicates this], Titus Domitius Hieron from Nicomedia, its prefect.
EDCS-07801208.summary: A dedication to Jupiter by the Second Cohort of Gauls, made by their prefect Titus Domitius Hieron.

EDCS-07801209.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Belatucadro et Numinibus Augustorum Iulius Augustalis actor Iuli Lupi praefecti\n
EDCS-07801209.translation: To the god Mars Belatucadrus and the Divine Powers of the Emperors. Iulius Augustalis, administrator for Iulius Lupus, the prefect.
EDCS-07801209.summary: A dedication to the god Mars Belatucadrus and the Divine Powers of the Emperors, made by Iulius Augustalis on behalf of his superior.

EDCS-07801216.text_edition: \n Vicribus T S votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801216.translation: To the Victories, T S fulfilled his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-07801216.summary: A votive inscription to the Victories, recording the fulfillment of a vow by an individual identified by the initials T S.

EDCS-07801217.text_edition: \n Omnibus dibus Unsenis Fersomeris Burcanius Arcavius Vagdavarcustus Povcarus vexillationis MAVI pro salute sua et suorum votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-07801217.translation: To all the gods. Unsenis, Fersomeris, Burcanius, Arcavius, Vagdavarcustus, Povcarus of the MAVI vexillation fulfilled their vow willingly and deservedly for their own safety and that of their families.
EDCS-07801217.summary: A collective votive inscription by members of the MAVI vexillation to all gods, for their safety and that of their families.

EDCS-07801218.text_edition: \n aedem hanc iam vetustate conlapsam restituit ex voto Ca Mimus \n
EDCS-07801218.translation: This temple, already collapsed from age, he restored according to his vow, Ca Mimus.
EDCS-07801218.summary: An inscription recording the restoration of a temple by Ca Mimus, fulfilling a vow.

EDCS-07801221.translation: To one born for the good of the state.
EDCS-07801221.summary: An epitaph honoring someone considered beneficial to the state.

EDCS-07801222.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius vixit annis XI Avo pater pientissimus tam sibi quam et filio suo posuit\n
EDCS-07801222.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelius lived 11 years. Avo, his most devoted father, erected this for himself and also for his son.
EDCS-07801222.summary: A tomb inscription for Aurelius, who died at 11, set up by his father Avo.

EDCS-07801223.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marci Coccei Nonni nnum VI hic situs est\n
EDCS-07801223.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Marcus Cocceius Nonnus lived 6 years. He is buried here.
EDCS-07801223.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Marcus Cocceius Nonnus, who died at the age of six, likely dating from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801224.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavio Martio senatori in civitate Carvetiorum questorio vixit annos XXXXV Martiola filia et heres ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-07801224.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Flavius Martius, senator, former quaestor in the civitas of the Carvetii, lived 45 years. Martiola, his daughter and heir, saw to his burial.
EDCS-07801224.summary: A funerary inscription for Flavius Martius, a Roman senator and former quaestor, who died at 45, with burial arrangements made by his daughter, likely from Roman Britain.

EDCS-07801225.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Crotilo Germanus vixit anis XXVI Greca vixit anis IIII Vindicianus frater titulum posuit\n
EDCS-07801225.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Crotilo the German lived 26 years. Greca lived 4 years. Vindicianus, their brother, erected this monument.
EDCS-07801225.summary: A tombstone commemorating Crotilo and Greca, with ages of death, erected by their brother Vindicianus, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801226.text_edition: \n gaduno Ulpia Traiana emerito alae Petrianae Martius filius ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-07801226.translation: To Gadunus. Ulpia Traiana, a veteran of the ala Petriana, saw to the erection of this monument through her son.
EDCS-07801226.summary: A dedication to the deity Gadunus by Ulpia Traiana, a female veteran of the ala Petriana cavalry unit, with her son acting as the erector, suggesting a military context in Roman Britain.

EDCS-07801227.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aicetuos mater vixit annos XXXXV et Lattio filia vixit annos XII Limisius coniugi et filiae pientissimis posuit\n
EDCS-07801227.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Aicetuos, mother, lived 45 years, and Lattio, daughter, lived 12 years. Limisius erected this for his most dutiful wife and daughter.
EDCS-07801227.summary: A funerary monument for a mother (Aicetuos) and daughter (Lattio), with their ages and the dedicator identified as Limisius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801228.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Ylae alumni karissimi vixit annos XIII Claudius Severus tribunus militum\n
EDCS-07801228.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Yla, most beloved foster child, lived 13 years. Claudius Severus, military tribune.
EDCS-07801228.summary: A funerary inscription for Yla, a foster child who died at 13, dedicated by Claudius Severus, a military tribune, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801238.text_edition: \n Marti Barregi Ianuarius Riregipau votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801238.translation: To Mars Barregus. Ianuarius Riregipau gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-07801238.summary: A votive inscription to the god Mars Barregus, indicating that Ianuarius Riregipau fulfilled a vow, likely from a military or provincial context.

EDCS-07801241.summary: A simple dedication or memorial to Maria Victoria, possibly a goddess or a deceased individual, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801242.text_edition: \n Matribus Parcis pro salute Sanctiae Geminae\n
EDCS-07801242.translation: To the Parcae Mothers for the health of Sanctia Gemina.
EDCS-07801242.summary: A dedication to the Fates (Parcae Matres) for the well-being of Sanctia Gemina, suggesting a concern for her health, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801243.translation: To the god Mercury. Gaius [erected this] at his own expense.
EDCS-07801243.summary: A dedication to the god Mercury by Gaius, likely a personal offering or completion of a vow, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801244.text_edition: \n Parcis pro Bodo Natalis pater votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801244.translation: Natalis' father, Bodo, gladly and deservedly paid his vow to the Parcae.
EDCS-07801244.summary: This inscription records a votive offering paid by Bodo, father of Natalis, to the Parcae.

EDCS-07801246.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Antigonus Papias civis Grecus vixit annos plus minus LX quem ad modum accomodatam fatis animam revocavit Septimia Do\n
EDCS-07801246.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Flavius Antigonus Papias, a citizen of Greece, lived about 60 years and thus recalled his soul, fittingly surrendered to fate. Septimia Do [erected this].
EDCS-07801246.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Flavius Antigonus Papias, a Greek citizen who lived approximately 60 years.

EDCS-07801248.text_edition: \n Luca praefectus alae Augustae Petrianae torquatae civium Romanorum dono dedit\n
EDCS-07801248.translation: Luca, prefect of the First Augustan Auxiliary Cavalry Wing, 'Torquata', composed of Roman citizens, dedicated this gift.
EDCS-07801248.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by Luca, prefect of the First Augustan Auxiliary Cavalry Wing, likely to a military deity or the emperor.

EDCS-07801249.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aniciae Luciliae vixit annos LV\n
EDCS-07801249.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Anicia Lucilia lived 55 years.
EDCS-07801249.summary: This is a funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Anicia Lucilia.

EDCS-07801250.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Aureliana vixsit annos XXXXI Ulpius Apolinaris coniugi carissime posuit\n
EDCS-07801250.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Aureliana lived 41 years. Ulpius Apolinaris set this up for his dearest wife.
EDCS-07801250.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aurelia Aureliana, set up by her husband Ulpius Apolinaris.

EDCS-07801251.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Senecita vixit annos XX Iulius Fortunatus \n
EDCS-07801251.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Aurelia Senecita lived 20 years. Iulius Fortunatus [erected this].
EDCS-07801251.summary: This funerary inscription records the death of Aurelia Senecita, who lived 20 years.

EDCS-07801252.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Vacia, an infant of 3 years.
EDCS-07801252.summary: This is a funerary inscription for a child named Vacia, who died at the age of three.

EDCS-07801256.text_edition: \n Deo Apollini (- - -) Aurelius Maternus pro salute sua et MI IIANAISA MA votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801256.translation: For the god Apollo... Aurelius Maternus gladly and deservedly paid his vow for his own health and [that of] MI IIANAISA MA.
EDCS-07801256.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Aurelius Maternus for his own health and that of another individual to the god Apollo.

EDCS-07801257.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Cocidio Paternius Maternus tribunus cohortis I Nervanae ex evocato Palatino votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801257.translation: For the holy god Cocidius, Paternius Maternus, tribune of the First Cohort of Nervans, formerly an 'evocatus' of the Palatine guard, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-07801257.summary: This inscription records a votive offering paid by Paternius Maternus, a tribune in the First Cohort of Nervans, to the god Cocidius.

EDCS-07801258.text_edition: \n Deo sancto E IVNLIIAR Monime PRID VNICI ERESIS PLERIVM ARIVS posuit\n
EDCS-07801258.translation: For the holy god E IVNLIIAR Monime PRID VNICI ERESIS PLERIVM ARIVS set this up.
EDCS-07801258.summary: This inscription records a dedication to a deity by an individual named Monime, with fragmented personal and divine names.

EDCS-07801259.text_edition: \n Deae sanctae Fortunae Conservatrici Marcus Aurelius Salvius tribunus cohortis I Aeliae Hispanorum equitatae votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801259.translation: To the holy goddess Fortuna the Preserver, Marcus Aurelius Salvius, tribune of the First Aelian Cohort of Spanish Horsemen, gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-07801259.summary: A military tribune dedicates a vow to the goddess Fortuna Conservatrix, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801260.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo votum solvit libens merito Deo Iuteri sancto Fortunat\n
EDCS-07801260.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, and to the holy god Iuter, Fortunatus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-07801260.summary: An individual named Fortunatus dedicates a vow to Jupiter and the god Iuter, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-07801261.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Belatucadro RO Aurelius Nicanor votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07801261.translation: To the god Mars Belatucadrus, Aurelius Nicanor gladly and joyfully paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-07801261.summary: Aurelius Nicanor dedicates a vow to the Romano-British god Mars Belatucadrus, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801262.text_edition: \n Deo Mogonti Vitire sancto Aelius Secundus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801262.translation: To the holy god Mogontus Vitir, Aelius Secundus gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-07801262.summary: Aelius Secundus dedicates a vow to the Germanic deity Mogontus Vitir, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801269.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Alexandro Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate consuli patri patriae cohors I Aelia Hispanorum equitata devota numini maiestatique eius baselicam equestrem exercitatoriam iam pridem a solo coeptam aedificavit consummavitque sub cura Mari Valeriani legati Augusti pro praetore instante Marco Aurelio Salvio tribuno cohortis imperatore domino nostro Severo Alexandro Pio Felice Augusto consule\n
EDCS-07801269.translation: The First Aelian Cohort of Spanish Horsemen built and completed a riding school and training facility, which had been begun from the ground up long ago, to the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power, consul, father of the country, devoted to his divine majesty, under the supervision of Marius Valerianus, legate of the emperor with praetorian authority, under the direction of Marcus Aurelius Salvius, tribune of the cohort, during the reign of our emperor and lord Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, consul.
EDCS-07801269.summary: The First Aelian Cohort of Spanish Horsemen dedicates a newly constructed riding and training facility to Emperor Severus Alexander, dating to his reign (222-235 CE).

EDCS-07801271.text_edition: \n templum imperatoris ANTOIII PIISVSBSCOS vexillationes legionum II Augustae et XX Valeriae victricis item cohortis I Aeliae Hispanorum |(milliariae) equitatae sub cura Modi Iuli legatus Augusti pro praetore instante - Aelius \n
EDCS-07801271.translation: Temple to the Emperor... under the supervision of Modius Iulius, legate of the emperor with praetorian authority, under the direction of - Aelius...
EDCS-07801271.summary: Detached fragments indicate the construction of a temple dedicated to an emperor, involving detachments of Legio II Augusta and Legio XX Valeria Victrix, and the First Aelian Cohort of Spanish Horsemen, during the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07801275.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Titullinia Pussitta civis Raeta vixsit annos XXXV menses VIII dies XV TV\n
EDCS-07801275.translation: To the departed spirits, Titullinia Pussitta, a citizen of Raetia, lived for 35 years, 8 months, and 15 days.
EDCS-07801275.summary: A funerary inscription recording the lifespan of Titullinia Pussitta, a citizen from Raetia, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-07801276.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Cocidio Annius Victor centurio legionis E\n
EDCS-07801276.translation: To the holy god Cocidius, Annius Victor, centurion of the E... legion...
EDCS-07801276.summary: Annius Victor, a centurion of a Roman legion, dedicates a vow to the Romano-British god Cocidius, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-07801278.translation: To the god Cocidius, Aventinus made [this].
EDCS-07801278.summary: Aventinus dedicates something to the god Cocidius, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-07801279.text_edition: \n Sancto Cocideo Aurunceius Felicessemus tribunus ex evocato votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801279.translation: To the holy Cocidius, Aurunceius Felicessemus, tribune from the evocati, gladly paid his vow, as was deserved.
EDCS-07801279.summary: Aurunceius Felicessemus, a tribune from the evocati, dedicates a vow to the god Cocidius, likely dating to the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07801280.text_edition: \n Deo sancto Cocidio Quintus Peltrasius Maximus tribunus ex corniculario praeffectorum praetorio eemminentissimorum vvirorum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801280.translation: To the holy god Cocidius, Quintus Peltrasius Maximus, tribune and former aide to the most eminent prefects of the praetorium, gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07801280.summary: A military officer, Quintus Peltrasius Maximus, dedicates a vow to the god Cocidius.

EDCS-07801284.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Cocidio sancto Aelius Vitalianus dono dedit libens merito\n
EDCS-07801284.translation: To the holy god Mars Cocidius, Aelius Vitalianus gladly and deservedly gave this offering.
EDCS-07801284.summary: Aelius Vitalianus dedicates an offering to the god Mars Cocidius.

EDCS-07801313.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolycheno Iulius Valentinus ordinatus Germania Superiore ex iussu ipsius posuit pro se et suis libens laetus merito Praesente et Extricato II consulibus\n
EDCS-07801313.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, Iulius Valentinus, having been ordained in Upper Germany by the god's own command, set this up for himself and his family, gladly and deservedly. With Praesens and Extricatus as consuls.
EDCS-07801313.summary: Iulius Valentinus dedicates an offering to Jupiter Dolichenus, mentioning his ordination and including his family.

EDCS-07801315.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Condati Attonius Quintianus mensor evocatus imperatum ex iussu solvit libens animo\n
EDCS-07801315.translation: To the god Mars Condates, Attonius Quintianus, a surveyor and recalled soldier, fulfilled what was commanded by his order, gladly and willingly.
EDCS-07801315.summary: Attonius Quintianus dedicates a fulfillment of a vow to Mars Condates.

EDCS-07801317.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gracili ordinato Germania Superiore legione XXII Aurelia illa coniugi faciendum curavit\n
