EDCS-07900917.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled to the goddess Ricagambeda by the pagus Vellaus, serving in the Second Cohort of Tungrians, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900918.text_edition: \n Deae Viradecthi pagus Condrustis militans in cohorte II Tungrorum sub Silvio Auspice praefecto\n
EDCS-07900918.translation: To the goddess Viradecthis, the pagus of Condrustis, serving in the Second Cohort of Tungrians, under the prefect Silvio Auspex.
EDCS-07900918.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddess Viradecthis by the pagus Condrustis, serving in the Second Cohort of Tungrians, under the command of Prefect Silvio Auspex, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900919.text_edition: \n Dibus Deabusque omnibus Frumentius miles cohortis II Tungrorum\n
EDCS-07900919.translation: To all gods and goddesses, Frumentius, a soldier of the Second Cohort of Tungrians.
EDCS-07900919.summary: This inscription records a dedication to all gods and goddesses by Frumentius, a soldier of the Second Cohort of Tungrians, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900920.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate XXI consuli IIII cohors II Tungrorum miliaria equitata civium Latinorum sub Iulio Vero legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-07900920.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, pontifex maximus, with tribunician power for the 21st time, consul for the 4th time, from the Second Cohort of Tungrians, a milliary equestrian unit of Roman citizens, under Julius Verus, imperial legate pro praetore.
EDCS-07900920.summary: This inscription records a dedication for Emperor Antoninus Pius by the Second Cohort of Tungrians, an equestrian unit, under the legate Julius Verus, dating to the reign of Antoninus Pius (138-161 AD).

EDCS-07900925.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Afutiano Bassi ordinato cohortis II Tungrorum Flavia Baetica coniunx faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-07900925.translation: For the spirits of the departed, Flavia Baetica, wife of Afutianus Bassus, a decurion of the Second Cohort of Tungrians, arranged for this to be made.
EDCS-07900925.summary: This inscription records the commission of a funerary monument for Afutianus Bassus, a decurion of the Second Cohort of Tungrians, by his wife Flavia Baetica, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900927.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo vexillatio Retorum Gaesatorum quorum curam agit Iulius Severinus tribunus\n
EDCS-07900927.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, from the vexillation of the Raetian Caeserati, whose commander is the tribune Julius Severinus.
EDCS-07900927.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by a detachment (vexillation) of Raetian Caeserati soldiers under the command of Tribune Julius Severinus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900928.text_edition: \n cohors I fida Vardullorum civium Romanorum |(miliaria) equitata et Gaius Quintius Severus tribunus cohortis eiusdem domo Camilia Ravenna votum solverunt laeti libentes merito\n
EDCS-07900928.translation: The First Cohort of Vardullians, loyal, Roman citizens, an equestrian mixed unit, and Gaius Quintius Severus, tribune of the same cohort, from Camilia Ravenna, happily, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-07900928.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by the First Cohort of Vardullians and its tribune, Gaius Quintius Severus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07900930.text_edition: \n Deo Apollini Lucius Maximius Gaetulicus |(centurio) legionis\n
EDCS-07900930.translation: To Apollo, Lucius Maximius Gaetulicus, centurion of a legion, [fulfilled his vow].
EDCS-07900930.summary: This fragmentary inscription records a vow made to Apollo by Lucius Maximius Gaetulicus, a centurion, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07900931.text_edition: \n Campestribus sacrum Aelius Marcus decurio alae Augustae Vocontiorum votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07900931.translation: To the Campestres, Aelius Marcus, decurion of the Augustan Wing of the Vocontii, happily, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07900931.summary: This inscription records a vow made to the Campestres by Aelius Marcus, a decurion of the Augustan Wing of the Vocontii, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07900932.text_edition: \n Dianae Reginae ob prosperos eventus Gaius Arrius Domitianus legionis XX Valeriae victricis votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07900932.translation: To Diana Regina, for successful outcomes, Gaius Arrius Domitianus of the 20th Valeria Victrix Legion happily, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07900932.summary: This inscription records a vow made to Diana Regina by Gaius Arrius Domitianus of the 20th Valeria Victrix Legion, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-07900933.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Gaius Arrius Domitianus |(centurio) legionis XX Valeriae victricis votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07900933.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, Gaius Arrius Domitianus, centurion of the 20th Valeria Victrix Legion, happily, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07900933.summary: This inscription records a vow made to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Gaius Arrius Domitianus, a centurion of the 20th Valeria Victrix Legion, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-07900934.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano pro salute sua et suorum Gaius Arrius Domitianus legionis XX Valeriae victricis votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07900934.translation: To Silvanus, for the safety of himself and his family, Gaius Arrius Domitianus of the 20th Valeria Victrix Legion happily, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07900934.summary: This inscription records a vow made to Silvanus by Gaius Arrius Domitianus of the 20th Valeria Victrix Legion, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-07900942.text_edition: \n Apollini Granno Quintus Lusius Sabinianus procurator Augusti votum solvit S libens V merito\n
EDCS-07900942.translation: To Apollo Grannus, Quintus Lusius Sabinianus, procurator of the Emperor, happily, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-07900942.summary: This inscription records a vow made to Apollo Grannus by Quintus Lusius Sabinianus, an imperial procurator, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07900944.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors V Gallorum cui praeest Lucius Minthonius Tertullus praefectus votum solvit laeta libens merito\n
EDCS-07900944.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the Fifth Cohort of Gauls, commanded by Lucius Minthonius Tertullus, prefect, happily, willingly, and deservedly fulfilled its vow.
EDCS-07900944.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by the Fifth Cohort of Gauls, commanded by its prefect Lucius Minthonius Tertullus, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07900946.text_edition: \n Gaius Publius Cr in Pomponian praefectus posuit dedit dedicavit\n
EDCS-07900946.translation: Gaius Publius Cr in Pomponian, prefect, erected, gave, and dedicated [this monument].
EDCS-07900946.summary: This inscription records the erection and dedication of a monument by Gaius Publius Cr in Pomponian, a prefect, likely from the 1st or 2nd century CE.

EDCS-07900949.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae legio II Augusta per milia passuum IIII DCLII fecit legio II Augusta\n
EDCS-07900949.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, the Second Augusta Legion constructed [this road] for 4652 Roman miles. The Second Augusta Legion.
EDCS-07900949.summary: This inscription records the construction of a road by the Second Augusta Legion for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely dating to his reign (138-161 CE).

EDCS-07900950.text_edition: \n Herculi Magusano sacrum Valerius Nigrinus duplicarius alae Tungrorum\n
EDCS-07900950.translation: Sacred to Hercules Magusanus. Valerius Nigrinus, a duplicarius of the Tungrorum ala.
EDCS-07900950.summary: This is a dedication to the god Hercules Magusanus by a cavalryman of the Tungrorum ala, likely dating to the 2nd or 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07900951.translation: Cassius, a standard-bearer, to the Mothers.
EDCS-07900951.summary: This is a dedication to the Matres (a group of maternal deities) by a soldier named Cassius, possibly from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900952.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nectovelius filius Vindicis annorum IXXX stipendiorum VIIII nationis Brigans militavit in cohorte II Thracum\n
EDCS-07900952.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Nectovelius, son of Vindex, aged eighty years, nine years of service, of the Brigantes tribe, served in the II Cohort of Thracians.
EDCS-07900952.summary: This is a tombstone inscription for Nectovelius, a Brigantian soldier who served in the II Cohort of Thracians, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900954.text_edition: \n Victoriae cohors VI Nerviorum cuius curam Flavius Betto |(centurio) legionis XX Valeriae victricis votum solvit laeta libens merito\n
EDCS-07900954.translation: The VI Cohort of Nervii, under the command of Flavius Betto, a centurion of the XX Legion Valeria Victrix, gladly, willingly, and deservedly paid their vow to Victoria.
EDCS-07900954.summary: This inscription records a vow paid to the goddess Victoria by the VI Cohort of Nervii, commanded by Flavius Betto, a centurion of the XX Valeria Victrix legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900956.text_edition: \n Fortunae vexillationes legionis II Augustae legionis VI victricis piae fidelis posuerunt laetae libentes\n
EDCS-07900956.translation: Detachments of the II Augusta Legion and the VI Victrix Legion, pious and faithful, gladly and willingly dedicated this to Fortuna.
EDCS-07900956.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the goddess Fortuna made by detachments of two legions, the II Augusta and the VI Victrix, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900959.text_edition: \n Deo Neptuno cohors I Fida Vardullorum civium Romanorum equitata |(milliaria) cui praeest Trebius Verus praefectus\n
EDCS-07900959.translation: To the god Neptune. The I Cohort Fida of Vardullians, Roman citizens, an equestrian unit of a thousand men, commanded by Trebius Verus, the prefect.
EDCS-07900959.summary: This inscription records the dedication to Neptune by the I Cohort Fida of Vardullians, a full-strength auxiliary cavalry unit, under the command of Prefect Trebius Verus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900961.text_edition: \n Caius Iulius Sp<e=I>ratus natione Mattiacus veteranus legionis VI victricis piae fidelis votum solvit libens laetus merito \n
EDCS-07900961.translation: Gaius Julius Speratus, of the Mattiacus tribe, a veteran of the VI Victrix Legion, pious and faithful, gladly, joyfully, and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-07900961.summary: This inscription records a vow paid by Gaius Julius Speratus, a Mattiaci veteran of the VI Victrix Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900966.translation: From the century of Antonius Aratus of the VI Cohort.
EDCS-07900966.summary: This inscription marks a dedication or property belonging to the century of Antonius Aratus within the VI Cohort, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900969.text_edition: \n Marti Gaius D B votum solvit\n
EDCS-07900969.translation: Gaius paid his vow to Mars.
EDCS-07900969.summary: This is a simple dedication to the god Mars by a man named Gaius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900970.text_edition: \n Nymphis vexillatio legionis VI victricis piae fidelis sub Fabio Liberali\n
EDCS-07900970.translation: A detachment of the VI Victrix Legion, pious and faithful, under the command of Fabius Liberalis, to the Nymphs.
EDCS-07900970.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Nymphs made by a detachment of the VI Victrix Legion, commanded by Fabius Liberalis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900974.summary: This inscription records the century belonging to Gliconius Abrucius, likely a military unit designation.

EDCS-07900977.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano Caristanius Iustianus praefectus cohortis I Hamiorum votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07900977.translation: To Silvanus, Caristanius Iustianus, prefect of the First Cohort of Hamians, paid his vow willingly, happily, and deservedly.
EDCS-07900977.summary: This is a dedication to the god Silvanus by Caristanius Iustianus, prefect of the First Cohort of Hamians, fulfilling a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900982.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gai Iuli Marcellini praefecti cohortis I Hamiorum\n
EDCS-07900982.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead, for Gaius Julius Marcellinus, prefect of the First Cohort of Hamians.
EDCS-07900982.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Gaius Julius Marcellinus, prefect of the First Cohort of Hamians, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900984.text_edition: \n Dianae Apollini Marcus Cocceius Firmus |(centurio) legionis II Augustae\n
EDCS-07900984.translation: To Diana and Apollo, Marcus Cocceius Firmus, centurion of the Second Augusta Legion.
EDCS-07900984.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the deities Diana and Apollo by Marcus Cocceius Firmus, a centurion of the Second Augusta Legion, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900985.text_edition: \n Genio Terrae Britannicae Marcus Cocceius Firmus |(centurio) legionis II Augustae\n
EDCS-07900985.translation: To the Genius of Britain, Marcus Cocceius Firmus, centurion of the Second Augusta Legion.
EDCS-07900985.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Genius of Britain by Marcus Cocceius Firmus, a centurion of the Second Augusta Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900986.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Victoriae Victrici pro salute imperatoris nostri et sua suorum Marcus Cocceius Firmus |(centurio) legionis II Augustae\n
EDCS-07900986.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Victorious Victory, for the health of our Emperor and his own and his family's, Marcus Cocceius Firmus, centurion of the Second Augusta Legion.
EDCS-07900986.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter and Victory for the welfare of the emperor and the dedicator's family by Marcus Cocceius Firmus, a centurion of the Second Augusta Legion, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900987.text_edition: \n Marti Minervae Campestribus Herculi Eponae Victoriae Marcus Cocceius Firmus |(centurio) legionis II Augustae\n
EDCS-07900987.translation: To Mars, Minerva, the Campestrian deities, Hercules, Epona, and Victory, Marcus Cocceius Firmus, centurion of the Second Augusta Legion.
EDCS-07900987.summary: This inscription is a dedication to various deities including Mars, Minerva, Hercules, Epona, and Victory by Marcus Cocceius Firmus, a centurion of the Second Augusta Legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900989.text_edition: \n NO MART Marcus Al Victor miles legionis II Augustae TALAEEX VLERNIS\n
EDCS-07900989.translation: To Mars... Marcus Al Victor, soldier of the Second Augusta Legion...[illegible].
EDCS-07900989.summary: This inscription is a partial dedication to Mars by Marcus Al Victor, a soldier of the Second Augusta Legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900991.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Flavius Lucianus miles legionis II Augustae\n
EDCS-07900991.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead, Flavius Lucianus, soldier of the Second Augusta Legion.
EDCS-07900991.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Flavius Lucianus, a soldier of the Second Augusta Legion, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900992.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Salmanes vixit annos XV Salmanes posuit\n
EDCS-07900992.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead, Salmanes lived for 15 years. Salmanes set this up.
EDCS-07900992.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Salmanes, who died at the age of 15 and for whom this monument was erected by a relative also named Salmanes, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900993.translation: To the divine spirits of Verecunda.
EDCS-07900993.summary: This is a funerary epitaph dedicated to Verecunda, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900995.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae vexillatio legionis VI victricis piae fidelis per mille passus\n
EDCS-07900995.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, a detachment of the Sixth Legion Victrix, dutiful and loyal, for 1,000 paces.
EDCS-07900995.summary: This inscription records the work done by a detachment of the Sixth Legion Victrix for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely relating to construction or a road, dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-07900997.text_edition: \n Deo Silvano Lucius Tanicius Verus praefectus votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07900997.translation: To the god Silvanus, Lucius Tanicius Verus, prefect, gladly and deservedly paid his vow freely.
EDCS-07900997.summary: This is a votive inscription to the god Silvanus by Lucius Tanicius Verus, a prefect, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07900999.translation: To the goddess Fortuna, Caecilius Nepos, tribune.
EDCS-07900999.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Fortuna by Caecilius Nepos, a tribune, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07901003.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae legio II Augusta <per=P> milia passuum III DCLXVI semis \n
EDCS-07901003.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, the Second Augusta Legion, for 3,666 and a half paces.
EDCS-07901003.summary: This inscription records work performed by the Second Augusta Legion for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely related to construction or a road, dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-07901004.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae vexillatio legionis VI victricis piae fidelis per milia passuum III DCLXVI semis\n
EDCS-07901004.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, a detachment of the Sixth Legion Victrix, dutiful and loyal, for 3,666 and a half paces.
EDCS-07901004.summary: This inscription records the work done by a detachment of the Sixth Legion Victrix for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely relating to construction or a road, dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-07901005.text_edition: \n Campestribus et nnae Quintus Pisentius Iustus praefectus cohortis IIII Gallorum votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-07901005.translation: To the Campestrian gods, Quintus Pisentius Iustus, prefect of the Fourth Cohort of Gauls, gladly and deservedly paid his vow freely.
EDCS-07901005.summary: This is a votive inscription to the Campestrian gods by Quintus Pisentius Iustus, prefect of the Fourth Cohort of Gauls, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-07901006.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae vexillatio legionis VI Victricis piae fidelis per milia passuum III DCLXVI semis\n
EDCS-07901006.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, a detachment of the Sixth Legion Victrix, dutiful and loyal, for 3,666 and a half paces.
EDCS-07901006.summary: This inscription records the work done by a detachment of the Sixth Legion Victrix for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely relating to construction or a road, dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-07901007.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae vexillatio legionis XX Valeriae victricis per pedum III\n
EDCS-07901007.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, a detachment of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix, for 3 feet.
EDCS-07901007.summary: This inscription records work done by a detachment of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely relating to construction or a road, dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-07901008.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae vexillatio legionis XX Valeriae victricis fecit per pedum III\n
EDCS-07901008.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, a detachment of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix made this for 3 feet.
EDCS-07901008.summary: This inscription records work done by a detachment of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely relating to construction or a road, dating to his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-07901009.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae vexillatio legionis XX Valeriae victricis fecit p\n
EDCS-07901009.translation: The detachment of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix built this for the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-07901009.summary: A military detachment records its construction work for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely in the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-07901010.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto patri patriae vexillatio legionis VI Victricis piae fidelis opus valli pedum MMMCCXL fecit Virtus Augusta\n
EDCS-07901010.translation: The detachment of the Sixth Legion Victrix, dutiful and loyal, built this wall, 3240 feet long, for the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus, father of the fatherland, at Virtus Augusta.
EDCS-07901010.summary: A military detachment records its construction of a wall measuring 3240 feet for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely in the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-07901014.text_edition: \n Imperatori Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae legio II Augusta fecit pedum III CCLXXI\n
EDCS-07901014.translation: The Second Augusta Legion built this for the Emperor Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland, 2271 feet long.
EDCS-07901014.summary: The Second Augusta Legion records its construction of a wall or structure measuring 2271 feet for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely in the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-07901015.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto patri patriae vexillatio legionis VI victricis piae fidelis opus valli pedum MMMMCXLI\n
EDCS-07901015.translation: The detachment of the Sixth Legion Victrix, dutiful and loyal, built this wall, 3941 feet long, for the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-07901015.summary: A military detachment records its construction of a wall measuring 3941 feet for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely in the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-07901017.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae vexillatio legionis XX Valeriae victricis fecit per pedum IIII C\xc3\x84XI\n
EDCS-07901017.translation: The detachment of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix built this, 4111 feet long, for the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the fatherland.
EDCS-07901017.summary: A military detachment records its construction of a structure measuring 4111 feet for Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely in the mid-2nd century AD.

EDCS-07901028.text_edition: \n Dominis Nostris Flavio Valerio Severo et Galerio Valerio Maximino nobilissimis Caesaribus\n
EDCS-07901028.translation: To our Lords, Flavius Valerius Severus and Galerius Valerius Maximinius, most noble Caesars.
EDCS-07901028.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Caesars Severus and Maximinius Daza, likely dating to their period of rule in the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-07901029.translation: To the son of the divine Constantius Pius Augustus.
EDCS-07901029.summary: This dedication is to Constantine the Great, son of Constantius Chlorus, dating to the early 4th century AD.

EDCS-07901031.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felice Augusto respublica Belgarum posuit\n
EDCS-07901031.translation: The state of the Belgae erected this for the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-07901031.summary: This inscription records the erection of a monument by the people of the Belgae for Emperor Gordian III, likely in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07901032.text_edition: \n Imperatoribus Caesaribus Gallo et Volusiano Aug(ustis)\n
EDCS-07901032.translation: For the Emperors Caesars Gallus and Volusianus, Augusts.
EDCS-07901032.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the co-emperors Gallus and Volusianus, dating to their brief reign in 251-253 AD.

EDCS-07901033.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Antonio Gordiano Ipmeratori Caesari Exsuvio Tetricus Pius Felix Augustus\n
EDCS-07901033.translation: For the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus, and for the Emperor Caesar Exsuvius Tetricus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-07901033.summary: This inscription appears to honor both Emperor Gordian III and possibly the usurper Tetricus I, likely dating to the period of the Crisis of the Third Century (mid-3rd century AD).

EDCS-07901034.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Gaio Pio Esuio Tetrico Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-07901034.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Pius Esuvius Tetricus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-07901034.summary: This inscription records an acclamation or dedication to the emperor Tetricus I, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07901035.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Gaio Aesuio Tetrico Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-07901035.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Aesuvius Tetricus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-07901035.summary: This inscription records an acclamation or dedication to the emperor Tetricus I, likely from the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07901036.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Lucius Domitius Aurelianus.
EDCS-07901036.summary: This inscription records an acclamation or dedication to the emperor Aurelian, dating to his reign from 270-275 AD.

EDCS-07901038.text_edition: \n Imperatori Flavio Valerio Severo Pio Felici nobilissimo Caesari\n
EDCS-07901038.translation: To the Emperor Flavius Valerius Severus Pius Felix, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-07901038.summary: This inscription records an acclamation to Caesar Severus II, who served as Caesar from 305-306 AD and Augustus from 306-307 AD.

EDCS-07901039.text_edition: \n Imperatoribus Caesaribus dominis nostris Gallo et Volusiano \n
EDCS-07901039.translation: To our Lords Emperors Caesar Gallus and Volusianus.
EDCS-07901039.summary: This inscription records an acclamation to the co-emperors Gallus and Volusianus, dating to their joint rule from 251-253 AD.

EDCS-07901040.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Galerio Valerio Liciniano Licinio \n
EDCS-07901040.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Galerius Valerius Licinianus Licinius.
EDCS-07901040.summary: This inscription records an acclamation to the emperor Licinius, who ruled various parts of the Roman Empire between 308 and 324 AD.

EDCS-07901041.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari domino nostro Marco Cassianio Latinio Postumo \n
EDCS-07901041.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, our Lord, Marcus Cassianius Latinus Postumus.
EDCS-07901041.summary: This inscription records an acclamation to the emperor Postumus, a usurper who ruled the Gallic Empire from 260-269 AD.

EDCS-07901042.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio nobilissimo Caesari divi Constanti Pii Felicis Augusti filio\n
EDCS-07901042.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius, most noble Caesar, son of the divine Constantius Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-07901042.summary: This inscription records an acclamation to Caesar Constantine I, likely from around 306-307 AD, acknowledging his father Constantius I.

EDCS-07901043.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici\n
EDCS-07901043.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Antoninus Gordianus Pius Felix.
EDCS-07901043.summary: This inscription records an acclamation to the emperor Gordian III, dating to his reign from 238-244 AD.

EDCS-07901044.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Annio Floriano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto mille passus I\n
EDCS-07901044.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Annius Florianus Pius Felix, Invictus Augustus, one mile.
EDCS-07901044.summary: This inscription records an acclamation to the emperor Florianus, who reigned briefly in 274 AD, possibly marking a distance of one mile.

EDCS-07901045.translation: To the most noble Caesar Augustus.
EDCS-07901045.summary: This is a dedication to the emperor Augustus Caesar, likely from a public monument.

EDCS-07901046.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio nobilissimo Caesari divi Constanti Augusti Pii filio Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio nobilissimo Caesari \n
EDCS-07901046.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, the pious, most noble Caesar, son of the deified Constantius Augustus, the pious, Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, the pious, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-07901046.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantine I, likely an honorary titulary on a monument during his reign in the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-07901047.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Piaonio Victorino Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia [potestate ------\n
EDCS-07901047.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Piaonius Victorinus, the pious, fortunate Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power for [several years].
EDCS-07901047.summary: This is an honorary inscription for Emperor Victorinus, a claimant emperor of the 3rd century CE, with a significant lacuna in the dating formula.

EDCS-07901048.text_edition: \n Domino Nostro Flavio Iulio Crispo nobilissimo Caesari Flavi Constantini Maximi Pii filio divi Constanti Pii nepoti\n
EDCS-07901048.translation: To Our Lord Flavius Julius Crispus, most noble Caesar, son of Flavius Constantinus Maximus, the pious, grandson of the deified Constantius, the pious.
EDCS-07901048.summary: This inscription honors Caesar Crispus, son of Constantine I, likely dating to the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-07901049.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Publio Licinio Valeriano Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis patri patriae Respublica Lindensis\n
EDCS-07901049.translation: To Emperor Caesar Publius Licinius Valerianus, the pious, fortunate Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, from the Lindensian Republic.
EDCS-07901049.summary: This is an honorary inscription for Emperor Valerian I, a dedication by the Republic of Lindum, likely dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901050.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Piavonio Victorino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis patri patriae a Lindo Segelocum milia passuum XIIII\n
EDCS-07901050.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Piavonius Victorinus, the pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, chief priest, holding tribunician power, father of the fatherland, from Lindo [to] Segelocum, 14 miles.
EDCS-07901050.summary: This milestone inscription records a distance from Lindo to Segelocum, dedicated to Emperor Victorinus, dating to his reign in the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901051.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto divi Constanti Pii Augusti filio\n
EDCS-07901051.translation: To Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, the pious, fortunate, unconquered Augustus, son of the deified Constantius, the pious Augustus.
EDCS-07901051.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Constantine I, likely an honorary titulary on a monument during his reign in the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-07901052.text_edition: \n tribuniciae potestatis consuli II patri patriae a Navione milia passuum XI\n
EDCS-07901052.translation: Holding tribunician power, consul for the second time, father of the fatherland, from Navione, 11 miles.
EDCS-07901052.summary: This milestone inscription records a distance from Navione, likely dating to the reign of an emperor holding these specific offices, possibly Constantine I.

EDCS-07901053.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar divi Traiani Parthici filius divi Nervae nepos Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pater patriae tribuniciae potestatis IV consul III a Ratis milia II\n
EDCS-07901053.translation: Emperor Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, father of the fatherland, holding tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, from Ratis, 2 miles.
EDCS-07901053.summary: This milestone inscription records a distance from Ratis, dedicated to Emperor Hadrian, dating to his reign in the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-07901056.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely the beginning of a dedication or titulary to an Emperor Caesar, possibly on a public monument.

EDCS-07901059.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Numoriano respublica civitatis Dobunnorum A\n
EDCS-07901059.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Numerius, the state of the town of the Dobunni.
EDCS-07901059.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the Romanized Celtic tribe of the Dobunni to the Emperor Marcus Aurelius Numerius, likely in the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901060.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco C Piavonio Victorino Augusto\n
EDCS-07901060.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Piavinius Victorinus Augustus.
EDCS-07901060.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the short-lived usurper Emperor Victorinus, dated to his reign in the Gallic Empire (269-271 CE).

EDCS-07901061.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus\n
EDCS-07901061.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antonius Gordianus Augustus.
EDCS-07901061.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, who reigned from 238 to 244 CE.

EDCS-07901064.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Cassianio Latinio Postumo Au[[gg(usto)]]\n
EDCS-07901064.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Cassianius Latinus Postumus Augustus.
EDCS-07901064.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Gallic Emperor Postumus, who reigned from 260 to 269 CE.

EDCS-07901065.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Diocletian Augustus.
EDCS-07901065.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Diocletian, who reigned from 284 to 305 CE.

EDCS-07901066.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantius.
EDCS-07901066.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Constantius Chlorus, father of Constantine the Great, who reigned as Caesar from 293 to 305 CE and as Augustus from 305 to 306 CE.

EDCS-07901067.text_edition: \n Imperatori Domino Nostro Marco Cassianio Latinio Postumo Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-07901067.translation: To our Emperor and Lord Marcus Cassianius Latinus Postumus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-07901067.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the Gallic Emperor Postumus, emphasizing his divine favor and piety, dating to his reign (260-269 CE).

EDCS-07901068.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Claudio Tacito Pio Felici Invicto Augusto\n
EDCS-07901068.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Claudius Tacitus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus.
EDCS-07901068.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Tacitus, who reigned briefly in 275-276 CE.

EDCS-07901070.text_edition: \n Numinibus Augustorum imperator Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Arabicus IX\n
EDCS-07901070.translation: To the divine spirits of the Emperors, Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Pius Felix Augustus Arabicus, holding the tribunician power for the ninth time.
EDCS-07901070.summary: This inscription records a dedication to Emperor Caracalla, likely made during his reign (198-217 CE) and mentioning his honorific titles and ninth tribunician power.

EDCS-07901071.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Traianus Hadrianus Augustus pontifex maximus tribuniciae potestatis V pater patriae consul III a Kanovio milia passuum VIII\n
EDCS-07901071.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, pontifex maximus, holder of the tribunician power for the 5th time, father of the country, consul for the 3rd time, 8 miles from Kanovium.
EDCS-07901071.summary: This inscription records a milestone set up under Emperor Hadrian, indicating his titles and the distance from the fort of Kanovium (North Wales), dating to his fifth tribunician power.

EDCS-07901072.text_edition: \n Imperatores Caesares Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax et Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti et Publius \n
EDCS-07901072.translation: To the Emperors Caesar Lucius Septimius Severus Pius Pertinax and Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augusti and Publius...
EDCS-07901072.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription honoring the co-emperors Septimius Severus and Caracalla, likely from the Severan period (late 2nd to early 3rd century CE).

EDCS-07901073.text_edition: \n invicto Augusto divi Constanti Pii Augusti filio\n
EDCS-07901073.translation: ...to the unconquered Augustus, son of the divine Constantius Pius Augustus.
EDCS-07901073.summary: This text likely formed part of an inscription honoring an emperor, possibly Constantine I, as the son of Constantius Chlorus, dating to the Tetrarchic or Constantinian period (late 3rd to early 4th century CE).

EDCS-07901074.text_edition: \n Domino Nostro Imperatori Caesari Gaio Messio Quinto Decio Traiano\n
EDCS-07901074.translation: To Our Lord the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Traianus...
EDCS-07901074.summary: This is the beginning of an inscription dedicated to Emperor Decius, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901075.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus...
EDCS-07901075.summary: This is the beginning of an inscription honoring Emperor Gordian III, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901076.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Augusto Nostro\n
EDCS-07901076.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus, Our Lord.
EDCS-07901076.summary: This is a complete inscription dedicated to Emperor Philip I (Philip the Arab), dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901077.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Domino Nostro Gaio Messio Quinto Decio Traiano Pio Felici Invicto Augusto\n
EDCS-07901077.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, Our Lord, Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Pius Felix Invictus Augustus.
EDCS-07901077.summary: This is a complete inscription dedicated to Emperor Decius, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901078.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Traiano Hadriano Augusto pontifici maximo tribuniciae potestatis consuli III patri patriae L milia passuum IIII\n
EDCS-07901078.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Trajan Hadrian Augustus, chief priest, holder of tribunician power for the 3rd time, consul for the 3rd time, father of the fatherland. Four Roman miles.
EDCS-07901078.summary: This is an inscription honoring Emperor Hadrian, dating to his reign (117-138 CE), which also records a distance of four Roman miles.

EDCS-07901079.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Gaio Messio Quinto Decio Pio Felici Augusto et Gaio Messio Quinto Etrusco Caesari\n
EDCS-07901079.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Pius Felix Augustus and to Gaius Messius Quintus Etruscus Caesar.
EDCS-07901079.summary: This inscription honors Emperor Decius and his son and Caesar, Quintus Etruscus, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901080.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Annio Floriano Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-07901080.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Annius Florianus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-07901080.summary: This is a complete inscription dedicated to the short-lived emperor Florian, dating to 276 CE.

EDCS-07901081.text_edition: \n Augusto Imperatori Caesari Gaio Messius Quinto Decio Traiano Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-07901081.translation: To Augustus, Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Decius Traianus Pius Felix Augustus.
EDCS-07901081.summary: This is a complete inscription dedicated to Emperor Decius, dating to the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901082.summary: This inscription is a fragment of a dedication likely to the emperor Gaius Messius (Decius), dating to his reign in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07901083.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Domino Nostro Gaio Messio \n
EDCS-07901083.translation: To the Emperor Caesar, Our Lord Gaius Messius
EDCS-07901083.summary: This inscription is a dedication fragment addressed to Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius (Decius), dating to his reign in the mid-3rd century AD.

EDCS-07901084.text_edition: \n Imperatoribus Dominis Nostris Gallo et Volusiano Augustis\n
EDCS-07901084.translation: To the Emperors, Our Lords Gallus and Volusianus Augusti
EDCS-07901084.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the co-emperors Gallus and Volusianus, dating their joint reign between 251 and 253 AD.

EDCS-07901085.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Annio Floriano Pio Felici Augusto Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Probo Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-07901085.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Annius Florianus Pius Felix Augustus, Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Probus Pius Felix Augustus
EDCS-07901085.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperors Florianus and Probus, dating their joint reign in 276 AD.

EDCS-07901086.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Caro Pio Felici Augusto milia\n
EDCS-07901086.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Carus Pius Felix Augustus
EDCS-07901086.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the emperor Carus, dating to his reign between 282 and 283 AD.

EDCS-07901087.text_edition: \n Caro Pio Felici E Invicto Augusto\n
EDCS-07901087.translation: Carus Pius Felix and Invictus Augustus
EDCS-07901087.summary: This inscription is a fragment likely honoring the emperor Carus, dating to his reign between 282 and 283 AD.

EDCS-07901089.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Invicto Augusto perpetuo et Marco Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari\n
EDCS-07901089.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus Perpetuus and to Marcus Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar
EDCS-07901089.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, Caesar Philip the Younger, dating to their joint rule between 244 and 249 AD.

EDCS-07901090.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Valerio Constantino pientissimo Augusto\n
EDCS-07901090.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Valerius Constantinus, most dutiful Augustus
EDCS-07901090.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine I, dating to his reign from 307 to 337 AD.

EDCS-07901091.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Iulio Philippo Pio Felici Augusto et Marco I Iulio Philippo nobilissimo Caesari tribuniciae potestatis consuli\n
EDCS-07901091.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Julius Philippus Pius Felix Augustus and to Marcus Julius Philippus, the most noble Caesar, vested with tribunician power and consulship
EDCS-07901091.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Philip the Arab and his son, Caesar Philip the Younger, dating to their joint rule between 244 and 249 AD.

EDCS-07901092.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Marcus Piavonius Victorinus Pius Felix\n
EDCS-07901092.translation: Emperor Caesar Marcus Piavonius Victorinus Pius Felix
EDCS-07901092.summary: This inscription is a fragment honoring the emperor Victorinus, dating to his reign between 269 and 271 AD.

EDCS-07901093.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto\n
EDCS-07901093.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus.
EDCS-07901093.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great, likely from the 4th century CE.

EDCS-07901095.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Mausaeus Carausius, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus; To Flavius Valerius Constantinus, Most Noble Caesar.
EDCS-07901095.summary: This inscription honors two Roman emperors, Carausius and Constantine, dating to the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-07901096.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Galerio Valerio Maximiano Pio Felici \n
EDCS-07901096.translation: To Emperor Caesar Galerius Valerius Maximianus, Pious, Fortunate.
EDCS-07901096.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Maximian, likely from the late 3rd or early 4th century CE.

EDCS-07901097.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Antoninus Gordianus.
EDCS-07901097.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901098.text_edition: \n Domino Nostro Imperatori Marco Antonio Gordiano Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-07901098.translation: To Our Lord Emperor Marcus Antoninus Gordianus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-07901098.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Gordian III, likely from the mid-3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901099.text_edition: \n Imperatori Marco Piavonio Victorino patri patriae Augusto\n
EDCS-07901099.translation: To Emperor Marcus Piavontius Victorinus, Father of the Fatherland, Augustus.
EDCS-07901099.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Emperor Victorinus, likely from the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901101.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto Arabico Adiabenico Parthico Maximo Britannico Maximo tribuniciae potestatis XVI consuli IIII imperatori II Gaio Iulio Marco legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-07901101.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Antoninus Augustus, Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered, the Greatest in Arabia, in Adiabene, in Parthia, the Greatest in Britain, holding tribunician power for the 16th time, consul for the 4th time, emperor for the 2nd time; [dedicated by] Gaius Julius Marcus, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul.
EDCS-07901101.summary: This inscription records a dedication by a legate to Emperor Caracalla, likely from the early 3rd century CE, noting his numerous titles and powers.

EDCS-07901102.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Alexandro Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli patri patriae curante Claudio Xenophonte legato Augusti pro praetore milia passuum XIIII\n
EDCS-07901102.translation: To Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus, Pontifex Maximus, holding tribunician power for the 2nd time, Father of the Fatherland; twelve and a half miles [of road] maintained by Claudius Xenophon, imperial legate with the power of a proconsul.
EDCS-07901102.summary: This inscription records a road measurement by Claudius Xenophon, legate to Emperor Severus Alexander, likely from the early 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901103.text_edition: \n Marcus Aurelius Probus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus\n
EDCS-07901103.translation: Marcus Aurelius Probus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus.
EDCS-07901103.summary: This inscription is a titulature for Emperor Probus, likely from the late 3rd century CE.

EDCS-07901104.text_edition: \n nobilissimo Caesari nobilissimo Caesari Imperatori Caesari Maximiano Pio Felici Augusto Imperatori Caesari Maximino Pio Felici Augusto\n
EDCS-07901104.translation: To the Most Noble Caesar, to the Most Noble Caesar, Emperor Caesar Maximian, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus; To Emperor Caesar Maximinus, Pious, Fortunate, Augustus.
EDCS-07901104.summary: This inscription honors two emperors, Maximian and Maximinus, likely from the early 4th century CE.

EDCS-07901105.text_edition: \n Imperator Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantinus Pius Felix Invictus Augustus divi Constanti\n
EDCS-07901105.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantine, Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Augustus, son of the divine Constantius.
EDCS-07901105.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Constantine the Great, likely from the early 4th century CE, listing his imperial titles.

EDCS-07901106.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici nobilissimo Caesari divi Constanti Pii Augusti filio\n
EDCS-07901106.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantine, Pious, Fortunate, Most Noble Caesar, son of the divine Constantius Pius Augustus.
EDCS-07901106.summary: This is a dedication to Constantine as Caesar (before he became Augustus), likely from the early 4th century CE, highlighting his lineage and titles.

EDCS-07901109.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Alexandro Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli patri patriae curante Claudio Xenephonte legato Augusti pro praetore milia passuum XVIII\n
EDCS-07901109.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the second time, consul, father of the fatherland, by the care of Claudius Xenophon, imperial legate with praetorian authority, eighteen miles.
EDCS-07901109.summary: This is a milestone inscription from the reign of Emperor Severus Alexander (222-235 CE), recording distance and the official responsible for its erection.

EDCS-07901110.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Numerian, Most Noble Caesar.
EDCS-07901110.summary: This is a dedication to Caesar Numerian, likely from the late 3rd century CE, recognizing his imperial rank.

EDCS-07901111.translation: Born for the good of the state.
EDCS-07901111.summary: This is a short, commendatory phrase, likely from an honorific inscription of an unknown individual, emphasizing their civic virtue.

EDCS-07901113.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio nobilissimo Caesari divi\n
EDCS-07901113.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Flavius Valerius Constantine, Pious, Most Noble Caesar, son of the divine.
EDCS-07901113.summary: This is a dedication to Constantine as Caesar, likely from the early 4th century CE, noting his imperial titles and divine parentage.

EDCS-07901114.text_edition: \n Imperator Diocletianus Pius Felix Augustus s<e=I>mper senior \n
EDCS-07901114.translation: Emperor Diocletian, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, senior emperor.
EDCS-07901114.summary: This inscription names Emperor Diocletian, Pious, Fortunate Augustus, and identifies him as the senior emperor, likely from his reign (284-305 CE).

EDCS-08100596.text_edition: \n Domine Neptune tibi dono hominem qui |(solidum) involavit Muconi et argentiolos sex ideo dono nomina qui decepit si mascel si femina si puuer sic si puuella sic ideo dono tibi Niske sic et Neptuno vitam valitudinem sanguem eius qui conscius fueris sic eius deceptionis animus qui hoc involavit et qui conscius fuerit ut eum decipias furem qui hoc involavit sanguem eiius sic consumas et decipias domine Neptune\n
EDCS-08100596.translation: Lord Neptune, to you I dedicate the person who stole a solidus and six silver pieces; therefore I dedicate the names of those who deceived [me]: whether man, whether woman, whether boy, thus; whether girl, thus. Therefore I dedicate to you, Niske, and to Neptune, his life, health, his blood. Whoever will have been aware, thus, of his deception, the soul of him who stole this, and whoever will have been aware, may you destroy him and deceive him, Lord Neptune, consume his blood, thus, and deceive the thief who stole this.
EDCS-08100596.summary: This is a curse tablet, likely from a provincial context, invoking Neptune and another deity (Niske) to punish a thief and those who know about the theft, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08100599.summary: This is a very brief inscription, possibly a dedication or a wish, related to eternal affection or love, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08100601.text_edition: \n Calpurni equitis TR |(denarium) I Arunti optionis\n
EDCS-08100601.translation: Calpurnius, a cavalryman, [paid] one denarius; Aruntius, a subordinate officer.
EDCS-08100601.summary: This inscription records a payment of one denarius made by Calpurnius, a cavalryman, and mentions Aruntius, likely an official, possibly related to a transaction or a record of services in the Roman army.

EDCS-08100620.translation: On the thirteenth day before the Kalends of November, in the consulship of Caelius...
EDCS-08100620.summary: This inscription records a date, likely for a public or private event, in the consular year of Caelius.

EDCS-08300451.text_edition: \n ------ Pio] Felici Augusto res publica Belgarum posuit\n
EDCS-08300451.translation: ...to the Pious and Fortunate Emperor, the state of the Belgae erected this.
EDCS-08300451.summary: This fragment records a dedication by the people of the Belgae to an emperor, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08400372.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Valerius Aurelius veteranus legionis XX V V Iulius \n
EDCS-08400372.translation: To the departed spirits: Lucius Valerius Aurelius, veteran of Legio XX Valeria Victrix...
EDCS-08400372.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Valerius Aurelius, a veteran of the Twentieth Legion, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08400373.text_edition: \n Aenum meum qui levavit exconfictus est templo Sulis dono si mulier si baro si servus si liber si pu<er=RE> si puella et qui hoc fecerit sanguinem suum in ips<um=MV> aen<um=MV> fundat Dono si mulier si baro si servus si liber si puer si puella eum latronem qui rem ipsam involavit deus inveniat \n
EDCS-08400373.translation: If anyone steals my bronze object, be they man or woman, slave or free, boy or girl, and if he does this, may his own blood pour onto him and the bronze. May the god find the thief who stole this object. May the god find the thief who stole this object, be they man or woman, slave or free, boy or girl.
EDCS-08400373.summary: This inscription is a curse against anyone who steals a bronze object dedicated to the temple of Sulis, likely a votive or protective inscription.

EDCS-08400374.summary: This inscription appears to record two personal names, possibly a dedication or ownership mark, originating from Roman Britain.

EDCS-08400375.text_edition: \n Louernisca donat eum qui sive vir isive femina sive puer sive puella qui ina sortium involaverit\n
EDCS-08400375.translation: Louernisca dedicates this object to him who, whether man or woman, boy or girl, steals it in a lottery.
EDCS-08400375.summary: This inscription is a curse or warning associated with an object dedicated by Louernisca, possibly related to a lottery or theft.

EDCS-08400376.text_edition: \n RI MITRPIO in (denariis III milibus cuius demediam sic partem tibi ut ita illum sic exigas a Vassicillo pecomini filio et uxore sua quoniam rtussum quod illi de hospitiolo meo alaverint sic nec illis permittas sanitatem nec bibere nec manducare nec dormire nec natos sanos habeant nessi hanc rem meam ad fanum tuum attulerint iteratis precibus te rogo ut ab ipsis nominibus inimicorum meorum hoc pertussum recipi perveniat\n
EDCS-08400376.translation: I beg you, by the names of my enemies, to have this pierced item received at your shrine: for 3,000 denarii, of which you will exact half from Vassicillus, son of Pecominus, and his wife, because they have brought me something from my small hospitium; may they not be permitted health, nor to drink, nor to eat, nor to sleep, nor to have healthy children, unless they bring this thing of mine to your shrine, through repeated prayers, that this piercing may be received from their very names.
EDCS-08400376.summary: This lengthy inscription is a complex curse and plea to a deity to recover stolen property, invoking enemies and detailing severe penalties for the thieves.

EDCS-08400377.translation: From the century of Mali Crassus, Lucilius Sabinianus.
EDCS-08400377.summary: This inscription likely records the ownership or assignment of property or a plot of land to Lucilius Sabinianus within the centuria of Mali Crassus, typical of land distribution in Roman military or colonial contexts.

EDCS-08400378.summary: This is a fragmented inscription likely recording a personal name and patronymic, possibly part of a funerary or public monument.

EDCS-08500431.text_edition: \n Primurudem Basilia donat in templum Martis anilum sic argenteum si servus si liber medius fuerit vel aliquid de hoc noverit ut sanguine et liminibus sic ob sic omnibus sic membris sic configatur vel etiam intestinis excomesis omnibus habeat is qui anilum sic involavit sic vel qui medius fuerit\n
EDCS-08500431.translation: Primurudem Basilia dedicates this silver ring to the temple of Mars. If a slave or free person is involved, or knows anything about this, may they be fastened with blood and thresholds, and all their limbs, or may the entrails be eaten out of all who stole the ring or were involved.
EDCS-08500431.summary: This inscription is a curse against anyone who steals a silver ring dedicated to the temple of Mars, detailing severe divine retribution.

EDCS-08500432.text_edition: \n Cunsa Maria Docimedis Vendibedis Sedebelia Cunsus Severiaanus Seni<i>la\n
EDCS-08500432.translation: Cunsa Maria, daughter of Docimedis, daughter of Vendibedis, of the Sedebelia tribe, and Cunus Severianus, her husband.
EDCS-08500432.summary: This inscription records the names of a couple, likely a dedication on a tomb, from the Sedebelia tribe.

EDCS-08500433.text_edition: \n Execro sic qui involaverit sic qui Deomiorix de hosipitio suo perdiderit sic quicumque reus deus illum inveniat sanguine et vitae sic suae sic illud redemat sic\n
EDCS-08500433.translation: Cursed be he who steals this, cursed be he who removes Deomiorix from his hospice, cursed be any god who finds the guilty person and makes him atone with his own blood and life. So be it.
EDCS-08500433.summary: This is a curse inscription warning against theft or removal of a person named Deomiorix from a place of refuge, likely on a tomb or boundary marker.

EDCS-08500435.text_edition: \n Victorinus Talipieinus Minantius Victorianus Compepedita Valaune<c=IC>us aBelia \n
EDCS-08500435.translation: Victorinus Talipieinus, Minantius Victorianus, Compepedita, Valaucus, and Abelia.
EDCS-08500435.summary: This inscription lists five personal names, possibly donors or individuals associated with a monument or funerary context.

EDCS-08600448.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae perpetuitati Aureliiae Marcianae quae vixit nnos X menses dies Aurelius decurio coloniae RTI eius faciundum curavit\n
EDCS-08600448.translation: To the spirits of the departed and to the eternal memory of Aurelia Marciana, who lived 10 years, months, days. Aurelius, a decurion of the colony, arranged for this to be made for her.
EDCS-08600448.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Aurelia Marciana, detailing her age and commissioned by her husband, Aurelius, a decurion.

EDCS-08600449.summary: This inscription records the name of Caius Aceronius Fur, likely a dedication or funerary marker.

EDCS-08600451.summary: This is a short dedication to the goddess Sulis Minerva.

EDCS-08600455.text_edition: \n Deae Suli donavi argentiolos sex quos perdidi a nominibus infrascriptis deae exactura est Senicianus sic et Saturniinus sed et Anniola carta picta perscripta Anniola Senicianus sic Saturninus\n
EDCS-08600455.translation: To the goddess Sulis, I donated six silver pieces, which were lost. The goddess will exact them from the names listed below: Senicianus, and Saturninus, and also Anniola, written on a painted sheet. Anniola, Senicianus, and Saturninus.
EDCS-08600455.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Sulis, involving a list of individuals from whom lost silver offerings are to be recovered.

EDCS-08600456.text_edition: \n Severa Dracontius Spectatus Innocentius Senicio Candidianus Applicius Belator Surilla Austus Carinianus\n
EDCS-08600456.translation: Severa, Dracontius, Spectatus, Innocentius, Senicio, Candidianus, Applicius, Belator, Surilla, Austus, Carinianus.
EDCS-08600456.summary: This inscription lists eleven personal names, possibly donors or individuals associated with a monument or funerary context.

EDCS-08600457.text_edition: \n Docilianus Bruceri deae sanctissimae Suli devoveo eum qui caracellam sic meam involaverit si vir si femina si servus si liber ut um dea Sulis maximo letum adigat nec ei somnum permittat nec natos nec nascentes donec caracallam meam ad templum sui numinis pertulerit\n
EDCS-08600457.translation: Docilianus, I vow to the most holy goddess Sulis that he who steals my cloak, whether man or woman, slave or free, may the goddess Sulis inflict the greatest death upon them and permit them no sleep, neither born nor yet to be born, until they bring my cloak to the temple of her divinity.
EDCS-08600457.summary: This is a curse inscription concerning a stolen cloak dedicated to the goddess Sulis, with a vow for retribution against the thief.

EDCS-08600458.text_edition: \n Uricalus Docilosa uxor sua Docilis filius suus et Docilina Decentinus frater suus Alogiosa nomina aeorum qui iuraverunt qui iuraverunt ad fontem deae Sulis pridie Idus Apriles quicumque illic periuraverit deae Suli facias illum sanguine suo illud satisfacere\n
EDCS-08600458.translation: Uricalus, Docilosa his wife, Docilis his son, and Docilina, Decentinus his brother, and Alogiosa. The names of those who swore at the fountain of the goddess Sulis the day before the Ides of April. Whoever swears falsely there, may the goddess Sulis make him satisfy it with his own blood.
EDCS-08600458.summary: This inscription records names of individuals who swore an oath at the fountain of Sulis and includes a curse for those who swear falsely.

EDCS-08600459.summary: A short dedication, likely to a deity, mentioning names, possibly from the Imperial period.

EDCS-08600461.text_edition: \n Petitio ro<go=VE> te Victoria vind Cun Minici Cunomolius Minervina u<x=SS>or Cunitius servus Senovara u<x=SS>or Lavidendus servus Mattonius servus Catinius Exsactoris fundo eo Methianus dono amicus TPIASV GINENINVSV gienusus \n
EDCS-08600461.translation: A petition to you, Victoria, daughter of Cunominius, from Cunitius, slave of Minervina, and Lavidendus, slave of Cunitius, and Mattonius, slave of Catinius, to hand over the property of Exsactoris to Methianus, a friend. TPIASV GINENINVSV (names) presented this gift.
EDCS-08600461.summary: A petition concerning property transfer involving several named individuals, some identified as slaves, possibly from the late Roman Republic or early Imperial period.

EDCS-08600464.text_edition: \n Seu gentilis seu Christianus quaecumque sic utrum vir utrum mulier utrum puer utrum puella utrum servus utrum liber mihi Anniano mantutene sic de bursa mea sex argenteos furaverit tu domina dea ab ipso perexige eos si mihi per fraudem aliquam INDEP REGSTVM dederit nec sic ipsi dona sed ut sanguinem suum reputes qui mihi hoc inrogaverit Postumianus Pisso Locinna Alauna Materna Gunsula Cnadidina sic Eutichius sic Peregrinus Latinus Senicianus Avitianus Victor Scutrius Aessicunia Paltucca Calliopis Celerianus\n
EDCS-08600464.translation: Whether Gentile or Christian, whoever, be it man, woman, boy, girl, slave, or free, has stolen six silver coins from my purse, you, my lady goddess, exact them from him. If he has given me anything by fraud, not as a gift to himself, but consider it as his own blood that he has inflicted this upon me. Postumianus, Pisso, Locinna, Alauna, Materna, Gunsula, Cnadidina, Eutichius, Peregrinus, Latinus, Senicianus, Avitianus, Victor, Scutrius, Aessicunia, Paltucca, Calliopis, Celerianus (were present/witnessed).
EDCS-08600464.summary: A curse tablet invoking a goddess against a thief, outlining retribution for stolen money and fraudulent gifts, with a list of witnesses, likely from the 3rd-4th century CE.

EDCS-08600465.text_edition: \n Cupitius Verinus decessit anno XXXVIIII Tusco et Basso consulibus VII Kalendas Septembres\n
EDCS-08600465.translation: Cupitius Verinus died in his 39th year, in the consulship of Tuscus and Bassus, seven days before the Kalends of September.
EDCS-08600465.summary: A funerary epitaph recording the death of Cupitius Verinus, including his age and the consuls of the year, dated to 211 CE.

EDCS-08600466.text_edition: \n A Nase evehit Vrocsius fasciam et armillas captolare sic spectrum cufiam sic duas ocrias X vasa stagnea si mascel si <f=M>emina si puer si puella duas ocrias si vullueris sic factae sanguine suo ut illum requeratat sic Neptunus et amictus et cufiaet armillae denarii XV capetolare sic tunc sanguine fasciam tenet fur e carta supra scripta ratione \n
EDCS-08600466.translation: Nase was lifted by Vrocsius. A headband and armlets, a cap, two jars, and ten pewter vessels, whether male, female, boy, or girl, two jars. If you want, have it made with his blood, as he has sought it. Neptune and the clothing, cap, and armlets, fifteen denarii. The thief will hold the headband from the paper written above, according to the record.
EDCS-08600466.summary: An inscription that seems to record a vow or curse involving stolen goods, deities, and payment, possibly related to magic or ritual, from the Roman period.

EDCS-08600470.summary: A dedication to the deity Faunus, identified by the epithet Cranius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600471.summary: A dedication to the deity Faunus, identified by the epithet Medigenus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600472.summary: A dedication to the deity Faunus, identified by the epithet Narius, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600473.translation: To the god Faunus, as Saturn.
EDCS-08600473.summary: A dedication to the deity Faunus, equated with Saturn, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-08600474.summary: A short, general blessing or wish for good fortune, possibly an epitaph or a general inscription, from the Roman period.

EDCS-08600476.translation: May you live as a freeborn person.
EDCS-08600476.summary: This is a personal wish for a freeborn person, likely an epitaph or a simple inscription.

EDCS-08600477.summary: This is a personal wish for someone named Primigenia, likely an epitaph or a simple inscription.

EDCS-08600480.summary: This is a personal wish for someone named Perseverance, likely an epitaph or a simple inscription.

EDCS-08600482.summary: This is a personal wish for a good man, likely an epitaph or a simple inscription.

EDCS-08600483.summary: This is a dedication or inscription naming Q. Lucillianus Penecille, purpose unclear due to truncation.

EDCS-08600691.summary: This inscription contains initials, possibly belonging to a named individual or a group, purpose unclear.

EDCS-08600703.summary: This inscription lists personal names, likely an epitaph or a record of individuals.

EDCS-08600704.summary: This inscription appears to be a shortened or abbreviated form of the name Lucius.

EDCS-08900458.text_edition: \n Ollam d<e=I>ndrofororum sic Verulamensium Regillinus donavit \n
EDCS-08900458.translation: Regillinus donated the cauldron of the Verulamian dendrophori.
EDCS-08900458.summary: This inscription records the donation of a cauldron by Regillinus to the dendrophori (tree-carriers) of Verulamium.

EDCS-08900459.text_edition: \n Herculi Augusto ob restitutionem templi a solo Proculus praefectus posuit idemque dedicavit\n
EDCS-08900459.translation: Proculus, the prefect, set up and also dedicated this in honor of Hercules Augustus for the rebuilding of the temple from its foundations.
EDCS-08900459.summary: This inscription records the rebuilding and rededication of a temple to Hercules Augustus by Proculus, a prefect.

EDCS-08900460.summary: This is a fragment of a dedication or honorific inscription, likely mentioning Titus Flavius Mar[...].

EDCS-08900463.text_edition: \n Cohors III centuria Marci Solli Iuliani\n
EDCS-08900463.translation: The third cohort, century of Marcus Sollius Iulianus.
EDCS-08900463.summary: This inscription records the unit and commander of a military cohort, likely from the Roman army.

EDCS-08901031.text_edition: \n Deo Mercurio Cenacus queritur de Vitalino et Natalino filio ipsius de iumento quod ei raptum est et rogat deum Mercurium ut nec ante sanitatem habeant nissi sic nissi repraesentaverint mihi iumentum quod rapuerunt et deo devotionem quam ipse ab his expostulaverit\n
EDCS-08901031.translation: To the god Mercury: Cenacus complains about Vitalinus and his son Natalinus concerning a beast of burden that was stolen from him, and he asks the god Mercury that they may not have health until they return to me the beast of burden that they stole, and [unless they give] to the god the devotion that he himself has demanded from them.
EDCS-08901031.summary: This is a vow or plea to the god Mercury from Cenacus, concerning a stolen beast of burden and requesting divine intervention for its return and for punishment of the thieves.

EDCS-08901032.text_edition: \n Commonitorium deo Mercurio Marti Silvano a Saturnina muliere de lintiamine quod amisit ut ille qui hoc circumvenit non ante laxetur nissi sic quando res supradictas ad fanum supradictum attulerit si vir si mulier si servus si liber deo supradicto tertiam partem donat ita ut exsigat sic istas res quae suprascripta sunt ACA quae sic perdidit deo Silvano tertia pars donatur ita ut hoc exsigat sic si vir si femina si servus si liber at\n
EDCS-08901032.translation: A warning to the god Mercury, Mars, Silvanus: Saturnina, a woman, concerning a lost necklace, that whoever defrauded [her] may not be released until, when they bring the aforementioned items to the aforementioned shrine, whether man, woman, slave, or free, they donate a third part to the aforementioned god, so that he may exact it, just as if man, woman, slave, or free, a third part is donated to the god Silvanus, so that he may exact it, whether man or woman, slave or free.
EDCS-08901032.summary: This is a vow or warning inscribed by Saturnina to Mercury, Mars, and Silvanus, regarding a lost necklace and stipulating a penalty for the thief, involving a donation of a third part of the recovered goods.

EDCS-08901036.text_edition: \n Discipulinae Imperatoris Hadriani Augusti ala Augusta ob virtutem appellata\n
EDCS-08901036.translation: To the Disciple of the Emperor Hadrian Augustus, the Augusta Ala, named for its valor.
EDCS-08901036.summary: This inscription honors a cavalry unit, the Augusta Ala, for its valor and its connection to Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-09200323.summary: This is a simple funerary or dedication inscription stating that Cassianus placed the monument.

EDCS-09200324.summary: This inscription likely records a period of service or a duration of 36 months, possibly related to a tomb or a military record, attributed to Matrona.

EDCS-09200327.text_edition: \n Tegulam Aulus Viducius Vero III consule legioni XX\n
EDCS-09200327.translation: A tile, Aulus Viducius Vero, in the third consulship, for the 20th legion.
EDCS-09200327.summary: This inscription is a maker's mark or record on a roof tile, identifying Aulus Viducius Vero and indicating it was made for the 20th legion during the consulship of 108 AD.

EDCS-09200328.text_edition: \n Matribus Buccio miles legionis XX Valeriae Victricis \n
EDCS-09200328.translation: To the Mothers, Buccio, soldier of the 20th Legion Valeria Victrix.
EDCS-09200328.summary: This is a dedication to the Matres (Mothers) by Buccio, a soldier of the 20th Legion Valeria Victrix.

EDCS-09200330.text_edition: \n Armea me docuit recte (tibi dicere cunctis\n
EDCS-09200330.translation: Armea taught me to speak rightly to all.
EDCS-09200330.summary: This is a short, possibly pedagogical or epigrammatic, inscription stating that Armea taught the writer to speak correctly.

EDCS-09200331.summary: A dedication to the god Mars Condatis.

EDCS-09300320.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo templum vetustate conlabsum sic Aquilinus Augusti libertus et Mercator et Audax et Graecus restituerunt\n
EDCS-09300320.translation: Aquilinus, freedman of Augustus, and Mercator, Audax, and Graecus restored this temple to Jupiter Optimus Maximus, which had collapsed with age.
EDCS-09300320.summary: A dedication recording the restoration of a temple to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by Aquilinus (a freedman of Augustus) and others.

EDCS-09300321.text_edition: \n In honorem domus divinae Marcus Martiannius Pulcher vir clarissimus legatus Auggustorum pro praetore templum Isidis ctis vetustate collabsum sic restitui praecepit\n
EDCS-09300321.translation: In honor of the divine house, Marcus Martiannius Pulcher, most distinguished man and governor-legate of the emperors, ordered this temple of Isis, which had collapsed with age, to be restored.
EDCS-09300321.summary: A dedication recording the restoration of a temple to Isis, ordered by Marcus Martiannius Pulcher, governor-legate of the emperors, in honor of the imperial family.

EDCS-09300326.translation: Legio XX Valeria Victrix, fourth cohort.
EDCS-09300326.summary: An inscription identifying the fourth cohort of the twentieth legion, Valeria Victrix.

EDCS-09300331.translation: From the barrack of the Messoris unit.
EDCS-09300331.summary: An inscription identifying the barrack of the Messoris unit.

EDCS-09300333.translation: He set this up to the Holy God Vetiris.
EDCS-09300333.summary: A dedication to the holy god Vetiris.

EDCS-09301065.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Valeri Verecundi veterani legionis II Augustae vixit annos LXX Onerata coniugi fecit\n
EDCS-09301065.translation: To the Spirits of the Dead. Onerata made this for her husband Valerius Verecundus, a veteran of the Second Augustan Legion, who lived seventy years.
EDCS-09301065.summary: A funerary monument for Valerius Verecundus, a veteran of the Second Augustan Legion, erected by his wife Onerata.

EDCS-09301073.translation: Comitius to the numina of the emperors.
EDCS-09301073.summary: A dedication to the divine powers of the emperors by Comitius.

EDCS-09301075.text_edition: \n Quinti Iuli Martini stactum Quinti Iuli Martini diapsoricum Quinti Iuli Martini penicilli Quinti Iuli Martini crocodes ad aspritudinem\n
EDCS-09301075.translation: Stacte of Quintus Julius Martinus, diapsoricum of Quintus Julius Martinus, penicilli of Quintus Julius Martinus, crocus of Quintus Julius Martinus for roughness.
EDCS-09301075.summary: A list of pharmaceutical or cosmetic products made by Quintus Julius Martinus.

EDCS-09301076.text_edition: \n Gaius Baebius Cai filius Niger pedes XII\n
EDCS-09301076.translation: Gaius Baebius Niger, son of Gaius, 12 feet.
EDCS-09301076.summary: An inscription recording the measurement of 12 feet by Gaius Baebius Niger.

EDCS-09301077.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Marco Mettio Primitivo Flavia Rhodine coniugi bene merenti fecit\n
EDCS-09301077.translation: To the Divine Shades. Flavia Rhodine made this for her deserving husband, Marcus Mettius Primitivus.
EDCS-09301077.summary: A funerary epitaph for Marcus Mettius Primitivus, dedicated by his wife Flavia Rhodine.

EDCS-09301082.text_edition: \n Divo Antonino Magno DVS IRMO HON D D \n
EDCS-09301082.translation: To the Divine Antoninus Magnus. [---] D D.
EDCS-09301082.summary: An inscription honoring the deified Emperor Antoninus Magnus, with unclear abbreviations.

EDCS-09301085.text_edition: \n Legionis VI victricis piae fidelis vexillatio fecit\n
EDCS-09301085.translation: A detachment of the Sixth Legion Victrix, the dutiful and loyal, made this.
EDCS-09301085.summary: A monument erected by a vexillation of the Sixth Legion Victrix, likely honoring a military achievement or individual.

EDCS-09301086.text_edition: \n Deo Neptuno Reginius Iustinus tribunus votum libens solvit merito\n
EDCS-09301086.translation: To the god Neptune. Tribune Reginius Iustinus willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-09301086.summary: A votive offering to Neptune made by the military tribune Reginius Iustinus.

EDCS-09301088.text_edition: \n Servandus Coloniae Claudiae Augustae Agrippinensis fecit\n
EDCS-09301088.translation: Servandus made this for Colonia Claudia Augusta Agrippinensis.
EDCS-09301088.summary: An inscription recording a dedication made by Servandus for the colony of Cologne (Agrippinensis).

EDCS-09301090.text_edition: \n cto aram fecit Saturninus voto soluto pro se et suis\n
EDCS-09301090.translation: Saturninus made an altar, having fulfilled his vow for himself and his family.
EDCS-09301090.summary: A dedication of an altar by Saturninus, fulfilling a vow for his family.

EDCS-09301091.translation: Quintus Lusius Sabinianus, procurator of the Emperor.
EDCS-09301091.summary: The inscription records the name and title of Quintus Lusius Sabinianus, an imperial procurator.

EDCS-09301093.text_edition: \n Legionis XX Valeriae victricis Quint fecit\n
EDCS-09301093.translation: Quintus of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix made this.
EDCS-09301093.summary: A dedication made by Quintus from the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix.

EDCS-09400464.text_edition: \n tubulos DL feci quassiavi sic LI\n
EDCS-09400464.translation: I made 550 tubes; I hammered them; 51.
EDCS-09400464.summary: An inscription possibly related to construction or engineering, recording the quantity of pipes made and worked on.

EDCS-09400469.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelius Ingennus vixit annis VI EX MESEX Aurelius Euticianus parens pos<s>uit\n
EDCS-09400469.translation: To the Divine Shades. Aurelius Ingennus lived 6 years and [---] months. Aurelius Euticianus, his parent, placed this.
EDCS-09400469.summary: A funerary epitaph for a young child, Aurelius Ingennus, dedicated by his parent Aurelius Euticianus.

EDCS-09400470.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Petronius vixit annos XXXX Comitinus possuit sic\n
EDCS-09400470.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Lucius Petronius lived for 40 years. Comitinus erected this.
EDCS-09400470.summary: A funerary epitaph for Lucius Petronius, erected by Comitinus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400474.summary: A very brief inscription recording the acquisition of property by Quietus Remus, probably Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400476.text_edition: \n Dio sic Mercurio dono tibi negotium Aetternae sic et ipsam nec sit invidia mei Timothneo sic sanguine suo Dono tibi Mercurius sic aliam sic negotium NAVIN NII MINSANGuine SVO\n
EDCS-09400476.translation: Dio, I give you this task, Mercury, and Aeternus as well, and may it not be by my envy. Timothneo, with his own blood, I give you this other task, Mercury.
EDCS-09400476.summary: A peculiar inscription, possibly a votive offering or a magical spell, addressed to Mercury, invoking Dio and Timothneo.

EDCS-09400477.summary: A simple inscription identifying Rubrius Carinus, possibly indicating his origin or a nickname, likely Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400478.text_edition: \n Deo Abandino Vatiaucus de suo dedit\n
EDCS-09400478.translation: Vatiaucus dedicated this to the god Abandinus from his own resources.
EDCS-09400478.summary: A dedication to the god Abandinus by Vatiaucus, likely from a provincial Roman context.

EDCS-09400487.text_edition: \n Imperatore Vespasiano VIIII Tito Imperatore VII consulibus Cnaeo Iulio Agricola legato Augusti pro praetore\n
EDCS-09400487.translation: During the consulship of Emperor Vespasian for the 9th time and Emperor Titus for the 7th time, Gnaeus Julius Agricola, legate of the emperor, pro-praetor.
