EDCS-09400487.summary: A dating clause mentioning consular years under Vespasian and Titus, and the governor Gnaeus Julius Agricola, placing it in the Flavian period (AD 79-81).

EDCS-09400490.text_edition: \n Genio loci Quintus Crepereius Marcus posuit\n
EDCS-09400490.translation: Quintus Crepereius Marcus erected this to the Genius of the place.
EDCS-09400490.summary: A dedication to the Genius Loci (spirit of the place) by Quintus Crepereius Marcus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400500.translation: Senilis erected this for the Old Gods.
EDCS-09400500.summary: A dedication to the 'Old Gods' by Senilis, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400501.translation: Longinus erected this for the Old Gods.
EDCS-09400501.summary: A dedication to the 'Old Gods' by Longinus, likely from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-09400502.text_edition: \n Deo Mogunti et Genio loci Lupulus votum solvit merito\n
EDCS-09400502.translation: Lupulus, as is fitting, fulfilled his vow to the god Moguntus and the Genius of the place.
EDCS-09400502.summary: A votive inscription by Lupulus to the god Moguntus and the Genius Loci, likely from a provincial Roman context, perhaps Germania Superior.

EDCS-09400508.translation: The century of Gellus Philippus of the Sixth Cohort.
EDCS-09400508.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the century of Gellus Philippus, belonging to the Sixth Cohort, likely a boundary marker or a unit identification.

EDCS-09400509.translation: The century of Nas Bassus of the First Cohort.
EDCS-09400509.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the century of Nas Bassus, belonging to the First Cohort, likely a boundary marker or a unit identification.

EDCS-09400510.text_edition: \n Cohortis I centuria Pompei Rufi principis primi\n
EDCS-09400510.translation: The century of Pompeius Rufus, first centurion.
EDCS-09400510.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the century of Pompeius Rufus, identifying him as the first centurion, likely a boundary marker or unit identification.

EDCS-09400512.translation: The Sixth Legion Victrix, pious and faithful.
EDCS-09400512.summary: This inscription is a dedication or epithet for the Sixth Legion Victrix, highlighting its loyalty and devotion, likely inscribed on military equipment or a standard.

EDCS-09400513.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the century of Cr..., likely a boundary marker or unit identification where the full name is lost.

EDCS-09400514.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the century of Candidus Crescens, likely a boundary marker or unit identification.

EDCS-09401461.text_edition: \n Polychroni dia choles Aureli Polychroni dia dya lithon post Polychron dia lepidos Aureli Polychroni cycnum at sic inpetum\n
EDCS-09401461.translation: Polychronus suffering from bile, of Aurelius Polychronus, suffering from stones, after Polychronus the skillful, of Aurelius Polychronus, a swan, and thus in attack...
EDCS-09401461.summary: This complex and possibly humorous inscription seems to be a playful or metaphorical description related to medical conditions or personal attributes associated with individuals named Polychronus, likely found on a tomb or monument.

EDCS-09700632.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Antoni Gargiliani equo publico ex praefecto legionis VI victricis annorum LVI mensium VI Claudius Florentinus decurio gener eius\n
EDCS-09700632.translation: To the Spirits of the Underworld. Antonius Gargilianus, of equestrian rank, former prefect of the Sixth Legion Victrix, aged 56 years and 6 months. Claudius Florentinus, his decurion and son-in-law, dedicated this.
EDCS-09700632.summary: This tomb inscription records the death of Antonius Gargilianus, a former equestrian prefect of the Sixth Legion Victrix, and is dedicated by his son-in-law, Claudius Florentinus, dating to the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-09700635.translation: Of the century of Covinus of the Sixth Legion Victrix.
EDCS-09700635.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the century of Covinus of the Sixth Legion Victrix, likely a boundary marker or unit identification.

EDCS-09700637.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Tito Aelio Hadriano Antonino Augusto Pio patri patriae vexillatio legionis XX Valeriae victricis fecit per pedum III\n
EDCS-09700637.translation: The vexillation of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix made this for the Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, father of the country, through three feet.
EDCS-09700637.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a vexillation (detachment) of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix to Emperor Antoninus Pius, likely marking a construction project or boundary during his reign (138-161 AD).

EDCS-09700638.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Baetasiorum civium Romanorum cui praeest Publicius Maternus praefectus curam agente Iulio Candido |(centurione) legionis I Italicae votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-09700638.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, the first cohort of Baetasii, citizens of Rome, commanded by Gaius Publicius Maternus, prefect, with Gaius Julius Candidus, centurion of the First Italian Legion, taking responsibility, gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-09700638.summary: This is a military dedication to Jupiter by the first cohort of Baetasii under their prefect, Publicius Maternus, and supervised by a centurion, likely from the 2nd century CE.

EDCS-09700986.text_edition: \n Silvano et Numini Augusti nostri Aurelius Pu\n
EDCS-09700986.translation: To Silvanus and the divine spirit of our Augustus, Aurelius Pu[...]
EDCS-09700986.summary: This is a partial dedication to the god Silvanus and the numen of the emperor by Aurelius Pu[...], likely dating to the imperial period.

EDCS-09700990.summary: This is a dedication to Catia Maria, possibly on a tomb or altar, from the Roman period.

EDCS-09700992.summary: This is a maker's inscription, likely for a statue or a building element, by Celsinus from the Roman period.

EDCS-09700993.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, possibly a dedication or ownership marker, referring to Publius Cipi[...], from the Roman period.

EDCS-09700994.summary: This is a maker's inscription, likely for a statue or a building element, by Maximinus from the Roman period.

EDCS-09700995.summary: This is a maker's inscription, likely for a statue or a building element, by Sabinianus from the Roman period.

EDCS-09700996.summary: This is a maker's inscription, likely for a statue or a building element, by Saguntinus Augustinus from the Roman period.

EDCS-09700998.summary: This is a fragmentary maker's inscription, likely for a statue or a building element, by [...]ndinus, from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701004.text_edition: \n Brigini Ing<e=F>nui liberti Brigini Ingenui liberti \n
EDCS-09701004.translation: To Briginus, freedman of Ingenuus; to Ingenuus, freedman of Briginus.
EDCS-09701004.summary: This inscription records a dedication or reciprocal relationship between two freedmen, Briginus and Ingenuus, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-09701008.summary: This is a dedication by Victor to Vienna, likely a local deity or personification.

EDCS-09701017.text_edition: \n Rei publicae Glevensium IIviri Publius Aelius F\n
EDCS-09701017.translation: Publius Aelius F., Duumvir of the Republic of the Glevensians.
EDCS-09701017.summary: This inscription records Publius Aelius F. as a Duumvir of the Roman community of Glevensium.

EDCS-09701519.text_edition: \n Dis et memoriae Nittiunis vixit annos XXXXV et menses VI Talio coniux titulum ponendum curavit\n
EDCS-09701519.translation: To the gods and for the memory of Nittiunis, who lived 45 years and 6 months. Her husband, Talio, saw to the placement of this monument.
EDCS-09701519.summary: This is a funerary inscription commemorating Nittiunis, who lived 45 years and 6 months, erected by her husband Talio.

EDCS-09701520.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Aurelia Cunovinda vixit annis \n
EDCS-09701520.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Aurelia Cunovinda lived for... years.
EDCS-09701520.summary: This is a partial funerary inscription for Aurelia Cunovinda.

EDCS-09701522.summary: This inscription records Aurelius Tata, identified by the honorific title V RQOPO (Sacred Orator).

EDCS-09701542.summary: This inscription likely refers to two individuals named Binio or members of a family group known as Biniones.

EDCS-09800193.text_edition: \n Deae Nemesi Sextius Marcianus |(centurio) ex visu\n
EDCS-09800193.translation: To the goddess Nemesis, Sextius Marcianus, centurion, in accordance with a vision.
EDCS-09800193.summary: This is a dedication to the goddess Nemesis by Sextius Marcianus, a centurion, made in response to a vision.

EDCS-09800196.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et memoriae Similiniae Verae Flavius Italicus coniugi karissimae faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-09800196.translation: To the spirits of the departed and for the memory of Similinia Vera, Flavius Italicus had this made for his dearest wife.
EDCS-09800196.summary: This is a funerary inscription by Flavius Italicus for his wife, Similinia Vera.

EDCS-09800197.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Soterae coniugi pientissim(a)e Iulius Senecianus ponendum pro condicione loci curavit\n
EDCS-09800197.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Julius Senecianus arranged for this monument to be placed for his most devoted wife, Sotera, according to the conditions of the place.
EDCS-09800197.summary: This funerary inscription records Julius Senecianus arranging for a monument for his wife Sotera, considering local circumstances.

EDCS-09800199.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dolicheno pro salute vexillationum legionis VI victricis et exercitus Germaniae utriusque |(curam) agente Marco Lollio Venatore |(centurione) legionis II Augustae votum solverunt libentes merito\n
EDCS-09800199.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Dolichenus, for the health of the detachments of the VI Victrix Legion and the army of both Germanies, with Marcus Lollio Venator, centurion of the II Augusta Legion, in charge, they gladly and deservedly fulfilled their vow.
EDCS-09800199.summary: This is a votive inscription to Jupiter Dolichenus by soldiers of the VI Victrix and German armies, overseen by a centurion of the II Augusta Legion, to fulfill a vow.

EDCS-09800202.summary: This inscription likely marks a boundary or property belonging to the century of Iulius Vale(r)ius, possibly dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-09800203.translation: Eppius, son of Con(n)itus, the centurion.
EDCS-09800203.summary: This text identifies Eppius, son of Con(n)itus, as a centurion, likely from the Roman army.

EDCS-09800212.summary: This is a fragment identifying a person named Publius Vinnius, possibly part of a larger dedication or epitaph.

EDCS-09800216.text_edition: \n feci RI ntirio rico filius inferas fecit\n
EDCS-09800216.translation: I made it. Ric(h)intirius, son of Rico, made the offerings.
EDCS-09800216.summary: This inscription records that Ric(h)intirius, son of Rico, created something for someone named Richintirius, possibly a funerary or votive monument.

EDCS-10701776.text_edition: \n Deae Suli Lucius Marcius Memor haruspex dedit dedicavit\n
EDCS-10701776.translation: Lucius Marcius Memor, the haruspex, gave and dedicated this to the goddess Sulis.
EDCS-10701776.summary: This votive inscription records the dedication of a gift by Lucius Marcius Memor, a haruspex (diviner), to the goddess Sulis, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-10701780.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Gaio Messio Quinto Troiano sic Decio Augusto \n
EDCS-10701780.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Gaius Messius Quintus Traianus Decius Augustus.
EDCS-10701780.summary: This is a dedication to the Roman Emperor Trajan Decius, indicating a public monument or building.

EDCS-10701781.text_edition: \n Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto\n
EDCS-10701781.translation: To Flavius Valerius Constantinus, the Pious, Fortunate, Unconquered Augustus.
EDCS-10701781.summary: This is an honorific inscription dedicated to the Roman Emperor Constantine I, likely from the Constantinian period.

EDCS-10701782.text_edition: \n Adalma Donatus L feliciter Lopiscallus Saturnino\n
EDCS-10701782.translation: Adalma and Donatus L... successfully to Lopiscallus Saturninus.
EDCS-10701782.summary: This is likely a funerary inscription, recording the successful journey or remembrance of Lopiscallus Saturninus by Adalma and Donatus.

EDCS-10701789.summary: This inscription identifies Mauri as a maker of eye-salves, possibly on a tombstone or a shop sign.

EDCS-10701798.summary: This inscription likely refers to a person named Virilis who is associated with Surinus, possibly a landowner or patron.

EDCS-11800716.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Casianio sic Latinianio sic Postimo sic Augusto Pio Felici res publica civitatis Carvetiorum\n
EDCS-11800716.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Cassianius Latinius Postumus Augustus, the pious, fortunate, the state of the province of the Carvetii.
EDCS-11800716.summary: This is a dedication by the province of the Carvetii to Emperor Postumus, likely from the 3rd century CE.

EDCS-11800930.summary: This is a very short inscription, possibly a dedication or ownership mark, mentioning Supertius and Patientia.

EDCS-11800943.text_edition: \n Titus Lusius Nymphius annorum XX hic situs est\n
EDCS-11800943.translation: Titus Lusius Nymphius is buried here, aged 20.
EDCS-11800943.summary: This is a tombstone inscription recording the name, age, and burial of Titus Lusius Nymphius.

EDCS-11800959.translation: Ursacius, may you live in Christ.
EDCS-11800959.summary: This is a Christian inscription, a short greeting or wish for Ursacius.

EDCS-11800974.summary: This inscription likely lists names, possibly indicating ownership, sponsorship, or a group of individuals.

EDCS-11800975.text_edition: \n turma Genialis ordei modios XXXXII frumenti modios XVIII turma Agilis ordei modios XXXIX frumenti modios XIIX turma Albini ordei modios XXXXV frumenti modios XVIII turma Gentilis ordei modios XXXIII frumenti modios XV turma Peculiaris ordei modios XXXIII frumenti modios XV turma Pacati ordei modios XXXIX frumenti modios XV turma ordei modios XXXVI frumenti modios XV turma ordei modios LX frumenti modios XIIX turma Sei ordei modios XXXIII frumenti modios XV turma Sodalis ordei modios XXXVI frumenti modios XV turma Docilis ordei modios XXXXV frumenti modios XVIII turma Sollemnis ordei modios XXXXV frumenti modios XVIII turma Mansueti ordei modios XXXXII frumenti modios XIIX turma Martialis ordei modios XXX frumenti modios XV turma Genialis ordei modios XXXIX frumenti modios XIIX turma Victoris ordei modios XXXXV frumenti modios XIIX Pacato decurioni ordei modios XXVII summa ordei modios DCLXIX frumenti modios CCLXVII\n
EDCS-11800975.translation: Turma Genialis: 42 modii of barley, 18 modii of wheat. Turma Agilis: 39 modii of barley, 12 modii of wheat. Turma Albini: 45 modii of barley, 18 modii of wheat. Turma Gentilis: 33 modii of barley, 15 modii of wheat. Turma Peculiaris: 33 modii of barley, 15 modii of wheat. Turma Pacati: 39 modii of barley, 15 modii of wheat. Turma: 36 modii of barley, 15 modii of wheat. Turma: 60 modii of barley, 18 modii of wheat. Turma Sei: 33 modii of barley, 15 modii of wheat. Turma Sodalis: 36 modii of barley, 15 modii of wheat. Turma Docilis: 45 modii of barley, 18 modii of wheat. Turma Sollemnis: 45 modii of barley, 18 modii of wheat. Turma Mansueti: 42 modii of barley, 18 modii of wheat. Turma Martialis: 30 modii of barley, 15 modii of wheat. Turma Genialis: 39 modii of barley, 18 modii of wheat. Turma Victoris: 45 modii of barley, 18 modii of wheat. Pacatus the decurion: 27 modii of barley. Total: 669 modii of barley, 267 modii of wheat.
EDCS-11800975.summary: This inscription is a detailed record of grain (barley and wheat) distribution, organized by turmae (cavalry troops or divisions) and totals, likely for military or civic supply purposes.

EDCS-11800976.text_edition: \n Docilis Augurino praefecto suo salutem ita ut praecepisti lanciariorum quibus lanciae deessent omnia nomina subiecimus aut qui lancias pugnatorias aut qui minores subarmales aut qui gladia instituta non habebant turma senioris Genialis Verecundus lanciam pugnatoriam item subarmales duas Turma Albini rma arda turma Docca subarmales duas turma Docilis Pastor subarmales duas Felicio lanciam pugnatoriam turma Sollemnis atus lanciam pugnatoriam item subarmales duas turma Mansueti s lanciam pugnatoriam Victorinus rae lanciam pugnatoriam turma Martialis so lanciam pugnatoriam turma Genialis Festus subarmales duas Maior subarmales duas subarmales lanciam pugnatoriam turma Victoris bene valeas Augurine cum tuis domine\n
EDCS-11800976.translation: Docilis to his prefect Augurinus, greetings. As you ordered, we have submitted all the names of those who were missing lances, or who did not have fighting lances, or who had smaller sub-armal lances, or who did not have the prescribed swords: Turma of the elder Genialis: Verecundus, a fighting lance. Turma of Albinus: [names missing], arms. Turma of Docca: two sub-armal lances. Turma of Docilis Pastor: two sub-armal lances. Felicio: a fighting lance. Turma of Sollemnis: [name missing]atus, a fighting lance, and two sub-armal lances. Turma of Mansuetus: [name missing]s, a fighting lance. Victorinus: [name missing]rae, a fighting lance. Turma of Martialis: [name missing]so, a fighting lance. Turma of Genialis Festus: two sub-armal lances. Maior: two sub-armal lances, a fighting lance. Turma of Victoris: Be well, Augurinus, with your people, my lord.
EDCS-11800976.summary: This is a report from Docilis to his prefect Augurinus listing soldiers' equipment, specifically focusing on lances and swords, likely for military record-keeping.

EDCS-11800977.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely the name of an individual, possibly on a tombstone or marking property.

EDCS-11800978.text_edition: \n Primus et Anoncletus Euphemio et Secundo et Rustico fratribus suiis sic salutem ecationem eupreos \n
EDCS-11800978.translation: Primus and Anoncletus to Euphemius, Secundus, and Rusticus, their brothers, greetings. The dedication of [illegible]...
EDCS-11800978.summary: This is a greeting and dedication from Primus and Anoncletus to their brothers, likely part of a personal letter or dedication.

EDCS-11800981.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription mentioning the name Vocontius, likely an individual's name within a larger text.

EDCS-11800982.summary: This inscription fragment likely refers to a brother named Crispus and is undated.

EDCS-11800984.text_edition: \n Ru Londini diutius quapropter tandem quo cohortales tardius superveniunt quibus mancipare debeo pedicas fficensu mat et pert\n
EDCS-11800984.translation: In London for a long time; therefore, finally, because the auxiliaries I should chain arrive too late, making a large quantity of shackles...
EDCS-11800984.summary: This inscription fragment, likely from London, concerns the delayed arrival of auxiliaries and the need for shackles, probably dating from the Roman occupation.

EDCS-11800987.summary: This inscription fragment mentions individuals named Vercono, Olloco, and Martianus, with its purpose and date unclear.

EDCS-11800988.summary: This personal note, likely a letter fragment, is a farewell from Scaeva and is undated.

EDCS-11800989.text_edition: \n salutabis Praesentem verbis meis et collegas omnes opto bene valeas Iuli \n
EDCS-11800989.translation: You will greet Praesens for me with my words and all his colleagues. I wish you well. Iuli...
EDCS-11800989.summary: This inscription fragment is a personal message from Iuli asking the recipient to greet Praesens and his colleagues, and is undated.

EDCS-11800990.translation: ...to Agricola, an auxiliary cavalryman of the Sebosian ala, singled out...
EDCS-11800990.summary: This inscription fragment likely commemorates Agricola, a distinguished cavalryman of the Sebosian ala, possibly dating from the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-11800991.summary: This inscription fragment mentions a name, Longinus, but provides no further context or date.

EDCS-11800992.summary: This inscription fragment is too short to provide a meaningful translation or summary, likely referring to someone present.

EDCS-11800993.text_edition: \n tradet scribere sero te frater Praesen[ti ------\n
EDCS-11800993.translation: You will hand over writing late, brother, to Praesens...
EDCS-11800993.summary: This inscription fragment appears to be a personal message about late delivery of writing to Praesens, from his brother, and is undated.

EDCS-11800994.translation: Farewell from Fulvius Natalis, your colleague.
EDCS-11800994.summary: This inscription fragment is a farewell message from Fulvius Natalis to his colleague, and is undated.

EDCS-12300252.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-12300252.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Nerva, Nerva Trajan Augustus Germanicus Dacicus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 7th time, imperator for the 4th time, father of the country, consul for the 5th time, has granted citizenship and the right to legal marriage to the cavalrymen and infantrymen serving in four alae and eleven cohorts, namely the 1st Thracian, 1st Pannonian Tampiana, 1st Gallic Sebosiana, 1st Spanish Vettones (Roman citizens), 1st Spanish, 1st Vangiones (miliaria), 1st Alpinorum, 1st Morinorum, 1st Cugernorum, 1st Baetasiorum, 1st Tungrorum (miliaria), 2nd Thracian, 3rd Bracaraugustanorom, 3rd Lingonum, and 4th Dalmatarum, who are in Britain under Lucius Neratius Marcellus, and who have completed twenty-five or more years of service, whose names are listed below, to them, their children, and their descendants, and to those who were unmarried at the time citizenship was granted, and to those who would marry later, but only one wife each. Dated on the 12th day before the Kalends of February, during the consulship of Manius Laberius Maximus (for the second time) and Quintus Glitius Atilius Agricola (for the second time). For the 1st Wing of Pannonians Tampiana, commanded by Gaius Valerius Celsus, to the decurion Reburrus, son of Severus, a Spaniard. Copied and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome.
EDCS-12300252.summary: This inscription records Emperor Trajan granting Roman citizenship and marriage rights to auxiliary soldiers serving in Britain who had completed long service, with specific details about the units and the date.

EDCS-12300274.translation: Emperor Caesar, son of the deified Trajan Parthicus, grandson of the deified Nerva, Trajan Hadrianus Augustus, chief priest, with tribunician power for the 8th time, consul for the 3rd time, proconsul, has granted citizenship and the right to legal marriage to the cavalrymen and infantrymen who served in six alae and twenty-one cohorts, namely the 1st Spanish Astur, 1st [unknown], 1st Thracian, Picentiana, [unknown], Petriana, 1st African (Roman citizens), 1st Spanish, 1st Frisiavones, 1st Hamiorum (archers), 1st Sunucorum, 1st Vangiones (miliaria), 1st Baetasiorum, 1st Dalmatarum, 1st Aquitanorum, 1st Menapiorum, 1st Ulpia Traiana Cugernorum (Roman citizens), 1st Fida Vardullorum (miliaria) (Roman citizens), 1st Batavorum, 1st Tungrorum, 2nd Lingonum, 2nd Astur, 2nd Pannoniorum, 2nd Nerviorum, 3rd Bracaraugustanorom, 3rd Nerviorum, and 6th Nerviorum, who are in Britain under Plotorus Nepos, having completed twenty-five or more years of service and were honorably discharged, to them, their children, and their descendants, and to those who were unmarried at the time citizenship was granted, and to those who would marry later, but only one wife each. Dated on the 16th day before the Kalends of [Unknown Month], during the consulship of Gaius Julius Gallus and Gaius Valerius Severus. For the 1st Cohort of Sunucorum, commanded by Marcus Iunius Claudianus, to the foot soldier CNTINT(?) Albani, son of the Sunucan. Copied and verified from a bronze tablet fixed in Rome on the wall behind the Temple of the Deified Augustus near Minerva.
EDCS-12300274.summary: This inscription records Emperor Hadrian granting Roman citizenship and marriage rights to auxiliary soldiers serving in Britain who had completed long service, detailing the numerous units involved and specifying the date and consuls.

EDCS-12800310.text_edition: \n Donatur deo Iovi Optimo Maximo ut exigat per mentem per memoriam per intus per intestinum per cor per medullas per venas per as si mascel si femina quivis involavit |denarios Cani Digni ut in corpore suo in brevi tempore pariat donatur deo esto decima pars eius pecuniae quam solverit\n
EDCS-12800310.translation: Given to the god Jupiter Optimus Maximus, so that he may extract through the mind, through memory, through the inside, through the intestines, through the heart, through the marrow, through the veins, through the arteries, whether male or female, whoever stole [currency], the tenth part of his money which he will pay shall be given to the god Canus Dignus, so that he may give birth in his body in a short time.
EDCS-12800310.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter, imploring the god to identify and punish a thief by causing them internal suffering, with a reward offered to the god from the recovered money.

EDCS-12800311.text_edition: \n Dis bonis Manibus Flavius Fuscinus emeritus ex ordinato vi<x=S>it annis LV Dis bonis Manibus Flavius Romanus actarius vixit nns XXXV in castris interfectus ab hostibus \n
EDCS-12800311.translation: To the Good Spirits of the Underworld. Flavius Fuscinus, a retired soldier, lived 55 years. To the Good Spirits of the Underworld. Flavius Romanus, a quartermaster, lived 35 years, killed in camp by the enemy.
EDCS-12800311.summary: This is a tombstone inscription honoring two deceased individuals, a retired soldier and a quartermaster, noting their ages and the latter's death in battle.

EDCS-12800319.text_edition: \n Silvanis et Quadruviis Caelestibus sacrum Vibia Pacata Flavi Verecundi |(centurionis) legionis VI victricis cum suis votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-12800319.translation: Sacred to the Silvan Deities and the Deities of the Crossroads. Vibia Pacata, wife of Flavius Verecundus, a centurion of the VI Victrix legion, with her family, willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-12800319.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Vibia Pacata to the Silvan and Crossroads deities, fulfilling a vow for herself and her family.

EDCS-12800320.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caio Flavio Valerio Constantino Pio Felici Invicto Augusto divi Constanti Pii Augusti filio\n
EDCS-12800320.translation: To the Emperor Gaius Flavius Valerius Constantine, the Pious, Fortunate, Invincible Emperor, son of the deified Constantius Pius Augustus.
EDCS-12800320.summary: This is an inscription dedicating something to Emperor Constantine the Great, praising his imperial titles and lineage.

EDCS-13302696.summary: This is a brief dedication to the goddess Minerva.

EDCS-13302697.translation: The 6th Cohort, the century of Faenius Alexander.
EDCS-13302697.summary: This inscription identifies a specific military unit, the 6th Cohort, and its century under Faenius Alexander.

EDCS-13302698.translation: The 6th Cohort, the century of Eppius Constans.
EDCS-13302698.summary: This inscription identifies a specific military unit, the 6th Cohort, and its century under Eppius Constans.

EDCS-13400106.text_edition: \n Matribus Domesticis Iulius Victor pro se et suis votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-13400106.translation: To the Matres Domesticæ (Mothers of the Household). Iulius Victor, for himself and his family, willingly, joyfully, and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-13400106.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Iulius Victor to the Matres Domesticæ for himself and his family.

EDCS-13400107.text_edition: \n Legio XX Valeria victrix cohors X centuria Flavi Norici\n
EDCS-13400107.translation: The 10th cohort of the 20th Valeria Victrix Legion, under the centuria of Flavius Noricus.
EDCS-13400107.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the 10th cohort of the 20th Valeria Victrix Legion, under a specific centurion, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13400108.translation: The 6th cohort, under the centuria of Caledon Secundus.
EDCS-13400108.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the 6th cohort, under a specific centurion, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13400109.text_edition: \n Aedem principiorum cum basilica subrio Rufino consule Fortunatus it\n
EDCS-13400109.translation: Fortunatus dedicated the temple of the principia and the basilica, under the consul Subrius Rufinus.
EDCS-13400109.summary: This inscription records the dedication of a temple and basilica by Fortunatus, under the consulship of Subrius Rufinus, likely in the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13400111.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Lucio Septimio Severo Pio Pertinaci Augusto et Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Antonino Pio Felici Augusto et Publio Septimio Getae nobilissimo Caesari Auggustis nnostris Imperatore Antonino II et Geta Caesare consulibus centuriam sub cura Cai Valeri Pudentis amplissimi consularis cohors VI Nerviorum fecit cui praeest Lucius Vinicius Pius praefectus cohortis eiusdem\n
EDCS-13400111.translation: Under Emperor Antoninus II and Caesar Geta as consuls, this century of the 6th cohort of Nervii was made by the care of Caius Valerius Pudentis, most excellent consular legate, under whose command is Lucius Vinicius Pius, prefect of the same cohort.
EDCS-13400111.summary: This inscription records the construction of a century of the 6th cohort of Nervii, commissioned by the care of Caius Valerius Pudentis, under the joint consulship of Antoninus II and Geta, and mentions the prefect Lucius Vinicius Pius, dating to the reign of Caracalla and Geta.

EDCS-13400112.text_edition: \n Nymphis et Genio loci Marcus Hispanius Modestinus praefectus cohortis I Batavorum pro se et suis libens merito\n
EDCS-13400112.translation: To the Nymphs and the Genius of the place, Marcus Hispanius Modestinus, prefect of the 1st cohort of Batavians, gladly and deservedly set this up for himself and his family.
EDCS-13400112.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Nymphs and the Genius loci by Marcus Hispanius Modestinus, prefect of the 1st cohort of Batavians, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13400113.translation: Saturninus the centurion, Rufinus Hrindenus the optio.
EDCS-13400113.summary: This inscription lists two military officers, Saturninus as centurion and Rufinus Hrindenus as optio, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13400114.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo cohors I Aelia Dacorum Probiana cui praeest Aurelius Verinus tribunus\n
EDCS-13400114.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the 1st Aelian Dacian cohort Probiana, under the command of Aurelius Verinus the tribune.
EDCS-13400114.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Jupiter Optimus Maximus by the 1st Aelian Dacian cohort Probiana, commanded by tribune Aurelius Verinus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13500192.summary: This inscription records a payment made by Iulius, likely a votive offering or settlement of a debt, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13500197.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus et maemoriae sic Iuliae Victorinae quae vixit annos XXVIIII menses II dies XV et Constantio qui vixit annos IIII dies XXI menses XI Septimius Lupianus |(centurio) ex evocato coniugi et filio memoriam possuit sic\n
EDCS-13500197.translation: For the departed spirits and in memory of Iulia Victorina, who lived 27 years, 2 months, and 15 days, and of Constantius, who lived 4 years, 11 months, and 21 days, Septimius Lupianus, centurion and former evocatus, set up this memorial for his wife and son.
EDCS-13500197.summary: This funerary inscription commemorates Iulia Victorina and her son Constantius, set up by Septimius Lupianus, a centurion and former evocatus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13500204.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo vikani sic consistentes castello Veluniate curam agente Aelio Mansueto votum solverunt libentes laeti merito\n
EDCS-13500204.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus, the villagers living in the fort of Velunium, under the administration of Aelius Mansuetus, gladly and deservedly paid their vow.
EDCS-13500204.summary: This inscription records a votive offering to Jupiter Optimus Maximus made by the villagers of the fort of Velunium, administered by Aelius Mansuetus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-13500205.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti Britannicum ex argentariis Veb LXV Tiberius Claudius Triferna\n
EDCS-13500205.translation: From the silversmiths' supply of Emperor Vespasian Augustus, 65 units, Tiberius Claudius Triferna.
EDCS-13500205.summary: This inscription likely records a quantity of silver, 65 units, supplied by Tiberius Claudius Triferna during the reign of Emperor Vespasian.

EDCS-13500206.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti Britannicum ex argentariis Veb LXXIIX LRAD\n
EDCS-13500206.translation: From the silversmiths' supply of Emperor Vespasian Augustus, 78 units.
EDCS-13500206.summary: This inscription likely records a quantity of silver, 78 units, supplied during the reign of Emperor Vespasian.

EDCS-13500207.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti LRAD Britannicum ex argentariis Veb Tiberius Claudius Triferna LXIIX Triferna\n
EDCS-13500207.translation: From the silversmiths' supply of Emperor Vespasian Augustus, 68 units, Tiberius Claudius Triferna.
EDCS-13500207.summary: This inscription likely records a quantity of silver, 68 units, supplied by Tiberius Claudius Triferna during the reign of Emperor Vespasian.

EDCS-13500208.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti Britannicum ex argentariis Veb Imperatoris Tiberius Claudius Triferna Tiberius Claudius Triferna Tiberius Claudius Triferna Tiberius Claudius Triferna\n
EDCS-13500208.translation: From the silversmiths' supply of Emperor Vespasian Augustus, Tiberius Claudius Triferna.
EDCS-13500208.summary: This inscription, likely related to a supply of silver, mentions Tiberius Claudius Triferna during the reign of Emperor Vespasian, though the quantity is garbled.

EDCS-13500209.summary: This inscription records a quantity of 120, possibly related to a commodity or unit, associated with the names Gaesteum Illaesesi and Cillentici.

EDCS-13500212.translation: May it go well for Ausonius Avitus.
EDCS-13500212.summary: This is a brief acclamation wishing good fortune to Ausonius Avitus.

EDCS-13600409.text_edition: \n Iuli Alexandri diamysius sic ad aspritudines\n
EDCS-13600409.translation: From Julius Alexander, a diamysus, thus for harsh conditions.
EDCS-13600409.summary: This inscription identifies a 'diamysus' from Julius Alexander, intended for rough use, likely related to a trade or product.

EDCS-13800058.text_edition: \n Londinio Lucius Vital Ad Rufus Callisuni salutem Epillico et omnibus contubernalibus certiores vos esse credo me recte valere si vos invicem fecistis rogo mittite omnia diligenter cura agas ut illam puellam ad nummum redigas\n
EDCS-13800058.translation: Lucius Vitalis to Rufus Callisunus, greetings. I believe you are well, Epillicus and all our comrades; if you are reciprocally well, I ask you to send word. Take care to bring that girl back to the proper state.
EDCS-13800058.summary: This is a personal letter from Lucius Vitalis to Rufus Callisunus, mentioning mutual friends and a request concerning a girl, likely from the 1st or 2nd century AD.

EDCS-13900091.translation: From the workshop in Mogontiacum, Valerius Valentinus.
EDCS-13900091.summary: This inscription records Valerius Valentinus as being from a workshop in Mogontiacum (modern Mainz).

EDCS-13900390.translation: Albinius Quartus, a soldier, dedicated this to the Matres.
EDCS-13900390.summary: This inscription records a dedication made by a soldier, Albinius Quartus, to the Matres (mother goddesses).

EDCS-13900394.text_edition: \n centuria Marci Valeri Ursi Luci Dulci centuria Martialis Lucius Postumus centuria Scriboni Rufi centuria Martialis Auli Sau<f=P>ei \n
EDCS-13900394.translation: The century of Marcus Valerius Ursi; the century of Lucius Dulcius Martialis; the century of Scribonius Rufus; the century of Aulus Saufeius Martialis.
EDCS-13900394.summary: This inscription lists four centuries, presumably military units or administrative divisions, identified by the names of their leaders, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-14700083.text_edition: \n Cintos II libram incroinaturae pastelos auri VIIII sabanum Alaxandri(num sic mappam locellum tumum ornatum anulum argenteum XI aurios X oiidi sic IV venti notanns sic a domino illuttrasi sic NIICIOCTORIS PVRDO\n
EDCS-14700083.translation: Cintos II, a pound of gold inlay, seven pastels; a Alexandrian towel, a decorated tunic, a silver ring XI, ten gold pieces; four pieces of gold; [unknown meaning].
EDCS-14700083.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of valuable items, possibly for a burial or as an inventory, including gold, silver, and textiles, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15100031.summary: This inscription records the rank of an individual, an imaginifer or standard-bearer, in the Ninth Legion, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15100061.summary: This is a very short dedication, stating that an individual named Felix made the object on which it is inscribed, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15300200.text_edition: \n Sociorum L<ut=F>(udarensium) Britannicum ex arcentariis sic \n
EDCS-15300200.translation: From the public funds of the Socii Lutudarenses.
EDCS-15300200.summary: This inscription indicates a financial record or a statement of origin for funds, related to the Lutudarenses community, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15300201.summary: This inscription records a name, Sennianus, and a place, Durobrivis, possibly indicating origin or fabrication, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-15300207.text_edition: \n Cai Iuli Proti Britannicum Lutadarense ex argentariis\n
EDCS-15300207.translation: Gaius Julius Protus, from Lutadara, from the money-changers.
EDCS-15300207.summary: This inscription identifies an individual, Gaius Julius Protus, and his origin from Lutadara, specifying his association with money-changers, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16000286.text_edition: \n Rei publicae Glevensis IIviri Publius Aelius Finiti\n
EDCS-16000286.translation: Publius Aelius Finitus, IIvir of the Republic of Glevens.
EDCS-16000286.summary: This inscription records the name and office of Publius Aelius Finitus, who served as a IIvir (a type of magistrate) for the community of Glevens, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100246.text_edition: \n Cai Silvi Tetrici euodes ad aspritudines Cai Silvi Tetrici pencillum ad impetum lippitudinis Cai Silvi Tetrici diamisus ad diatheses cicatrices Cai Silvi Tetrici biprosopum ad impetum \n
EDCS-16100246.translation: Gaius Silvius Tetricus: a cosmetic for rough skin; Gaius Silvius Tetricus: a brush for the affliction of blear-eyes; Gaius Silvius Tetricus: a balm for diatheses; [unknown] for scars.
EDCS-16100246.summary: This inscription lists several cosmetic or medicinal products, likely sold by Gaius Silvius Tetricus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16100363.translation: Sacred to the cheerful Genius of Hesperus.
EDCS-16100363.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Genius (guardian spirit) of Hesperus, described as cheerful, likely from a private shrine or place of worship dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-16200017.translation: The tenth cohort, century of Flavius Iulianus.
EDCS-16200017.summary: This inscription identifies a military unit, specifically the tenth cohort and the century of Flavius Iulianus.

EDCS-16200018.translation: The third cohort, century of Rufinius Primus.
EDCS-16200018.summary: This inscription identifies a military unit, specifically the third cohort and the century of Rufinius Primus.

EDCS-16200019.summary: This inscription identifies a military unit, specifically the century of Fulvius Macer.

EDCS-16200020.summary: This inscription identifies a military unit, specifically the century of Licinius Nerva.

EDCS-16200021.summary: This inscription identifies a military unit, specifically the century of Sadus Tirus.

EDCS-16200022.summary: This inscription identifies a military unit, specifically the century of Vinuleius Super.

EDCS-16200749.summary: This inscription, likely military, records Tiberius Claudius Pacatus and indicates a numerical designation, possibly his 14th command or achievement.

EDCS-16200850.translation: From Flavius Litugenus, a 'diapsoricum' for clarity.
EDCS-16200850.summary: This inscription, likely a dedication or marker, records Flavius Litugenus and a term ('diapsoricum') related to clarity or possibly a medical treatment.

EDCS-16201000.summary: This inscription begins to record a dedication or monument related to Emperor Nerva Caesar, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-16201217.text_edition: \n Imperatoris Vespasiani Augusti Britannicum ex argentariis Veb Sociorum Novaec--- sociorum novaec--- VIII\n
EDCS-16201217.translation: From Emperor Vespasian Augustus, the Britannicum from the moneychangers Veb Sociorum Novaec--- eight.
EDCS-16201217.summary: This inscription records a contribution or award of 'Britannicum' by Emperor Vespasian Augustus from the moneychangers Veb Sociorum Novaec, with a numerical value of eight indicated.

EDCS-16300038.summary: A simple funerary or building inscription, likely dating to the Roman period, recording the maker of a monument.

EDCS-16400031.summary: A very brief inscription, likely Roman period, recording the builder of a wall.

EDCS-17900011.text_edition: \n involaverit llum involaveritvirus mulieris illido us llum Vitali Espeditus tatirum\n
EDCS-17900011.translation: If he steals her, if he steals the woman's life, Vitalis Espeditus has... [---]
EDCS-17900011.summary: This is a curse inscription, likely Roman period, invoking divine punishment on someone who steals from Vitalis Espeditus.

EDCS-17900013.text_edition: \n Senianus Magnus MO Lucianus Marcellianus Mallianus Mutata Medolgeacus\n
EDCS-17900013.translation: Senianus Magnus, Lucius Lucianus Marcellianus, Mallianus, Mutata Medolgeacus.
EDCS-17900013.summary: This inscription lists several names, possibly indicating a group of people involved in a project or a communal dedication, likely from the Roman period.

EDCS-17900026.text_edition: \n Si quis vomerem Civilis involavit ut animam suuam in templo deponat si non vomerem ub si servus si liber si libertinus unano finem faciam\n
EDCS-17900026.translation: If anyone has stolen Civilis's ploughshare, may he deposit his soul in the temple; if not, the ploughshare, whether he be slave, free, or freedman, I will make one end.
EDCS-17900026.summary: A curse inscription, likely Roman period, from Civilis, threatening divine retribution and a swift end to anyone who steals his ploughshare.

EDCS-17900027.text_edition: \n Deae Suli Minervae Solinus dono numini tuo maiestati paxsam balnearem et palleum nec permittas somnum nec sanitatem ei qui mihi fraudem fecit si vir si femina si servus si liber nissi se retegens istas species ad templum tuum detulerit liberi sui sua et qui deg ei quoque xe somnum nec sanitatem nalleum et relinquas nissi ad templum tuum istas res retulerint\n
EDCS-17900027.translation: To the Goddess Sulis Minerva, Solinus offers, by your divine majesty, a bathhouse and a cloak, and permit no sleep nor health to him who defrauded me, whether man, woman, slave, or free, unless he shall bring these things to your temple bare of his own free will; free his children, and also him who wronged him, and leave him neither sleep nor health nor cloak, unless they bring these things to your temple.
EDCS-17900027.summary: A votive and curse inscription, likely Roman Britain, from Solinus to Sulis Minerva, dedicating a bathhouse and cloak and asking for divine punishment on a defrauder.

EDCS-17900029.text_edition: \n Deae Suli Minervae rogo sanctissimam maiestatem tuam ut vindices ab his qui fraudem fecerunt ut eis permittas nec somnum [nec ------\n
EDCS-17900029.translation: To the Goddess Sulis Minerva, I beg your most holy majesty to avenge me against those who have wronged me, so that you permit them neither sleep [nor ------
EDCS-17900029.summary: A plea for vengeance inscription, likely Roman Britain, to Sulis Minerva, asking the goddess to punish those who have wronged the dedicator.

EDCS-17900492.text_edition: \n Illorum anima lassetur TITUMNUS SEDILEUBISEDIAC USAQUEPANUM Exsibuus Lothuius Masentius Aesibuas Petiacus\n
EDCS-17900492.translation: May Titun(n)us's spirit be weary; Sedilius, Bisdiacus, Usaquepanus, Exsibuus Lothuius, Masentius, Aesibuas, Petiacus.
EDCS-17900492.summary: This inscription appears to be a curse or imprecation directed against Titun(n)us, listing several other individuals who may have been involved.

EDCS-17900506.text_edition: \n Deo Mercurio ISAMVALV DINIT sanguinem Civilis fuerit de Trinni familiam Velvalis am suam Markellinum sic familiam Velorigam et familiam suam Morivassum et familiam Riovassum et familiam Minoven et familiam suam \n
EDCS-17900506.translation: To the god Mercury, Isamvalv(s) gave blood. Civilis, of the Trinni family, his freedwoman Velvalis, and her freedmen Markellinus, Veloriga, Morivassus, Riovassus, and Minovus, and his own freedman.
EDCS-17900506.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mercury, likely recording the emancipation or offering of several freedpersons by Civilis and Velvalis, with a blood offering.

EDCS-17900507.text_edition: \n B ATADITAMO conquaeror tibi Sulis Arminia Verecundinum Terenti consumas qui argent<e=I>olos duos mihi revavit non illi permittas nec sedere nec iacere nec ambulare nec somnum nec sanitatem illum quantocius consumas et iterum non perveniat \n
EDCS-17900507.translation: Ataditamus, I complain to you: Sulis Arminia, may you consume Verecundinus Terenti, who owes me two silver vessels; do not allow him to sit, nor lie down, nor walk, nor sleep, nor have health, consume him as quickly as possible, and may he never reach [his goal].
EDCS-17900507.summary: This is a curse tablet directed to the goddess Sulis Arminia, asking for divine retribution against Verecundinus Terenti for an unspecified debt.

EDCS-17900528.translation: Bitilus Linus, son of Bitiluus Linus.
EDCS-17900528.summary: This inscription is a fragmented funerary epitaph for an individual named Bitilus Linus, dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-17900541.text_edition: \n Si puer si puella si vir si femina qui hoc involavit non ei remittatur nisi innocsentiam ALE non illi dimittatur nec somnum nisi ut Eutichia modium nebulae modium veniat fumi\n
EDCS-17900541.translation: Whether boy, whether girl, whether man, whether woman, whoever stole this, may it not be remitted to them unless [it is concerning] innocence; may it not be remitted to him, nor sleep, unless Eutichia comes with a modius of mist and a modius of smoke.
EDCS-17900541.summary: This is a curse inscribed on a monument, likely a tomb, threatening anyone who steals from it with ill fortune.

EDCS-20401655.text_edition: \n Cordula summa uraea annorum LXXX Gai Asici Probi Domiti\n
EDCS-20401655.translation: Cordula, of the highest rank, aged eighty years, of Gaius Asicius Probus Domitius.
EDCS-20401655.summary: This inscription records the death of Cordula, an elderly woman, whose connection to Gaius Asicius Probus Domitius is indicated, possibly as her patron or relation.

EDCS-20500206.summary: This inscription is a dedication or memorial for two individuals, Proculus and Urbius.

EDCS-20500210.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely a name, Sulpicius Senex, possibly on a tomb or dedication.

EDCS-23900147.text_edition: \n Deo Hercul<i=L> Marus tribunus legionis XX fecit \n
EDCS-23900147.translation: Marus, tribune of the Twentieth Legion, made [this] to the god Hercules.
EDCS-23900147.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the god Hercules by Marus, a military tribune of the Twentieth Legion.

EDCS-23900148.text_edition: \n Marti Toutati sacrum Vinoma votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-23900148.translation: A sacred offering to Mars Toutatis. Vinoma paid his vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-23900148.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Vinoma to the deity Mars Toutatis.

EDCS-25100850.translation: In the 9th consulship of Emperor Vespasian and the 7th consulship of Emperor Titus, when Gnaeus Julius Agricola was governor (legate Augusti pro praetore).
EDCS-25100850.summary: This is a formal dating formula for an official document or inscription, specifying the consuls and governor during the Flavian period.

EDCS-25100851.translation: In the 9th consulship of Emperor Vespasian and the 7th consulship of Emperor Titus, when Gnaeus Julius Agricola was governor (legate Augusti pro praetore).
EDCS-25100851.summary: This is a formal dating formula for an official document or inscription, specifying the consuls and governor during the Flavian period.

EDCS-25100853.translation: From the imperial mines of Lutudarensis, belonging to Emperor Caesar Hadrian Augustus.
EDCS-25100853.summary: This inscription identifies the origin of materials from the imperial mines of Lutudarensis, under the reign of Emperor Hadrian.

EDCS-25100857.translation: To Publius Rubrius Abascantius, of the Lutudarensis mine.
EDCS-25100857.summary: This is a dedication to a mine official named Publius Rubrius Abascantius, likely from the Roman mining region of Lutudarensis.

EDCS-25100866.text_edition: \n Luci Iuli Senis crocodes ad aspritudinem\n
EDCS-25100866.translation: To Lucius Iulius Senis, for roughness.
EDCS-25100866.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication or offering by Lucius Iulius Senis, possibly related to a remedy for roughness or a similar condition.

EDCS-28500378.text_edition: \n Numinibus Auggustorum deo Marti Camulo Tiberinius Celerianus civis Bellovacus moritix Londiniensium primus VA\n
EDCS-28500378.translation: To the divine powers of the Emperors, to the god Mars Camulus, Tiberinius Celerianus, a citizen of the Belovaci, first of the London mariners.
EDCS-28500378.summary: This is a dedication to the Emperors' divine powers and the god Mars Camulus by Tiberinius Celerianus, a mariner from London and a citizen of the Belovaci tribe, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-28500384.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the name of an individual, Publius Iulius Rufus.

EDCS-28500385.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the genitive name of Tiberius Claudius Severus, possibly indicating ownership or a dedication.

EDCS-28500387.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the genitive name of Tiberius Claudius, possibly indicating ownership or a dedication.

EDCS-28500391.summary: This inscription records that Bodukus was the maker of the object, likely a craftsman or artisan.

EDCS-28500392.summary: This is a fragmentary inscription, likely the name of an individual, Lucius Egnatius Valerius.

EDCS-28500397.translation: To the holy god Viridius [restored text].
EDCS-28500397.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to the god Viridius, likely from a pagan sanctuary.

EDCS-28500402.text_edition: \n Cordula Tingitanum vetus penuarium exscellens sic sumaur \n
EDCS-28500402.translation: Cordula, an excellent, old provider from Tingis, thus I have set (it up).
EDCS-28500402.summary: This is a dedication or memorial by Cordula, described as an excellent and long-standing provisioner from Tingis (modern Tangier), likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-30000058.text_edition: \n Plautius Nobilianus Aurelius Saturninus Domitia Attiola et si qui afuere\n
EDCS-30000058.translation: To Plautius Nobilianus Aurelius Saturninus, and to Domitia Attiola, and to whomever else was there.
EDCS-30000058.summary: This inscription is a dedication to Plautius Nobilianus Aurelius Saturninus and Domitia Attiola, likely for a public monument.

EDCS-30000059.text_edition: \n Lucio Iulio Miano a Septimio Silvano \n
EDCS-30000059.translation: To Lucius Julius Mianus, from Septimius Silvanus.
EDCS-30000059.summary: This is a dedication to Lucius Julius Mianus by Septimius Silvanus, possibly on a tombstone or a gift.

EDCS-30000067.summary: This inscription records the name Crescens, possibly on a personal item or grave marker.

EDCS-30000078.summary: This inscription likely records the name and perhaps a nickname or profession of Leo, possibly on a tombstone or a military record.

EDCS-30000079.text_edition: \n XVII Kalendas Maias renuntium cohortis VIIII Batavorum omnes ad loca qui deb<e=V>nt et inpedimenta renuntiarunt optiones et curatores detulit Arcuittius optio |(centuriae) Crescentis \n
EDCS-30000079.translation: On the 15th day before the Kalends of May, the messenger of the 9th cohort of Batavians reported all those who were due at their posts, and the baggage train; the optio and curatores presented it. Arcuittius, optio of the century of Crescens.
EDCS-30000079.summary: This is a military report from the 9th cohort of Batavians detailing the reporting of personnel and baggage, dated to April 17th.

EDCS-30000083.translation: To the Dibus Vitribus, son of Mamus.
EDCS-30000083.summary: This is a dedication to the Dibus Vitribus by his son Mamus.

EDCS-30000085.summary: This inscription records the name Annius Martialis, likely on a tombstone or personal object.

EDCS-30000088.text_edition: \n Luci C H LLucii duo Lucii oliva nigra\n
EDCS-30000088.translation: Lucius, son of C.H. Lucius, two Lucii, the black olive.
EDCS-30000088.summary: This inscription lists two individuals named Lucius, possibly noting a familial connection and a distinguishing feature or symbol like a 'black olive'.

EDCS-30000091.summary: This inscription records the name Genialis, son of Nonnus, possibly on a tombstone or personal item.

EDCS-30000094.summary: This inscription likely refers to a military unit, the Turma Prusoniana, possibly associated with Marcus.

EDCS-31900425.summary: This inscription likely records an abstract concept or the faith associated with an individual named Constantius, potentially on a monument or as a personal motto.

EDCS-31900426.summary: This inscription likely records an abstract concept or the faith associated with an individual named Constantius, potentially on a monument or as a personal motto.

EDCS-32001603.translation: Exuperius, bishop of the church of Boge, gave this.
EDCS-32001603.summary: This inscription records a donation made by Exuperius, the bishop of the church of Boge, likely in the early Christian period.

EDCS-32001604.text_edition: \n Marcus L N fecit centuria Claudi Senioris Anni Cohors IIII Breucorum\n
EDCS-32001604.translation: Marcus, son of Lucius, made this for the century of Claudius Senior, Cohors IIII Breucorum.
EDCS-32001604.summary: This inscription records the maker of an object or structure for a military unit, the Cohors IIII Breucorum, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-32001610.translation: In the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus, [this was made] by Felix.
EDCS-32001610.summary: This inscription records a dedication or creation by Felix, dated to the consulship of Orfitus and Priscus (likely referring to the 3rd century AD).

EDCS-33400610.summary: This is a simple maker's mark, indicating that Bruscius is the creator of the object or structure, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33800062.summary: This inscription records the artist or builder, Cabriabanus, who created a wall painting, likely from a domestic or public building.

EDCS-33800067.summary: This inscription appears to be a funerary or dedication text identifying Rabirinius as the son of Severus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-33800075.text_edition: \n Annoto de duas ocrias asciam scalprum manicam si mulier aut si baro RIANTINE duas partis deo AC CEVM\n
EDCS-33800075.translation: To Annotus, from two measures of barley, a sickle, a handle, if it is for a woman or if it is for a man, two parts of Riantine, and to Deo AC CEVM.
EDCS-33800075.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication to deities or spirits, listing offerings of grain, tools, and possibly names or places, likely from a religious context.

EDCS-33800076.text_edition: \n Rigore valli Aeli Draconis Mais Co<n=G>gabata Uxelodunum Cam<b=M>og<l=I>anna \n
EDCS-33800076.translation: At the rampart of Aelius Draco, from Mais [at] Congabata, Uxelodunum, Camboglanna.
EDCS-33800076.summary: This inscription lists place names or topographical features along a Roman frontier, likely indicating a location or a march route associated with Aelius Draco, possibly from the Roman military presence in Britain.

EDCS-33800086.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription likely related to a workshop, possibly indicating a quantity or item number.

EDCS-36400173.text_edition: \n Vegetus Montani Imperatoris Augusti servi Iucundiani vicarius emit mancipioque accepit puellam Fortunatam sive quo alio nomine est natione Diablintem de Albiciano LEG |(denariis) sescentis eamque puellam de qua agitur sanam traditam esse erronem fugitivam non esse praestari quod si quis eam puellam de qua agitur partemve quam quis ex ea evicerit, quo minus Vegetum Montani Imperatoris Caesaris servi vicarium eumve at quem ea res pertinebit habere possidereque recte liceat\n
EDCS-36400173.translation: Vegetus, a slave of the Emperor Montanus, acting as a deputy, bought and received into his possession the girl Fortunata, or by whatever other name she is known, of the Diablintes tribe, for 600 denarii, and he guarantees that the girl in question was delivered sound, not a runaway, and that if anyone should successfully claim ownership of her or any part of her, such that Vegetus, the deputy slave of the Emperor Caesar Montanus, or whomever this matter concerns, may not rightfully hold and possess her.
EDCS-36400173.summary: This is a legal document recording the purchase of a slave girl named Fortunata by Vegetus, a deputy slave of Emperor Montanus, for 600 denarii, with guarantees about her condition and legal status.

EDCS-37600969.translation: From the century of Surus, son of Vitalis Similis.
EDCS-37600969.summary: This inscription likely records a name and affiliation, possibly belonging to a soldier or a tombstone.

EDCS-37600970.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription containing personal names, possibly from a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-37600980.text_edition: \n Pondo XI plena pondo XXXIII Veri\n
EDCS-37600980.translation: 11 pounds, a full 33 pounds from Veri.
EDCS-37600980.summary: This inscription records weights, likely related to a commercial transaction or inventory.

EDCS-37600983.summary: This is a very short inscription, likely an ownership mark or a person's initials.

EDCS-37600987.summary: This inscription appears to record a quantity or measurement, possibly related to wine or another commodity.

EDCS-37600990.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Iusta Doriti piisime vixit \n
EDCS-37600990.translation: To the Divine Shades. Iusta Doriti lived most piously.
EDCS-37600990.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicated to the Divine Shades, commemorating Iusta Doriti.

EDCS-37600991.translation: Olus, decurion of the Wing of the Tungri.
EDCS-37600991.summary: This inscription records the name and rank of Olus, a decurion in the Ala Tungrorum, likely military.

EDCS-37600993.translation: Asper, decurion of the Wing of the Tungri.
EDCS-37600993.summary: This inscription records the name and rank of Asper, a decurion in the Ala Tungrorum, likely military.

EDCS-37600994.translation: To the Wing of the Tungrians, military advantage.
EDCS-37600994.summary: This is a military inscription likely related to the Ala Tungrorum, a Roman cavalry unit, possibly recording a benefit or achievement.

EDCS-37600996.text_edition: \n MA(3) stivi Luci A--- Ver--- Uxopillus\n
EDCS-37600996.translation: Lucius Ver---, from Uxopillus, served as a beneficia(rius).
EDCS-37600996.summary: This inscription likely identifies Lucius Ver--- from Uxopillus, possibly a soldier or official, perhaps as a beneficia(rius), a soldier with special duties.

EDCS-37600999.translation: Belonging to the centuria of Candidus the provincial.
EDCS-37600999.summary: This inscription indicates a division or unit named after Candidus, possibly a Roman soldier or official, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-37601010.summary: This inscription is a simple personal name, Gaius Familius Martialis, likely identifying an individual.

EDCS-37601013.translation: Of Marcus H---, son of N---.
EDCS-37601013.summary: This inscription records the name of Marcus H---, son of N---, likely a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-37601023.summary: This inscription refers to 'beneficiaries,' possibly soldiers or individuals receiving benefits, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-37601025.translation: Belonging to the centuria of Felicio.
EDCS-37601025.summary: This inscription indicates a military unit or division named after Felicio, likely dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-38600009.summary: This inscription records a sum of thirty denarii, possibly related to a transaction, debt, or payment.

EDCS-38600010.summary: This inscription is a curse tablet (defixio) recording that Peminius Novalis was cursed, with further text incomplete.

EDCS-38800008.summary: This inscription records a dedication or offering by someone named Genitus to the god Mercury.

EDCS-38800021.translation: To the divine Mercury Severinus gives.
EDCS-38800021.summary: A gift to the god Mercury, likely made by Severinus.

EDCS-39800478.summary: This inscription records the name and title of Cassius Vnus, a prefect.

EDCS-44100115.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely records the name Publius Cipius Polibus, possibly with an indication of an action or statement.

EDCS-44100159.summary: This fragmentary inscription likely indicates ownership or belonging to Aventinus Primus.

EDCS-44100160.summary: This inscription identifies a workshop belonging to Rutti and names Karus.

EDCS-44100162.summary: This inscription states that Sangus was the maker of the object.

EDCS-44300052.summary: A dedication to the god Mars.

EDCS-44300054.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Mamma Victoris filia Baibius Duianus coniugi sanctissimae quae vixit annos XXIIII\n
EDCS-44300054.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed. Baibius Duianus to his most holy wife Mamma, daughter of Victor, who lived 24 years.
EDCS-44300054.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Mamma, wife of Baibius Duianus, recording her parentage and age at death.

EDCS-44300055.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum Gaius! Cossutius Saturninus Hippone Regio miles legionis VI Victricis Piae Fidelis \n
EDCS-44300055.translation: To the Spirits of the Departed, sacred. Gaius Cossutius Saturninus of Hippone Regio, a soldier of the VI Legion Victrix, Pious and Faithful.
EDCS-44300055.summary: This funerary inscription records the name, origin, and military affiliation of Gaius Cossutius Saturninus.

EDCS-44300061.text_edition: \n Deo Marti Rigonemeti et Numinibus Augustorum Quintus Neratius Proxsimus sic arcum de suo donavit\n
EDCS-44300061.translation: Quintus Neratius Proxsimus, out of his own resources, dedicated this arch to the god Mars Rigonemetus and the Divine Augusti.
EDCS-44300061.summary: This inscription records the dedication of an arch by Quintus Neratius Proxsimus to the god Mars Rigonemetus and the Imperial numina.

EDCS-44500016.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription likely honoring Tiberius Claudius T..., possibly a high-ranking individual, date unknown.

EDCS-44500018.text_edition: \n Deo Apollini Cunomaglo Corotica Iuti filia votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-44500018.translation: To the god Apollo Cunomaglus, Corotica, daughter of Iutus, willingly paid her vow as deserved.
EDCS-44500018.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Apollo Cunomaglus by Corotica, daughter of Iutus, likely dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44500019.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription with very little legible text, making its purpose and date impossible to determine.

EDCS-44500020.text_edition: \n Searigillus Searigis fecit Lovernius posuit votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-44500020.translation: Searigillus, son of Searigis, made it; Lovernius placed it, willingly paid his vow as deserved.
EDCS-44500020.summary: This inscription records the creation and placement of a monument by Searigillus and Lovernius, who fulfilled a vow, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44500021.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Nemmoni Verecundi vixit annos LXXV heres posuit\n
EDCS-44500021.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Nemmo Verecundus lived 75 years; his heir set this up.
EDCS-44500021.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Nemmo Verecundus, who lived 75 years, with the monument erected by his heir, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44500025.text_edition: \n Luci Octavi Luci Pollia Martialis Eporedia militis legionis XX\n
EDCS-44500025.translation: Lucius Octavius, son of Lucius, Pollia Martialis, of Eporedia, a soldier of the 20th legion...
EDCS-44500025.summary: This is a fragment of an inscription that likely records the name and military affiliation of Lucius Octavius, a soldier of the 20th legion, date unknown.

EDCS-44500027.summary: This is a heavily damaged inscription mentioning 'Dacicus' and 'tribunician power', possibly referring to an imperial figure or provincial governor, date unknown.

EDCS-44500037.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Sanctinius Exsuperatus...
EDCS-44500037.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Sanctinius Exsuperatus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44500048.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Lucius Valerius Luci Pollia Geminus Foro Germanorum veteranus legionis II Augustae annorum L hic situs est heres faciendum curavit ex testamento\n
EDCS-44500048.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Lucius Valerius Geminus, son of Lucius, of the Pollian tribe, a veteran of the 2nd Augusta legion, aged 50 years, is buried here; his heir had this made according to his will.
EDCS-44500048.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Lucius Valerius Geminus, a 50-year-old veteran of the 2nd Augusta legion, whose heir erected the monument according to his will, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44500055.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Caius S Ro |(centurio) legionis \n
EDCS-44500055.translation: To the spirits of the departed, Gaius S. Ro..., centurion of the ... legion...
EDCS-44500055.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription for Gaius S. Ro..., a centurion of an unknown legion, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-44500068.summary: This inscription, likely a funerary marker or a dedication, refers to an individual who held the rank of singularis consularis, possibly in the late Roman Empire.

EDCS-44500076.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Iusta, most devoted daughter of Doritus.
EDCS-44500076.summary: This is a funerary dedication (Dis Manibus) for Iusta, the devoted daughter of Doritus.

EDCS-44500080.translation: To the genius of the place, happily.
EDCS-44500080.summary: This is a dedication to the local protective spirit (genius loci) of a place.

EDCS-44500082.translation: Decumanus dedicated this from his own resources.
EDCS-44500082.summary: This is a dedication recording a gift or construction made by Decumanus at his own expense.

EDCS-44500083.text_edition: \n Deo Viridio Trenico arcum fecit de suo donavit\n
EDCS-44500083.translation: He built an arch to the god Viridius Trenicus and dedicated it from his own resources.
EDCS-44500083.summary: This inscription records the construction and dedication of an arch to the god Viridius Trenicus, funded by the dedicator.

EDCS-44500090.text_edition: \n Matribus Marulla Insequentis votum solvit libens laeta merito\n
EDCS-44500090.translation: Marulla Insequentis fulfilled her vow to the Mothers, willingly, joyfully, as deserved.
EDCS-44500090.summary: This is a votive dedication to the Matres (Mothers) by Marulla Insequentis, who fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-44500091.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Insus Vodulli filius cives sic Trever eques alae Augustae turma Victoris curator Domitia h(eres) f(aciundum) c(uravit)\n
EDCS-44500091.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Insus, son of Vodullus, a citizen of the Treveri, a cavalryman of the troop of Victor in the Augustan ala, the curator, Domitia, the heir, took care to have this made.
EDCS-44500091.summary: This is a complex funerary inscription for Insus, a Roman cavalryman of Trever origin, detailing his military service and noting his heir, Domitia, who arranged the burial.

EDCS-44500092.translation: To Felicis, his son, his father.
EDCS-44500092.summary: This is a funerary dedication from a father to his deceased son, Felicis.

EDCS-44500135.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Gai! Pomponi Gai! filii Valentis Victricensis beneficiarii tribuni legionis \n
EDCS-44500135.translation: To the spirits of the departed. Gaius Pomponius Valens, son of Gaius, of Victricensis, a beneficiary of the tribune of the legion.
EDCS-44500135.summary: This is a funerary dedication for Gaius Pomponius Valens, a soldier who served as a beneficiary of a legionary tribune.

EDCS-44500207.text_edition: \n Deae Senunae Flavia Cunoris votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-44500207.translation: To the goddess Senuna, Flavia Cunoris fulfilled her vow willingly and deservedly.
EDCS-44500207.summary: This is a votive dedication to the goddess Senuna by Flavia Cunoris, who fulfilled a vow.

EDCS-44500208.text_edition: \n Claudius Celsus votum fecit deae Senunae libens merito\n
EDCS-44500208.translation: Claudius Celsus gladly and deservedly made a vow to the goddess Senuna.
EDCS-44500208.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Claudius Celsus to the goddess Senuna.

EDCS-44500209.text_edition: \n Cariatia Ressa votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-44500209.translation: Cariatia Ressa gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-44500209.summary: This inscription records a fulfilled vow made by Cariatia Ressa.

EDCS-44500210.translation: Quintianus [dedicated?] a small plaque to the goddess Senuna.
EDCS-44500210.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a plaque to the goddess Senuna by Quintianus.

EDCS-44500211.text_edition: \n Bell Memorianus votum fecit deae Senunae\n
EDCS-44500211.translation: Bell Memorianus made a vow to the goddess Senuna.
EDCS-44500211.summary: This inscription records a votive offering made by Bell Memorianus to the goddess Senuna.

EDCS-44500212.text_edition: \n Deae Senae Lucilia Sena votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-44500212.translation: Lucilia Sena gladly and deservedly fulfilled her vow to the goddess Sena.
EDCS-44500212.summary: This inscription records a fulfilled vow made by Lucilia Sena to the goddess Sena.

EDCS-44500213.text_edition: \n Deae Senunae Lucius L Herbonianus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-44500213.translation: Lucius L Herbonianus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the goddess Senuna.
EDCS-44500213.summary: This inscription records a fulfilled vow made by Lucius L Herbonianus to the goddess Senuna.

EDCS-44500214.text_edition: \n Deae Senunae Servandus Hispani votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-44500214.translation: Servandus Hispani gladly fulfilled his vow to the goddess Senuna.
EDCS-44500214.summary: This inscription records a fulfilled vow made by Servandus Hispani to the goddess Senuna.

EDCS-44500215.text_edition: \n Deae Senunae Servandus Hispani votum solvit libens\n
EDCS-44500215.translation: Servandus Hispani gladly fulfilled his vow to the goddess Senuna.
EDCS-44500215.summary: This inscription records a fulfilled vow made by Servandus Hispani to the goddess Senuna.

EDCS-44600008.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus sacrum C Elie Maxsime quae uixit annos X et Elio Alexsandro qui uixit annos IIX Elius Feluminus pater pientissimis filiis faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-44600008.translation: Sacred to the Spirits of the Departed: Elius Feluminus had this made for his most devoted children, C. Elie Maxsime, who lived 10 years, and Aelius Alexander, who lived 8 years.
EDCS-44600008.summary: This funerary inscription records the commemoration of two young children, Elie Maxsime and Aelius Alexander, by their father Elius Feluminus.

EDCS-44600092.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Cosconiae Mammiolae coniugi piissimae Aurelius Serenus\n
EDCS-44600092.translation: To the Divine Shades: Aurelius Serenus to his most devoted wife, Cosconia Mammiola.
EDCS-44600092.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Cosconia Mammiola, dedicated by her husband Aurelius Serenus.

EDCS-44600096.text_edition: \n Cohortis VI Nerviorum centuria Iuli Martini\n
EDCS-44600096.translation: Of the century of Iulius Martinus of the Sixth Cohort of Nervii.
EDCS-44600096.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the century of Iulius Martinus from the Sixth Cohort of Nervii, likely part of a boundary marker or unit identification.

EDCS-44600097.text_edition: \n CIANOVA F annorum II mensis unius Fontia\n
EDCS-44600097.translation: Fontia, daughter of Cianova, aged two years and one month.
EDCS-44600097.summary: This inscription records the burial of a young girl named Fontia, who died at the age of two years and one month.

EDCS-44600098.text_edition: \n Dis Inferis Manibus Apullio vixit annos XXXV Sabina vixit annos XVII Huctia vixit annos XLII\n
EDCS-44600098.translation: To the Infernal Gods. Apullius lived 35 years, Sabina lived 17 years, Huctia lived 42 years.
EDCS-44600098.summary: This funerary inscription honors the deceased Apullius, Sabina, and Huctia, providing their ages at death.

EDCS-44600099.summary: This inscription records the age of a deceased individual who lived for only one month.

EDCS-44600101.translation: To the Gods of the Underworld. Aurelius...
EDCS-44600101.summary: This is a funerary inscription beginning with a dedication to the Gods of the Underworld and the name Aurelius, with the rest of the text missing.

EDCS-44600103.translation: had the monument erected for his father.
EDCS-44600103.summary: This inscription records that someone arranged for a monument to be erected for their father.

EDCS-44600139.translation: The squadron of Paternus across the rampart.
EDCS-44600139.summary: This inscription likely identifies a military unit, the turma (cavalry squadron) of Paternus, in relation to a fortification or defensive line.

EDCS-44600154.translation: Of the Tenth Cohort, century of Munatius Maximus.
EDCS-44600154.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the century of Munatius Maximus from the Tenth Cohort.

EDCS-44600155.summary: This inscription records the name Canius Vindecatus, possibly a dedication or a tombstone.

EDCS-44600158.summary: This inscription records a military unit, the century of Camianus.

EDCS-44600163.text_edition: \n Numinibus Augustorum Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Alexandro A Pio Felici Augusto tribunicia potestate proconsuli P\n
EDCS-44600163.translation: To the divine spirits of the Emperors, Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, in his tribunician power, proconsul.
EDCS-44600163.summary: This is a dedication to Emperor Severus Alexander, likely from the early 3rd century CE, honoring his imperial titles.

EDCS-44600168.text_edition: \n Cohortis III centuria Caessenni Faesti sic fecit\n
EDCS-44600168.translation: The century of Caessennius Faestus of the Third Cohort made this.
EDCS-44600168.summary: This is a building record or dedication from the 3rd century CE, indicating construction or a contribution by a century of the Third Cohort.

EDCS-44600178.translation: To the departed spirits of Livia Nae.
EDCS-44600178.summary: This is a funerary dedication to Livia Nae, typical of Roman burial inscriptions.

EDCS-44600187.translation: The century of Valerius Rufus of the Fifth Cohort.
EDCS-44600187.summary: This is a building record or dedication, likely from the Roman military, indicating the century of Valerius Rufus of the Fifth Cohort was responsible.

EDCS-44600191.translation: The century of Libo of the First Cohort.
EDCS-44600191.summary: This is a building record or dedication, likely from the Roman military, indicating the century of Libo of the First Cohort was responsible.

EDCS-44600192.summary: This is a building record or dedication, likely from the Roman military, indicating the century of Ulpius Volusianus was responsible.

EDCS-44600194.summary: This is a building record or dedication, likely from the Roman military, indicating the century of Caledon Secundus was responsible.

EDCS-44600195.translation: The century of Maximus of the Third Cohort.
EDCS-44600195.summary: This is a building record or dedication, likely from the Roman military, indicating the century of Maximus of the Third Cohort was responsible.

EDCS-44600196.translation: The century of Maximus Terentius of the Third Cohort.
EDCS-44600196.summary: This is a building record or dedication, likely from the Roman military, indicating the century of Maximus Terentius of the Third Cohort was responsible.

EDCS-44600197.translation: The century of O... of the Third Cohort.
EDCS-44600197.summary: This is a building record or dedication, likely from the Roman military, indicating a century of the Third Cohort responsible, with the centurion's name incomplete.

EDCS-44600198.summary: This inscription records the name of a military unit, specifically the century of Marus Dexter, likely found on a tombstone or boundary marker.

EDCS-44600199.summary: This inscription records the name of a military unit, specifically the century of Valerius Verus, likely found on a tombstone or boundary marker.

EDCS-44600200.translation: Century of Laetianus of the Second Cohort.
EDCS-44600200.summary: This inscription records the name of a military unit, specifically the century of Laetianus of the Second Cohort, likely found on a tombstone or boundary marker.

EDCS-44600205.translation: Lousius Suavis of the Sixth Cohort.
EDCS-44600205.summary: This inscription records the name of a military individual, Lousius Suavis of the Sixth Cohort, likely on a tombstone or military equipment.

EDCS-44600206.summary: This inscription records the name of a military unit, specifically the century of Cocceius Regulus, likely found on a tombstone or boundary marker.

EDCS-44600207.summary: This inscription records the name of a military unit, specifically the century of Iulius Ianalis, likely found on a tombstone or boundary marker.

EDCS-44600209.summary: This inscription records the name of a military unit, specifically the century of Regulus, likely found on a tombstone or boundary marker.

EDCS-44600210.translation: Century of Gellus Philippus of the Fifth Cohort.
EDCS-44600210.summary: This inscription records the name of a military unit, specifically the century of Gellus Philippus of the Fifth Cohort, likely found on a tombstone or boundary marker.

EDCS-44600211.summary: This inscription records the name of a military officer, Serenus, who held the rank of primus pilus (first spearman), likely on a tombstone.

EDCS-44600213.translation: Century of Obscenus Libo of the Second Cohort.
