EDCS-44600213.summary: This inscription records the name of a military unit, specifically the century of Obscenus Libo of the Second Cohort, likely found on a tombstone or boundary marker.

EDCS-44600214.translation: The century of Valerius Sabinus of the First Cohort.
EDCS-44600214.summary: This inscription records the ownership or identification of a century belonging to the First Cohort, likely on a military object or marker.

EDCS-44600215.translation: The century of Socellus of the Third Cohort.
EDCS-44600215.summary: This inscription records the ownership or identification of a century belonging to the Third Cohort, likely on a military object or marker.

EDCS-44600216.translation: The century of Dida, belonging to Petta.
EDCS-44600216.summary: This inscription records the ownership or identification of a century, noting Dida and Petta, likely on a military object or marker.

EDCS-44600218.translation: The century of Pompeius Aemilianus, 30 feet.
EDCS-44600218.summary: This inscription records the century of Pompeius Aemilianus and a measurement of 30 feet, possibly related to land division or construction.

EDCS-44600219.summary: This inscription records the ownership or identification of a century belonging to Marcus Rufus, likely on a military object or marker.

EDCS-44600220.summary: This inscription records the ownership or identification of a century belonging to Marcus Rufus, likely on a military object or marker.

EDCS-44600221.summary: This inscription records the ownership or identification of a century belonging to Carius Scipio, likely on a military object or marker.

EDCS-44600232.summary: This inscription is a dedication to the Goddess Victory, with the beginning of the inscription partially preserved.

EDCS-44600236.translation: The century of Pompeius Albinus of the Sixth Cohort.
EDCS-44600236.summary: This inscription records the ownership or identification of a century belonging to the Sixth Cohort, belonging to Pompeius Albinus.

EDCS-44600239.summary: This inscription records the ownership or identification of a century belonging to Cassius Priscus, likely on a military object or marker.

EDCS-44600243.summary: This is a personal name, likely an owner or the subject of a dedication, possibly from a tombstone.

EDCS-44600249.translation: Husband of the Second Augustan Legion.
EDCS-44600249.summary: This inscription likely refers to a soldier or a relative of a soldier serving in the Legio II Augusta.

EDCS-44600256.text_edition: \n Rubrius Successus Nymphabus votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-44600256.translation: Rubrius Successus Nymphabus gladly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-44600256.summary: This is a votive inscription recording the fulfillment of a vow by Rubrius Successus Nymphabus.

EDCS-44600267.translation: To Esuvius Tetricus, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-44600267.summary: This is a dedication to Esuvius Tetricus, addressed as 'nobilissimo Caesari', likely a high-ranking official.

EDCS-44600268.text_edition: \n Postumo Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo Germanico maximo tribunicia pootestate IV\n
EDCS-44600268.translation: To Postumus Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, greatest German, with tribunician power for the fourth time.
EDCS-44600268.summary: This is a titulature inscription for an emperor or high dignitary, likely Postumus, dating to his fourth tribunician power.

EDCS-44600269.text_edition: \n Imperatori Caesari Marco Aurelio Severo Alexandro Pio Felici Augusto pontifici maximo tribunicia potestate II consuli procons(uli) patri patriae civitas Carvetiorum Luguvalio milia passuum XVIIII\n
EDCS-44600269.translation: To the Emperor Caesar Marcus Aurelius Severus Alexander Pius Felix Augustus, chief priest, consul, proconsul, father of the fatherland, the civitas of the Carvetii at Luguvalium, 19 miles.
EDCS-44600269.summary: This inscription records a dedication by the civitas of the Carvetii at Luguvalium to Emperor Severus Alexander, indicating a distance of 19 miles.

EDCS-46400876.text_edition: \n Daeo Maglo <do=OD> euum qui fraud<e=U>m fecit de paedagogio <do=OD> elaeum qui furtum de padagogium sagum qui sagum Servandi involavit Silvester Rigomandus Senelis Venustinus Vorvena Calaminus Felicianus Rufaedo Vendicina Ingenuinus Iuventius Alocus Cennosus Germanus Senedo Cunovendus Regalis Nigella Senicianus <do=OD> antae nonum diem illum tollat qui sagum involavit Servandi \n
EDCS-46400876.translation: To the god Euum who committed theft from the paedagogium; and to the god Elaeum who stole the cloak from the paedagogium; and to the god Sagum who stole the cloak. May Silvester Rigomandus Senelis Venustinus Vorvena Calaminus Felicianus Rufaedo Vendicina Ingenuinus Iuventius Alocus Cennosus Germanus Senedo Cunovendus Regalis Nigella Senicianus not keep the thief who stole the cloak of Servandus for nine days.
EDCS-46400876.summary: This is a curse inscription naming multiple gods and individuals involved in a theft, particularly of a cloak from a paedagogium, dating to the Roman period.

EDCS-46400877.text_edition: \n U abhereo ilis res irr qui argentios Sabiniani furaverunt id est Similis Cupitus et Lochita hos deus siderabit in hoc septi<z=S>onio et peto ut vitam suam perdant ante dies septem \n
EDCS-46400877.translation: I hate them, those who stole the silver of Sabinianus, namely Similis, Cupitus, and Lochita. May these gods strike them down in this tomb, and I ask that they lose their lives within seven days.
EDCS-46400877.summary: This is a curse inscription targeting individuals who stole silver, asking for divine retribution upon them in their tomb.

EDCS-46400943.text_edition: \n as Ianuarias numerus purus cohortis I Tungrorum cui praeest Iulius Verecundus praefectus in his |(centuriones) VI ex eis absentes XLVI in his |(centurio) I o |(centurio) I II a VIII s |(centurio) I XXXI um III XXXI \n
EDCS-46400943.translation: To Ianuarias, the full complement of the First Cohort of Tungrians, commanded by Julius Verecundus, prefect; among them, 6 centurions, 46 absent, among them 1 centurion, 1 centurion, 8...; 1 centurion, 31...; 31...
EDCS-46400943.summary: This inscription records personnel numbers and status for a cohort of Tungrians, mentioning a prefect and centurions.

EDCS-46600247.translation: Of the Second Augustan Legion, of Flavius, son of Flavius [the centurion].
EDCS-46600247.summary: This is a military inscription identifying an individual, Flavius, likely a soldier or veteran, associated with the Legio II Augusta.

EDCS-46600253.translation: Of the Twentieth Legion, Gaius Julius T...
EDCS-46600253.summary: This is a fragment that likely names a soldier, Gaius Julius T., from the Twentieth Legion, possibly on a military monument or tombstone.

EDCS-46600254.translation: Of the Sixth Legion, Publius, son of S...
EDCS-46600254.summary: This fragment probably identifies a soldier, Publius, son of S., from the Sixth Legion, potentially on a military marker.

EDCS-46600255.text_edition: \n Legionis VI Victricis Marci V C Legionis III Cyrenaicae\n
EDCS-46600255.translation: Of the Sixth Legion Victrix, Marcus V.C., and of the Third Cyrenaic Legion.
EDCS-46600255.summary: This text likely records individuals or units associated with two legions, the Sixth Victrix and the Third Cyrenaic, perhaps on a building inscription or honorary monument.

EDCS-46600263.translation: Of Quintus Sollonius, from the century of Cupitus.
EDCS-46600263.summary: This inscription likely refers to a soldier named Quintus Sollonius, belonging to the century commanded by Cupitus, possibly on a military tombstone.

EDCS-46600264.translation: Of the century of Lucius Octavius, N.A., son of S.
EDCS-46600264.summary: This fragment names the century of Lucius Octavius and abbreviates personal details, likely appearing on a military marker or tombstone.

EDCS-46600265.translation: Of the Fifth Cohort of Gauls, Aemilius the decurion.
EDCS-46600265.summary: This inscription identifies a military officer, Aemilius, a decurion in the Fifth Cohort of Gauls, likely on a tombstone or military dedication.

EDCS-46800227.text_edition: \n Quintus Natalius Natalinus et Bodeni VO\n
EDCS-46800227.translation: Quintus Natalius Natalinus and Bodeni, V.O.
EDCS-46800227.summary: This records two individuals, Quintus Natalius Natalinus and Bodeni, identified by abbreviated titles, likely on a tombstone or dedication.

EDCS-46800229.text_edition: \n WVMANI NIIIS Egnatius pavimentum tesselatum stratum SE de sua pecunia dedit\n
EDCS-46800229.translation: Egnatius dedicated this tessellated pavement, paved from his own funds.
EDCS-46800229.summary: This inscription records Egnatius dedicating a paved floor, funded by himself, likely in a public building or bathhouse.

EDCS-48800360.text_edition: \n Cohortis VIIII Luci Vanii Setino dossuario iumento \n
EDCS-48800360.translation: Of the Ninth Cohort, Lucius Vanius Setinus, dossuarius [pack-animal handler] of the auxiliary force.
EDCS-48800360.summary: This inscription identifies Lucius Vanius Setinus as a dossuarius in the Ninth Cohort, likely on his tombstone.

EDCS-48800496.translation: Constantinus the Younger, most noble Caesar.
EDCS-48800496.summary: This inscription refers to Constantinus the Younger in his capacity as a Caesar, likely on a public building or honorary monument from the 4th century AD.

EDCS-48800548.translation: Tiberius Claudius Caesar Augustus, chief priest, in his ninth tribunician power, emperor for the sixteenth time, from the conquered Britons.
EDCS-48800548.summary: This is a dedication by Emperor Claudius, likely on a monument celebrating his victory in Britain, dating to around 51-52 AD.

EDCS-48800550.translation: Of Nero Augustus from the Kalends of January, fourth consul. British // From the Kalends of July, chief priest, consul // From the money-changers of Gaius Nips Ascanius, thirty.
EDCS-48800550.summary: This inscription, possibly from a monetary context, mentions Nero's consulship and tribunician powers and associates items with the money-changer Gaius Nips Ascanius, dating to the Neronian period.

EDCS-48800559.summary: This is a simple ownership or dedication inscription mentioning Gaius Nips Ascanius, likely related to a commercial context.

EDCS-48800597.text_edition: \n Cai Iuli Proti Britannicum Lutadarense ex argentariis\n
EDCS-48800597.translation: Of Gaius Julius Protus, the British Lutadarense, from the money-changers.
EDCS-48800597.summary: This inscription records a payment or transaction made by Gaius Julius Protus, possibly related to a British commodity, from the money-changers.

EDCS-48800598.text_edition: \n Cai Iuli Proti Britannicum Lutadarense ex argentariis\n
EDCS-48800598.translation: Of Gaius Julius Protus, the British Lutadarense, from the money-changers.
EDCS-48800598.summary: This inscription records a payment or transaction made by Gaius Julius Protus, possibly related to a British commodity, from the money-changers.

EDCS-48900025.summary: This is a dedication to the deity Faunus Narius.

EDCS-48900166.text_edition: \n Imperatore [Domitiano] Caesare Augusto Germanico XV consule exactus ad sextarios XVII semis habet pondo XXXIIX\n
EDCS-48900166.translation: During the fifteenth consulship of Emperor Domitian Caesar Augustus Germanicus, it was extracted to seventeen and a half sextarii and weighs thirty-two pounds.
EDCS-48900166.summary: This inscription records a measurement and weight of a substance, likely a commodity, during Domitian's fifteenth consulship (90 AD).

EDCS-48900175.text_edition: \n Severianus filius Brigomallae Patarnianus sic filius Matarnus sic ussor sic Catonius Potentini Marinianus Belcati Lucillus Lucciani Aeternus Ingenui Bellaus Bellini\n
EDCS-48900175.translation: Severianus, son of Brigomalla, Patarnianus, thus son of Matarnus, thus uxor, thus Catonius, son of Potentinus, Marinianus, son of Belcatus, Luccillus, son of Luccianus, Aeternus, son of Ingenuus, Bellaus, son of Bellinus.
EDCS-48900175.summary: This inscription lists a series of individuals with their patronymics or matronymics, possibly recording family lineage or a complex transaction, with many of them identified as 'thus' (sic), suggesting a legal or administrative context.

EDCS-48900218.summary: This inscription appears to be a dedication or possibly ownership marker for Melluna, with Somia also mentioned.

EDCS-48900221.text_edition: \n Emita sic parta sua Lovernianus donavit\n
EDCS-48900221.translation: Emita, thus born from his own, Lovernianus donated.
EDCS-48900221.summary: This inscription records a donation by Lovernianus, possibly of something belonging to or originating from Emita.

EDCS-49100019.translation: Iamcilla fulfilled the vow which she had promised.
EDCS-49100019.summary: This votive inscription records the fulfillment of a vow by Iamcilla, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49100075.text_edition: \n Publius Oranius Facilis Iovi sigillum ex testamento\n
EDCS-49100075.translation: Publius Oranius Facilis gave a figurine to Jupiter in accordance with his will.
EDCS-49100075.summary: This inscription records a dedication of a figurine to Jupiter by Publius Oranius Facilis, bequeathed by will, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49100164.text_edition: \n Aurelius Atticianus Mercurio votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-49100164.translation: Aurelius Atticianus willingly and deservedly paid his vow to Mercury.
EDCS-49100164.summary: This inscription records the fulfillment of a vow to Mercury by Aurelius Atticianus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49100200.translation: Vatiaucus gave (this) to the god Abandinus from his own resources.
EDCS-49100200.summary: This inscription records a dedication from private means to the god Abandinus by Vatiaucus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49100219.translation: Severa Iosul[?] Felix gave (this) to the god Mercury.
EDCS-49100219.summary: This incomplete inscription records a dedication to Mercury by Severa Iosul[?] Felix, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49100222.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription with no discernible content, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49100231.translation: To Jupiter Optimus Maximus Augustus, on the occasion of the twentieth (decennial) vows. To Fortunatus(?).
EDCS-49100231.summary: This inscription likely records a dedication to Jupiter, possibly related to imperial vows and a person named Fortunatus, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49100268.translation: A gift to the goddess Minerva.
EDCS-49100268.summary: This inscription records a gift dedicated to the goddess Minerva, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49100278.text_edition: \n Legio VIII Augusta centuria Iuli Magni Iuni Dubitati\n
EDCS-49100278.translation: The Eighth August Legion, from the century of Iulius Magnus, belonging to Iunius Dubitus.
EDCS-49100278.summary: This inscription records military personnel of the Legio VIII Augusta, identifying a centurion and possibly a soldier, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49602567.text_edition: \n Trimontio aut Luguvalio Marco Iulio Martiali\n
EDCS-49602567.translation: To Trimontius or Luguvallius, dedicated by Marcus Iulius Martialis.
EDCS-49602567.summary: This inscription records a dedication to the deities Trimontius or Luguvallius by Marcus Iulius Martialis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-49602568.translation: To Postumus Londinius, by Jupiter Best and Greatest, and by the Genius of the Emperor Domitian Caesar Augustus Germanicus, and by the ancestral gods...
EDCS-49602568.summary: This is a dedication to Jupiter, the Emperor's Genius, and ancestral gods, likely from the 1st century AD, with partially restored names.

EDCS-49602570.translation: Domitius Tertius, a brazier, likewise Luguvalius.
EDCS-49602570.summary: This is a simple inscription identifying two individuals, Domitius Tertius (a brazier) and Luguvalius, likely from Roman Britain.

EDCS-49602847.translation: Lucius Aebutius Thales, son of Titus.
EDCS-49602847.summary: This is a simple funerary or biographical inscription identifying Lucius Aebutius Thales, son of Titus.

EDCS-50801125.text_edition: \n Iulius Aventinus mil<e=I>s cohortis primae Sunicorum legionis XX Valeriae victricis \n
EDCS-50801125.translation: Julius Aventinus, a soldier of the first cohort of the Sunicans, of the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix.
EDCS-50801125.summary: This inscription identifies Julius Aventinus as a soldier serving in the Twentieth Legion Valeria Victrix, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-50801180.text_edition: \n Deo Invicto Herculi sacrum Lucius Aemilius Salvianus tribunus cohortis I Vangionum votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-50801180.translation: To the Unconquered God Hercules, Lucius Aemilius Salvianus, tribune of the First Cohort of Vangiones, gladly and deservedly paid his vow.
EDCS-50801180.summary: This is a votive inscription dedicated to Hercules by Lucius Aemilius Salvianus, a tribune of the First Cohort of Vangiones, probably from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-51100300.text_edition: \n Liquamen Antipolitanum excellens Luci Tetti Africani Africani\n
EDCS-51100300.translation: The excellent Antipolitan garum of Lucius Tettius Africanus.
EDCS-51100300.summary: This inscription is an advertisement for a high-quality fish sauce (garum) produced by Lucius Tettius Africanus.

EDCS-51600073.text_edition: \n Litteras has adolexerum in furtum eum ne ante loduntur ne ILENCE exigentur VEONS et BEAR furtum et accusatorem neget furtum Caelianus in furtum fecit\n
EDCS-51600073.translation: Caelianus made these letters into a theft, so that they may not be damaged, nor collected for penalty, and that the penalty and the accuser may not deny the theft.
EDCS-51600073.summary: This is a legalistic inscription, possibly related to the registration or protection of documents, indicating a dispute or penalty regarding a theft.

EDCS-51600368.text_edition: \n et deae nae ARIO Varia R de suo \n
EDCS-51600368.translation: ...and to the goddess Naias, Varia of her own accord.
EDCS-51600368.summary: This is a fragmentary dedication to a goddess, possibly a Naiad, made by Varia, likely from a provincial area.

EDCS-51600475.text_edition: \n Dis Manibus Crescentis equitis alae Sebosianae ex numero equitum singularium stipendiorum XV heres faciendum curavit\n
EDCS-51600475.translation: To the Divine Shades of Crescens, an equestrian of the ala Sebosiana, from the number of the singular cavalrymen, having served fifteen years, his heir had this made.
EDCS-51600475.summary: This is a funerary inscription for Crescens, a cavalryman who served fifteen years in the ala Sebosiana, commissioned by his heir, likely from the 2nd or 3rd century AD.

EDCS-51600491.text_edition: \n Carinus Similis Consortius Comes Maslorius Senorix Cunittus Cunittus Cunedecanes Ceanatis Tiberinus\n
EDCS-51600491.translation: Carinus, Similis, Consortius, Comes, Maslorius, Senorix, Cunittus, Cunittus, Cunedecanes, Ceanatis, Tiberinus.
EDCS-51600491.summary: This inscription appears to be a list of names, possibly of a group of individuals, a military unit, or a family, of Romano-British origin.

EDCS-51600492.summary: This inscription records the name of a father and son, likely on a tomb or funerary monument, from a presumably late Roman period.

EDCS-51600532.translation: I am Gaius, your devoted attendant.
EDCS-51600532.summary: This inscription, likely from a funerary context, records the name and humble status of the deceased, Gaius, and his role as a 'peculiaris'.

EDCS-51600695.summary: This inscription likely records the name of an individual, possibly a tomb inscription, and the repetition may emphasize ownership or be a marker of a simple epitaph.

EDCS-55701743.translation: To the departed spirits, Flavia, wife of Marcus.
EDCS-55701743.summary: This is a funerary inscription dedicating the monument to the 'Dis Manibus' (departed spirits) and identifying the deceased as Flavia, wife of Marcus.

EDCS-55701744.translation: Century of Flavius Rufus, first spear (centurion).
EDCS-55701744.summary: This inscription likely refers to a military unit, specifically the century of Flavius Rufus, identified as a 'primus pilus' (first spearman or chief centurion).

EDCS-55701755.text_edition: \n Nimincillus Iunctinus Docillinae Lon<g=O>inus VSCANIMIHS NISIC eum qui inv<o=A>laverit deus det malam plagam \n
EDCS-55701755.translation: Nimincillus, son of Docillina (or Docillinus), and Iunctinus. Whoever violates this, may the god strike him with an evil plague.
EDCS-55701755.summary: This is a curse inscription intended to protect a burial, naming individuals Nimincillus and Iunctinus, and invoking divine retribution against desecrators.

EDCS-55701758.text_edition: \n Quintus M L Quinti libertus Olivae albae MCLI dulcia L pondo VI\n
EDCS-55701758.translation: Quintus, son of Lucius Quintus, freedman. 'Albae olivae' (White Olives), 1150; sweet wine, 6 pounds.
EDCS-55701758.summary: This inscription records the name of a freedman, Quintus, and lists quantities of goods, likely related to funerary offerings or provisions for the afterlife.

EDCS-55701764.summary: This inscription likely records the names of two individuals, possibly a married couple or parent and child, perhaps on a tombstone.

EDCS-55701768.text_edition: \n Sexti Servandi Sexti Servandi CIP Capriacum C VIIII CVmodii XIsextarii semis\n
EDCS-55701768.translation: Of Sextus Servandus, son of Sextus Servandus. Capriacum, 97; Cuamodii, 11 sextarii and a half.
EDCS-55701768.summary: This inscription records the name and parentage of Sextus Servandus, and lists quantities of wine ('sextarii') and possibly land ('Capriacum' and 'Cuamodii'), likely for funerary purposes.

EDCS-55701774.translation: Aged 80 years, old wine for Eutyches.
EDCS-55701774.summary: This inscription records the age of the deceased, Eutyches, and mentions old wine, likely as a funerary dedication or offering.

EDCS-55701776.summary: This is a record of a quantity of grain, 8.5 modii, associated with the god Janus, possibly for offering or storage.

EDCS-55701802.text_edition: \n Deabus Matribus Hananeftis et Ollototis Aelius Victor votum solvit libens laetus merito\n
EDCS-55701802.translation: Aelius Victor happily and deservedly fulfilled his vow to the Mother Goddesses Hananeftis and Ollototis.
EDCS-55701802.summary: This is a votive inscription recording Aelius Victor's fulfillment of a vow to the Mother Goddesses Hananeftis and Ollototis, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-55701825.summary: This is a very brief funerary or dedication inscription, possibly naming Marcus, son of Publius, as Victor.

EDCS-56500302.text_edition: \n Daeo Invicto M<i=Y>(thrae) Caius Cassius Fla \n
EDCS-56500302.translation: To the Invincible God Mithras, Gaius Cassius Fla[vius?].
EDCS-56500302.summary: This is a dedication to the god Mithras by Gaius Cassius Fla[vius?], likely dating to the Roman Imperial period when Mithraism was popular.

EDCS-56500303.summary: This is a dedication to the sun god Sol by Gaius Cassius Fla[vius?], identified as a centurion, probably from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-59500218.translation: May you live well, may you use [this] happily.
EDCS-59500218.summary: This is a personal inscription wishing good fortune and happiness, likely a private dedication or a message inscribed on an object.

EDCS-59500221.translation: [---] of Aulus, son of Publius, was honored.
EDCS-59500221.summary: This is a fragmented inscription, likely funerary, mentioning an individual named Aulus, son of Publius, and indicating a posthumous honor.

EDCS-59500224.summary: This is a fragmented dedication to the Celtic deity Toutatis, mentioning an individual named Lu[cius?], probably from a Romanized Celtic region.

EDCS-59500260.summary: This inscription likely records a member of the Legio Victrix (the Victorious Legion), identified as Marcus Ap[---], possibly indicating rank or a specific cohort.

EDCS-59500262.summary: This inscription seems to record military service or a ranking, mentioning Junius Verus Celer and potentially his term of service or position within a unit.

EDCS-59500263.summary: This inscription, likely a dedication or memorial, is heavily damaged and only contains fragments of names.

EDCS-59500264.text_edition: \n Iovi Optimo Maximo Dol<i=O>cheno Sulpicius Pudens praefectus cohortis IIII Gallorum votum solvit libens merito \n
EDCS-59500264.translation: To Jupiter Best and Greatest, Sulpicius Pudens, prefect of the Fourth Cohort of Gauls, willingly and deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-59500264.summary: A military dedication to Jupiter by Sulpicius Pudens, a prefect of a Gallic cohort.

EDCS-59500265.text_edition: \n ius V praefectus cohortis II Nerviorum\n
EDCS-59500265.translation: [...]us V, prefect of the Second Cohort of Nervians.
EDCS-59500265.summary: This inscription records the name and title of a prefect of the Second Cohort of Nervians.

EDCS-59500266.text_edition: \n EXMA aram votum solvit libens merito\n
EDCS-59500266.translation: Exma willingly and deservedly fulfilled her vow.
EDCS-59500266.summary: A votive offering by a woman named Exma.

EDCS-59500282.text_edition: \n centuria Ianuari Crescens scutarius aria |(denarium) I semis ato |(denarios) s |(denarium) I semis s |(denarium) I IIasses Geuus uria |(denarium) I semis Cs alabra |(denarios) Amtus lanceas |(denarium) I IIasses Govaus men |(denarios) II lanceas |(denarium) I debet Albus lanceas |(denarium) I debet Liber sagum |(denarios) II debet Tagomas vex<i=E>llarius lanceas |(denarium) I debet Victor venator lanceas |(denarios) V debet Verrinus lanceas |(denarios) Ndrs sagellum |(denarios) II debet Neso lanceas |(denarium) I Tullio debet sudarium |(denarium) I summa |(denarios) XXIX semis \n
EDCS-59500282.translation: From centurion Ianuarius Crescens, a scutarius (shield bearer): 1 denarius and a half for leather straps; [for] 3 denarii: 1 denarius and a half, [for] 1 denarius and a half, 1 denarius and a half, 2 asses, Geuus for leather straps: 1 denarius and a half, Cs for a helmet, Amtus for leather straps: 1 denarius, 2 asses, Govaus for men's leather straps: 1 denarius, 2 asses. Albus owes for leather straps: 1 denarius. Liber owes for a sagum (cloak): 2 denarii. Tagomas the vexillarius (standard bearer) owes for leather straps: 1 denarius. Victor the hunter owes for leather straps: 5 denarii. Verrinus owes for leather straps: [amount not specified]. Ndrs owes for a small sagum: 2 denarii. Neso owes for leather straps: 1 denarius. To Tullius: 1 denarius for a head covering. Total: 29 denarii and a half.
EDCS-59500282.summary: This is a detailed record of debts and payments, likely related to military equipment, involving various individuals and specifying amounts in denarii and asses.

EDCS-59500283.text_edition: \n XII Kalendas Maiasfa opus fabricae centuria Firmi vocridem factam ad veturam iussu Musuruni |(centurionis) circolas factas atsas numero VIII Huennius faber transversaria facta numero V item ferrum productum Andaver faber Tagomas faber\n
EDCS-59500283.translation: On the twelfth day before the Kalends of May, the work of the workshop of Firmius was carried out for the roadway, by order of Musurus the centurion, 8 circular objects were made, 5 transverse objects were made. Also, iron was produced by Andaver the craftsman and Tagomas the craftsman.
EDCS-59500283.summary: This inscription records a construction project, including the date, the overseer Musurus, and the craftsmen involved in producing various items and materials.

EDCS-59500284.text_edition: \n so XXXV semis mand r Manduorix Vastini filia\n
EDCS-59500284.translation: [...] 35 and a half denarii, from Manduorix, daughter of Vastinus.
EDCS-59500284.summary: This inscription records a sum of money and identifies the donor as Manduorix, daughter of Vastinus.

EDCS-59500285.text_edition: \n Medragus Acceptus Proculus Tagomas Secundus Mansuetus |(denarios) \n
EDCS-59500285.translation: Medragus, Acceptus, Proculus, Tagomas, Secundus, Mansuetus, [amount in] denarii.
EDCS-59500285.summary: This inscription lists several names followed by a monetary unit, possibly indicating a group payment or distribution.

EDCS-59500289.translation: Sent to you, brother, by the holy Iustus.
EDCS-59500289.summary: A short message indicating something was sent to a brother by someone named Iustus.

EDCS-59500290.text_edition: \n salutem s tum subscripseris usque quo res exegerit bene vale frater karissime tuo Secundo io praefectus cohortis \n
EDCS-59500290.translation: Greetings. As long as you have been collecting the accounts, fare well, my dearest brother, from your Secundus, prefect of the cohort.
EDCS-59500290.summary: A personal greeting and farewell from Secundus, a cohort prefect, to his brother, concerning the collection of accounts.

EDCS-59500292.summary: This is a very brief inscription, likely recording income, possibly related to a property or debt, with an incomplete name.

EDCS-59500302.summary: This is a dedication to the Celtic god Toutates, likely from a traveler or a local worshipper.

EDCS-64100285.text_edition: \n centuria Domiti Materni Aureli Severi levigatam et pingatarem\n
EDCS-64100285.translation: The smoothed and painted area of the centuria of Domitius Maternus Aurelius Severus.
EDCS-64100285.summary: This inscription describes a smoothed and painted area belonging to the centuria (a land division) of Domitius Maternus Aurelius Severus, likely related to land surveying or property description.

EDCS-64100287.summary: This inscription simply states that Victorinus was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-64100291.summary: This inscription simply states that Ianuarius was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-64100292.text_edition: \n Cassius tu sunt cu aufert Tittius et adfirmat ille se optatos numeratos accepisse et debere placet pecuniam supra scriptam reddi Aprili abhinc cum usuris ex die praesenti sine ulla replicatione actum Larae III Nonas Decembres Tertullo et Sacerdote sic\n
EDCS-64100292.translation: Cassius, you are the one who accepted from Titius and he confirms that he has received the promised sums and owes them; it is agreed that the above-written money is to be returned to April starting from today, with interest, without any repetition, done at Larae on the third day before the Nones of December, with Tertullus and Sacerdos as witnesses.
EDCS-64100292.summary: This inscription records a legal agreement or confirmation of a debt and repayment plan, specifying the parties involved, the amount, interest, and the date and location of its execution.

EDCS-64100300.summary: This inscription appears to record a personal name or names, possibly a dedication or an ownership mark.

EDCS-64100302.summary: This inscription simply states that Natalis was the maker of the object or monument.

EDCS-64100316.text_edition: \n Decuminus Sidoni fratri suo salutem necessaria qui infra scripti sunt Candidi Ha Ca Me Fifusius Carant nus us Vedavonis Nevvassius Minconis bene vale mihi Sido frater karissime a Decumino\n
EDCS-64100316.translation: Decuminus sends greetings to his brother Sidonius. The following are necessary: the ones written below, Candidus, Ha [---] Me[---]fius, Carantnus, Us[---] Vedavonis, Nevvassius, son of Minco. Farewell, my dearest brother Sido, from Decuminus.
EDCS-64100316.summary: This is a personal letter or message, likely inscribed on a tomb or monument, where Decuminus sends greetings and farewells to his brother Sidonius and lists other individuals.

EDCS-64100329.summary: This inscription likely records the name Carantii and a cognomen or tribal affiliation Capictinus, possibly as an ownership mark or a dedication.

EDCS-64100330.translation: Quintus Licinius Tutinus made this in Arles.
EDCS-64100330.summary: This is a dedication made by Quintus Licinius Tutinus in Arles.

EDCS-64100332.translation: Martialis, from the area above the river.
EDCS-64100332.summary: This is a personal inscription for Martialis, identifying him as being from Aquinus.

EDCS-64100336.translation: Reginus made this, [a gift] for Netacius.
EDCS-64100336.summary: This inscription records a dedication or commission by Reginus for Netacius.

EDCS-64100339.translation: Of the decurion of the Fifth Cohort of Gauls.
EDCS-64100339.summary: This is a fragment referring to an officer, specifically a decurion of the Fifth Cohort of Gauls.

EDCS-65000163.translation: May those who are here live well.
EDCS-65000163.summary: This is a general wish for well-being, likely for a group of people at a location.

EDCS-65000164.translation: May those who are here live well.
EDCS-65000164.summary: This is a general wish for well-being, likely for a group of people at a location.

EDCS-65000165.summary: This is a wish for the well-being of an individual named Peregrinus.

EDCS-65000166.summary: This inscription identifies the writer as a 'Silvicola' (forest dweller).

EDCS-65000167.translation: May you live as a forest dweller.
EDCS-65000167.summary: This is a wish for someone to live as a 'Silvicola' (forest dweller).

EDCS-68000087.text_edition: \n ab III Idus Apriles decurion I CERV XVII Kalendas Iunias cervesario XV Kalendas Iunias a pulli Traiano V VI Kalendas Maias ab Crescente eodem die ab anseres Nonis Iunis Suetio ADIV IIII Idus Iunias cervesario III Idus Iunias Vattone K I Sexto Attio Suburano Kalendis Ianuaris ab veterano pull eodem die ab SAV ab Chnissone IIII Nonas Ianuarias pulli Kalendis Martis ab Ma eodem die ab Candido III Kalendas Apriles ab Mar pridie Kalendas Apriles ab Exspedito [] VIIII Kalendas Maias ab V iumentario br summa anser item anseres pulli adempti item adempti pulli pridie Kalendas Maias item pulli a per Commodum summa pull IIII Kalendas se expensa XV Kalendas Iunias mnas pullus eodem die cena absumptus pullus VIII Kalendas Iunias nio pullus Idibus Iuniis c legati XVIII Kalendas Iulias Coris iussu [] eodem die in perierunt IIII Idus Iunias missio Flavi III Kalendas Septembres Nigro Broccho VIII Kalendas Maias Ianuarias cenante Broccho Kalendis Ianuariis per XVI Kalendas Februarias Broccho VIIII Kalendas Martias a stabulo Kalendis Martiis dom matronarum Idibus Martiis Nigro et E XII Kalendas Apriles abnae pridie Nonas Apriles Broccho III Kalendas Maias pr Septembre IIII Nonas Maias apud Sautenum summa expensorum --- reliqu et anseres numero ex VII Idus Maias Onesimo ad si eodem die Sauteno in stabulo IIII Kalendas Iunias pranden et Flavino ab eodem die mor apud Sautenum Kalendis Maiarum sim adventu consularis --- in prandio absumpt--- item Coris m VIII Idus Iunias pulli numero IIII XVII Kalendas Augustas per Surenum |(centurionem) nes numero XII eodem die no pulli penes Sautenum ribus pulli summa pulli numero XX ex eis taaru reliqui ser summa pulli numero VII\n
EDCS-68000087.translation: From the 3rd of the Ides of April, a decurion. For CERV XVII. From the Kalends of June, a wine merchant for XV. From the Kalends of June, from Trajan for V. From the VI Kalends of May, from Crescens. On the same day, from [---]. From the Nones of June, to Suetius, ADIV. For IIII Idus of June, a wine merchant. For III Idus of June, from Vatto. K I [---]. From Sextus Attius Suburanus on the Kalends of January. From the veteran pull [---]. On the same day, from SAV [---]. From Chnisson, on the IIII Nones of January, pulli. On the Kalends of March, from Ma [---]. On the same day, from Candidus. For III Kalends of April, from Mar. The day before the Kalends of April, from Exspeditus. For VIIII Kalends of May, from V, a mule driver. Total geese, also geese, pulli taken. Also pulli taken the day before the Kalends of May. Also pulli by Commodus. Total pulli IIII. From the se [---]. Expenses XV. From the Kalends of June, mnas. Pullus on the same day. Dinner, pullus consumed. VIII Kalends of June, nio pullus. On the Ides of June, c legati. For XVIII Kalends of July, Coris ordered [---]. On the same day, in perierunt. IIII Idus of June, missio Flavi. III Kalends of September, to Nigro Brocchus. VIII Kalends of May, from January, while dining with Brocchus. On the Kalends of January, through XVI Kalends of February. To Brocchus, VIIII Kalends of March, from the stable. On the Kalends of March, from the matrons. On the Ides of March, to Nigro and E. XII Kalends of April, from [---]. The day before the Nones of April, to Brocchus. III Kalends of May, pr [---]. Septembre. IIII Nones of May, at Sautenum. Total expenses --- reliqu [---] and geese, number from. VII Idus of May, to Onesimus. For si, on the same day, from Sauteno, in the stable. IIII Kalendas of June, prandium and from Flavinus. From the same day, mor [---] at Sautenum. On the Kalends of May, sim adventu consularis --- consumed in the prandium. Also Coris m. VIII Idus of June, pulli, number IIII. XVII Kalendas of August, through Surenum, centurion. nes number XII. On the same day, no pulli in the possession of Sautenum. ribus pulli. Total pulli number XX, from which taaru the rest. Total pulli number VII.
EDCS-68000087.summary: This is a detailed accounting record listing expenditures, dates, and recipients for various items including food (pulli, anseres, veterans, etc.) and services, likely for a communal meal or supply management, spanning from January to August.

EDCS-68000113.text_edition: \n sto Criaras Frissia Suasco Germanus Catussa drelun Modius Senecio Sactius X IIS Viator s X IIS Crenscens Gir X S Crenscens I us X I Leubius mallus alu of Varcenus is X II\n
EDCS-68000113.translation: Sto, Frissia Suasco Germanus, Catussa, Modius Senecio, Sactius (paid) X IIS, Viator (paid) X IIS, Crescens Gir (paid) X S, Crescens Ius (paid) X I, Leubius Mallus (paid) alu Varcenus (paid) is X II.
EDCS-68000113.summary: This inscription appears to be a record of payments or work contributions from various individuals, possibly related to a construction project or administrative duty, with names and amounts paid.

EDCS-68000133.text_edition: \n Masculus Ceriali regi suo salutem cras quid velis nos fecisse rogo domine praecipias utrumne cum vexsillo sic omnes rediemus an alterni crum aeque felicissimus et sis mihi propitius cervesam commilitones non habunt quam rogo iubeas mitti Flavio Ceriali praefecto a Masculo decurione\n
EDCS-68000133.translation: Masculus to his lord Cerialis, greetings. I ask you, my lord, to command whether we should return with the standard or in turns; may you be most fortunate and gracious to me. The soldiers will not have beer, which I ask you to order to be sent. From Masculus, decurion, to Flavius Cerialis, prefect.
EDCS-68000133.summary: This is a letter from a military subordinate, Masculus, to his superior, Flavius Cerialis, requesting instructions on troop movements and the provision of beer.

EDCS-68000153.text_edition: \n Cril Ol suo salutem unoin sicut ouium enes de carris Brittonum III aricarromauco bracis mcc id est modios trecentos octo ar onerari in am pin ccocrr MLIII p quemedam e et modios LXIII cu Vindolanda cum et velatura alent a itas vecturas id est XCVIIIIS et omnem velaturam eo quam vecturam eis solves merce tibi recte dixerat Sacer si de cera offeres Verecundo suis siquid ei fueris a quists tis vale simils \n
EDCS-68000153.translation: To Cril from his (master), greetings. Three wagons of British beer, 1308 modii, to be loaded into a transport wagon, 1053 (modii); and 63 modii with Vindolanda and the transport costs, that is 98 sesterces, and you will pay him the transport costs for all the transport. Sacer had told you correctly that if you offered it from the wax, to Verecundus and his household. Farewell, if you have bought anything from him. Farewell.
EDCS-68000153.summary: This is a fragment of a receipt or account detailing the delivery of British beer, quantities, transport costs, and instructions regarding payment and a potential transaction involving wax.

EDCS-68000154.text_edition: \n ut remittat meos denarios CCLXXXIS sic ostras conscientiam praefecti sui saluta Verecundum et Sanctum Lupum Capitonem et omnes cives et amicos cum quibus opto bene valeas vale Mensori ab Aspanio Comiciano\n
EDCS-68000154.translation: So that he may return to me 280 sesterces. So, greet the prefect, Verecundus and Sanctus Lupus, Capito, and all the citizens and friends with whom I wish you well. Farewell. From Aspanius Comicianus to Mensori.
EDCS-68000154.summary: This is a message from Aspanius Comicianus to Mensori, requesting the repayment of 280 sesterces and conveying greetings to various individuals including the prefect.

EDCS-69100067.translation: To Vialicus, freedman of Secundionis, greetings. Accept this letter from the slave of Marcus Salvius Mutandus.
EDCS-69100067.summary: This is a brief letter fragment from someone to Vialicus, a freedman, acknowledging receipt of a letter or document from the slave of Marcus Salvius Mutandus.

EDCS-69100069.translation: Taurus to his dearest lord Macrinus, greetings. When Catarrius arrived and you had not taken the opportunity, he had led away the pack animals. I cannot repair them within three months. I was present at Diadumenus, but he arrived later for one day.
EDCS-69100069.summary: This is a fragment of a letter from Taurus to his master Macrinus, explaining that pack animals were taken and are now in disrepair, and detailing a past interaction with a person named Diadumenus.

EDCS-69100070.translation: You will give to Titus the aviarius. Because they are boasting all over the forum that you have made a profit, I ask you, for your own sake, not to appear disreputable. You will not love your things like this, and you will think...
EDCS-69100070.summary: This is a fragment of a letter from someone to Titus the aviarius, warning him against appearing disreputable due to boasting about profits and advising him on how to manage his affairs.

EDCS-69100071.translation: ...Atticus. I beg you, by bread and salt, to send as soon as possible twenty-six denarii in victoriates and ten denarii from Paterion.
EDCS-69100071.summary: This is a plea from an unknown sender to Atticus, urgently requesting the delivery of money, specifically 26 denarii in victoriates and 10 denarii from Paterion.

EDCS-69100090.translation: In the consulship of Marcus Licinius Crassus Frugi and Gaius Laecanius Bassus. Florentinus, slave of Sextus Cassius [?], I have written, by order of my master, that he received two pension payments from the Vodatio estate.
EDCS-69100090.summary: This inscription records that Florentinus, a slave, acting on his master's orders, has written to confirm the receipt of two pension payments from a specific estate during the consulship of Crassus Frugi and Laecanius Bassus.

EDCS-69100091.translation: Under the consulship of Emperor Caesar Vespasian (for the 7th time) and Titus (for the 11th time), 11 days before the Kalends of November. Work on the Kalends of November. Decision given by Caesar between Litugenum and Magunum.
EDCS-69100091.summary: This is a dated administrative record, likely from a legal or military context, noting a decision made by Caesar concerning the area between Litugenum and Magunum, in the year 80 AD.

EDCS-69100112.translation: Ratio Crispi: beer (5 denarii); [?] CV (7 denarii); (3 asses) beer per Butus (1/2 denarius); [?] (51 asses) [?]; beer (2 asses); beer (1/2 denarius, 1/4 as); per Ianuarius beer (1 denarius, 1/2 as); from this [account]...
EDCS-69100112.summary: This inscription appears to be a fragment of an accounting record, likely listing purchases of beer and their costs, with a mention of a person named Crispus and transactions managed by Butus and Ianuarius.

EDCS-69200355.translation: Quintus, son of Marcus and Lucius, freedman of Quintus. Olives [?] sweet [?] 6 pounds.
EDCS-69200355.summary: This is a fragmentary list of goods, possibly for sale or inventory, noting olives and sweet items, with weights indicated, likely from a market or shop.

EDCS-69200359.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely the beginning of a name, possibly Gaius Atisius, with illegible text following.

EDCS-69200362.summary: This inscription simply records the name Quintus Attius Crescens, likely on a personal monument or marker.

EDCS-71300115.translation: On the Ides of July [?] of Lucrio.
EDCS-71300115.summary: This inscription notes a date, the Ides of July, and a name, Lucrio, possibly in reference to an event or a property.

EDCS-71300121.translation: Antoni, may you live in God.
EDCS-71300121.summary: This is a short inscription, likely a personal greeting or blessing addressed to someone named Antoni, possibly with Christian connotations.

EDCS-71300137.summary: This is a very fragmentary inscription, likely mentioning the emperor Maximinus Augustus, perhaps in a dedicatory or official context.

EDCS-71300141.translation: To Fortune the Returner, Eltaominus, a retired architect from the Vettunum cavalry wing, willingly, gladly, deservedly fulfilled his vow.
EDCS-71300141.summary: This inscription records a vow fulfilled by Eltaominus, a retired architect of the Vettunum cavalry, to the goddess Fortuna Redux.

EDCS-71300143.translation: [?] Aurelia Toisia [?], Aurelius Marinus [?], Aurelius Marianus [?], Lupia lived 25 years [?], lived 5 years, Pannonia [?], Vitalis lived 5 years [?], and Saturninus [?].
EDCS-71300143.summary: This is a fragmentary funerary inscription listing several names (Aurelia Toisia, Aurelius Marinus, Aurelius Marianus, Lupia, Vitalis, Saturninus) and the ages of some deceased individuals, suggesting it marks multiple burials.

EDCS-72200129.summary: This is a highly fragmentary inscription containing some letters and names, including Alioni Securio, the meaning of which is unclear due to damage.

EDCS-72200130.summary: This inscription likely records the names of two individuals, Ingenuus Maternos and Bolanius Diviorus, possibly as donors or honorees, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72200131.translation: Brocchus, Decimus G. S., Aspis, MXXIII S.

EDCS-72200140.summary: This inscription records the name Lucius C. Ma(...), likely identifying an individual associated with the monument, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72200142.summary: This inscription records a partially preserved word or name and the name Brocchus, whose relationship is unclear, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72600230.translation: By the hand of the Atilii.
EDCS-72600230.summary: This inscription indicates that the work was done by the hand of the Atilii family, possibly craftsmen or patrons, dating to the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72600385.summary: This inscription indicates that an object was made in the workshop of Reb[urr]ius.

EDCS-72700165.translation: To the gods of the underworld. Bodicacia lived 27 years.
EDCS-72700165.summary: A funerary epitaph for Bodicacia, who died at the age of 27, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72700166.summary: A dedication made by Dominica during her lifetime, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-72900031.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-72900031.translation: Emperor Caesar Titus Aelius Hadrianus Antoninus Augustus Pius, son of the deified Hadrian, grandson of the deified Trajan the Parthian, great-grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 13th time, imperator for the 2nd time, consul for the 4th time, father of the country, has granted Roman citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had or with those they would later marry (but only one wife each) to the equites and pedites of the Roman Germanici Pii Fideles army who served in the 4 alae and 14 cohorts (named as Noricorum, Sulpicia, Afrorum Veterana, I Thracum, I Flavia Hisp(anorum), I Latobicorum, Varcianorum, I Pannoniorum, Dalmatium, II Civium Romanorum, I Raetorum, VI Brittonum Pia Fidelis, II Asturum Pia Fidelis, I Classica Pia Fidelis, III and VI Breucorum, I Lucensium Pia Fidelis, II Varcianorum, VI Raetorum, IV Thracum) and have completed 25 years of service, and to the 26 men in the fleet with honorable discharge, whose names are listed below. This decree was issued on the 13th day before the Kalends of December, during the consulship of Gaius Curtius Justus and Gaius Iulius Iulianus. The fleet of the Germanici Pii Fideles, commanded by Marcus Ulpius Ulpianus, has officially recorded and verified this from a bronze tablet fixed to the wall in Rome behind the Temple of Divus Augustus by the Minervas. The recording is for Velvotigernus, son of Magiotigernus, a Briton.
EDCS-72900031.summary: An imperial grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Antoninus Pius to soldiers and sailors of the Germanici Pii Fideles army who had completed their service in Germania Inferior, dated to 185 AD.

EDCS-72900124.text_edition: \n Imperatorum duorum Augustorum Antonini et Veri Armeniacorum\n
EDCS-72900124.translation: Of the two emperors Augusti Antoninus and Verus, conquerors of Armenia.
EDCS-72900124.summary: A dedication or title mentioning the joint reign and military achievements of Emperors Marcus Aurelius Antoninus and Lucius Verus, likely from the Roman Imperial period.

EDCS-73700010.inscription_type: diplomata militaria, tituli operum, tituli sacri
EDCS-73700010.translation: Emperor Caesar Trajanus Hadrianus Augustus, son of the deified Trajan the Parthian, grandson of the deified Nerva, pontifex maximus, holder of tribunician power for the 4th time, consul for the 3rd time, has granted Roman citizenship and the right of marriage with the wives they then had or with those they would later marry (but only one wife each) to the equites and pedites who served in the five alae and eight cohorts (named as I Vespasiana Dardanorum, I Gallorum et Pannoniorum, I Flavia Gaetulorum, Gallorum Atectorigiana, II Hispanorum Aravacorum, I Sugambrorum veterana, I Bracarorum civium Romanorum, I Lepidiana civium Romanorum, I Flavia Numidarum, II Chalcidenorum, II Lucensium, II Flavia Brittonum, II Mattiacorum) and have completed 25 or more years of service, and to the 26 men in the fleet with honorable discharge, whose names are listed below. This decree was issued on the 14th day before the Kalends of November, during the consulship of Gaius Arminius Gallus and Gaius Atilius Seranus. The fleet of Flavia Moesica, commanded by Tiberius Claudius Marcellus, has officially recorded and verified this from a bronze tablet fixed to the wall in Rome behind the Temple of Divus Augustus by the Minervas. The recording is for Gaius Iulius Felix Vernus, son of Gaius Iulius, and his daughter Pulchra.
EDCS-73700010.summary: An imperial grant of citizenship and marriage rights by Emperor Hadrian to soldiers and sailors of the Classis Flavia Moesica in Lower Moesia who had completed their service, dated to 136 AD.

EDCS-73800021.summary: This is a maker's signature by Claudius Saturninus, likely on a sculpture or other crafted object.

EDCS-73900074.translation: From the workshop of Patricius Solita.
EDCS-73900074.summary: This indicates the workshop of Patricius Solita, possibly a mosaicist or craftsman.

EDCS-73900079.summary: This is a maker's signature, stating Albinus produced the object at Lugdunum (modern Lyon).

EDCS-73900084.summary: A maker's signature by Maximus, indicating they created the object.

EDCS-73900094.summary: This fragment likely records the name Quintus Valerius, possibly of the owner or creator of an object.

EDCS-73900097.summary: This inscription attributes the work to Sacrillus, indicating the artisan.

EDCS-73900103.summary: This inscription possibly indicates the donor or owner, Dominica, during her lifetime.

EDCS-75300138.translation: Genitus to Mercury. To the Genius who stole from me my sacred ring, whether a slave, freedman, woman, boy, or girl, may you permit him to be shamed, may he be trodden underfoot, may he walk without eating, drinking, or defecating, unless he has presented it back to your temple.
EDCS-75300138.summary: This is a dedication to Mercury containing a curse against the thief of a ring, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-76900067.translation: From the works of Nero Caesar Augustus Brit(annicus), diffused in the province, with Trebellius Maximus as governor.
EDCS-76900067.summary: This inscription records the diffusion of works related to Emperor Nero's Britannicus triumph in the province under the legate Trebellius Maximus, likely dating to Nero's reign (54-68 AD).

EDCS-78300053.summary: This inscription records a patronymic or filiation, identifying Giamillus in relation to his father Atticus and grandfather Nico, likely dating to the Roman imperial period.

EDCS-78300655.translation: Atticus promised to faithfully deliver to Narcissus, freedman of Rogatus Lingonius, and to Ingenuus, to whom it pertains, the legacies and their interest, which the upright must be properly cared for and when requested, and Atticus stated in person to those listed above that he owes, holds, and has received them before this day.
EDCS-78300655.summary: This inscription records an oath made by Atticus regarding the proper distribution of legacies and interest to specific individuals, likely from the late Roman Republic or early Empire.

EDCS-81200215.translation: Lucius Didius Bassus, son of Lucius, of the Fabia tribe, from Heraclea, a veteran of the II Augusta legion, aged 55, ordered this to be done by his will.
EDCS-81200215.summary: This is a funerary epitaph for Lucius Didius Bassus, a 55-year-old veteran of the II Augusta legion, dating from the Roman Imperial period.
